# Thread: CT and allergies
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-allergies.585/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pardus — 2012-04-01T19:49:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-allergies.585/post-9866>

Hi all,

When I got to the office on friday, one of my co-workers was wearing a really strong perfume which made my nose runny and my eyes water. It was a pretty bad reaction.

I decided to go outside and get some fresh air. It was around 25F, a little windy but not too bad so I went without a jacket and took a walk until my symptoms disappeared, about 30 minutes.

When I came back into the office, I could still smell the strong perfume, but my allergic reaction didn't return!

Anyone else notice their allergies improve with CT? This might be useful with spring and the coming allergy season...

### Lahrie — 2012-04-01T21:08:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-allergies.585/post-9903>

That's great to hear! I was hoping it would have some benefit for allergies too!

I added face dunks back in to my cold baths. It seems to make my sinuses drain more efficiently. For years the cycle has been allergies-plugged sinuses-infection-antibiotics. Repeat for several years. Then, have surgery-nasal spray steroids/allergy Rx-start cycle over again. I do not want to continue that crazy cycle.

Getting my sinuses to drain properly heads off the infection which means the rest does not need to recur. Hopefully, the allergies will disappear.

I still have a difficult time keeping my face in the water. The back of my throat doesn't seal shut when I hold my breath and water still gets in. So, I hold my nose shut with my hand. Hopefully, this doesn't short circuit anything. It's helping me for now though.

### Destiny — 2012-04-01T21:20:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-allergies.585/post-9909>

> **Lahrie;9966 wrote:** That's great to hear! I was hoping it would have some benefit for allergies too!
>
>
>
> I added face dunks back in to my cold baths. It seems to make my sinuses drain more efficiently. For years the cycle has been allergies-plugged sinuses-infection-antibiotics. Repeat for several years. Then, have surgery-nasal spray steroids/allergy Rx-start cycle over again. I do not want to continue that crazy cycle.
>
>
>
> Getting my sinuses to drain properly heads off the infection which means the rest does not need to recur. Hopefully, the allergies will disappear.
>
>
>
> I still have a difficult time keeping my face in the water. The back of my throat doesn't seal shut when I hold my breath and water still gets in. So, I hold my nose shut with my hand. Hopefully, this doesn't short circuit anything. It's helping me for now though.

My husband has been on Claritin D 24 hrs all year round for many years. He also was using a lot of Afrin to clear any extra congestion during the day. Two weeks into doing CT, he stopped using both. I could not believe it!

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-01T21:28:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/membership-pay-subscriptions.586/post-9911>

Since you are a doctor, I would think the lab costs should be covered by insurance or health savings accounts...or even out of pocket depending on circumstances.

### sandy — 2012-04-07T19:37:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/membership-pay-subscriptions.586/post-12488>

Dr K, if I sign up for the pay subscription, is there anything I should do before I have my first consult? labs run? or does that all come out after you sign up?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-07T19:51:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/membership-pay-subscriptions.586/post-12494>

@Sandy you need to assess your needs and order the labs you need with your doctor at home. You can use my blog on the optimizing labs here t guide you and you can ask questions on the forum to help figure out what is best. If you want the opne time bio hack health coach they are offering now it would behoove you to have all the things you want tested done before you get a talk with me. If you want a talk to me first to determine what to order you could do that too. Then you need to make sure you have a doc a home who will help execute your plan. If you want a detailed plan and have no labs.......it would be smart to provide us a very details story of your problems and history before hand so that we could help you and your doc construct an action plan to get you on the road to optimal. This option is really how you decide to use the health coach option you all asked for........we are willing to try it to see how it goes. One hour is a long time but how you choose to use the time is completely up to you.

# Thread: The MRSA experiment: how do we get Dr Kruse nominated for a Nobel?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 79
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SimonM — 2012-04-01T22:39:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-9936>

I only ask, because the last time anything similar was performed was when Dr Barry Marshall got frustrated by the medical profession's lack of interest in his research showing that the bacterium Helicobacter pylori was the cause of the majority of cases of peptic ulcer. He first presented his findings in 1983. They were dismissed. ["One prominent gastroenterologist dismissed him as 'a crazy guy saying crazy things'.](http://http://www.achievement.org/autodoc/page/mar1bio-1)" Sound familiar??

In 1985, to the horror of his colleagues, he deliberately infected himself with H.pylori - and sure enough, got seriously ill. Despite all he'd done, it took another ten years before his findings made it into clinical practice - now peptic ulcers are routinely treated with antibiotics. In 2005, he and colleague Dr Robin Warren were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. The Big One.

Yeah, it only took 20 years!! But compared to MRSA, H.pylori and peptic ulcers are a "nothing" disease. MRSA is an out-of-control, sometimes lethal superbug that costs billions of dollars a year just to TRY to contain. It has evolved resistance to almost all current antibiotics. And now Jack has shown how to totally defeat it. And that's just ONE affliction the Kruse protocol deals with.

So, who do you know? How can we get this out there?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-02T05:29:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10153>

No one knew a thing but me.....I injected myself the morning of the surgery and smeared the stuff on my torso.......and I refused all antibiotics. I new what kind of cuts I was going to have, so the skin barrier was going to be demolished and allow the bacteria in. I knew they were going to prep my skin so I decided to inject my body with the MRSA to make sure the CT would be tested to the max that I could test this way........I was looking for my own proof so that I could use this information on my own neurosurgical patients in the future.......And on January 27th I used it on a man who I would have never considered operating on before my last experiment. Diabetics are very at risk for infections, especially MRSA..........so I felt really comfortable operating on him after my last bio hack........and his results.......made my experience well worth the effort. His life is totally changed because of it. I feel bad I made him wait two yrs before I agreed to cut on him

### ColdBren — 2012-04-02T05:35:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10156>

The skeptics wil want some kind of verification of the MRSA injection...did you have any neutral witnesses etc?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-02T05:38:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10158>

nope.....no one knew what I was up to including my wife. But she saw the results......and so did my daughter. And do I really care what skeptic thinks? this was done to help patients......and 3 weeks later my idea, now proven did just that. If i cared what anyone thought I would not even get out of bed in the morning.

### Jake — 2012-04-02T07:23:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10214>

Dr.K. I feel I am a part of this (smashed finger) though I have not consciously chosen to be. I, too, don't really care what anyone thinks. (Way too boring!). I just want to know the truth. Of everything. It's so interesting...and frustrating...

### AmyK — 2012-04-02T08:12:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10222>

> **AmyK;10212 wrote: The other thing that is unclear to me is if the surgical staff knew about the MRSA. If so, even if YOU are cold-adapted, how do you convince them that knowingly bringing MRSA into a hospital full of sick, warm-adapted patients and the warm-adapted medical staff who care for them will turn out ok for everyone?
>
>
> Jack Kruse;10216 wrote:** No one knew a thing but me.....I inject myself the morning of the surgery and smeared the stuff on my torso.......and I refused all antibiotics. I new what kind of cuts I was going to have, so the skin barrier was going to be demolished and allow the bacteria in. I knew they were going to prep my skin so I decided to inject my body with the MRSA to make sure the CT would be tested to the max that I could test this way........I was looking for my own proof so that I could use this information on my own neurosurgical patients in the future.......And on January 27th I used it on a man who I would have never considered operating on before my last experiment. Diabetics are very at risk for infections, especially MRSA..........so I felt really comfortable operating on him after my last bio hack........and his results.......made my experience well worth the effort. His life is totally changed because of it. I feel bad I made him wait two yrs before I agreed to cut on him

I fully understand that you did this to prove to yourself that the pathway exists in order to help your patients in profound ways... and that you felt it was a "safe" option because you were only putting yourself at risk. I wonder, though, how you convinced yourself that smearing MRSA on your torso (which would reasonably transfer to your clothes, as well as anything they or you touched) would not pose any risk to your surgical staff or the other patients at the hospital. I have to admit, this is still a bit unsettling to me.

### Melinda — 2012-04-02T08:31:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10228>

I have to admit, this is still a bit unsettling to me.

I agree, as a former OR nurse, I can't imagine how this was safe for unknowing bystanders. Proper procedures protect the staff of unknown dangers so they are mostly OK. But did they know of your experiment? it's always a crap shoot for your health when you are part of an OR staff, glove break, needles stab.

### AmyK — 2012-04-02T14:54:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10266>

But did they know of your experiment? it's always a crap shoot for your health when you are part of an OR staff, glove break, needles stab.

He is saying he didn't tell anyone. If icing people down would indeed protect them from MRSA, I suppose I wouldn't be as disturbed by this, and I guess that's what originally had me feeling like this could save so many people NOW...but it seems it is not that simple. The person must be previously/presently cold-adapted, in which case he himself would have been the only one that was protected at the time of his surgery.

### ealachan — 2012-04-02T21:24:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10436>

I have to say I'm in agreement with all of the above. Putting a bunch of totally unknowing people in harm's way to prove a point sort of elevates the action from "selfless" to "reckless".

I'm not saying that I think the science behind all this stuff isn't legit, because I do. I'm simply saying that there was likely a better way to go about proving it. Do I know what that way was? Nope. But I can tell you right now that if I was one of those doctors or nurses in that OR and I found out after the fact about what kind of risk I'd been put at, I'd be mad as hell. Not that that clearly matters to Dr. K, as he has repeatedly pointed out.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-03T01:28:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10556>

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-03T03:01:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10594>

> **Jack Kruse;10625 wrote:** cant happen......i was the patient. I signed my consent.

Dr K I strongly believe you have a brilliant mind and I very much respect you for that... and still, I agree with Amy, Adriana and ealachan.

1\. N=1 proves nothing. We all know that. You can't use an n=1 to support a new treatment approach. That can very likely cost you your license.

2\. _You drove yourself home from the hospital after invasive surgery, general anesthesia, and in excruciating pain. Yes, you did put others in danger.

3\. What about your family? If one of my close loved-ones felt the need to do something this dramatic, putting themselves in life-threatening danger _so \*I\* will listen, I would have a whole lot of thinking to do... a whole lot._

I realize they are probably well taken care of financially even in the event that this experiment would have not been successful but that does not at all erase losing a father and husband. Not even thinking about the trauma your daughter must have experienced witnessing her father in this condition... by choice_.

4\. This experiment has not proven successful as of now. You are still hardcore icing. It could be latent and the moment you ease up it might come to a full-on infection.

5\. This has nothing to do with "not getting" it. I get it way too well.... and it's my personal choice to support it or not.

6\. You and I agreed that this revolution will come from the people and not government or scientists... this is your "people's review".

Your mind is brilliant enough to come up with many much safer experiments to prove your point. I do not understand your motivation for inflicting this damage onto yourself.

I trust in your mind and science much more than in this unnecessary self-mutilation

### hellojtm — 2012-04-03T03:11:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10601>

Good points Souldanzer. I think Dr. K. is aware he's done something drastic. I'm not one to speak about his family situation or the sacrifices he makes. I think he risked other's safety and I think all of us have at one time or another with nowhere near the potential payout of what Dr. K. has done.

But the N=1 is the important point. I believe that many people have been exposed to MRSA without getting it and not all of them (any of them?) are cold adapted. There are people exposed to HIV that don't get it so our immune system is obviously a beautiful thing if we treat it properly. We also don't have any way of removing the benefits of 6.5 years of good diet and proper sleep hygiene. All of these things are true of everything that Dr. K. has shared with us. He is one man. Art DeVany is one man and people have adopted his lifestyle to good effect. I guess the important thing is that we have a data point now, whether someone can take it and run with it scientifically based on these results is up to them (i'm thinking of the researchers). So, with some apparent risk (Dr. K. may clarify in his book, blog, forum why he believes we don't get it and he may be right), Dr. K. has at least made a mark that can't be ignored and like he said he put some skin in the game. How many times have I risked myself (auto racing, snowboarding, some people skydive, etc...) for completely frivolous reasons. Also, I think the MRSA dangers would mostly be to other post-op or grievously ill folks at the hospital, not the healthy and well cleaned staff (no pun intended). I've been hospitalized for massive infection before and I learned a lot about it while I was there.

We can somewhat solve the n=1 problem in this community. Of course without any purposeful infections etc.. we just need to track and share... track and share.. pay attention to the details...

When Dr. K. provides the mechanisms that applied to his situation that any biochemist and biologist can agree upon then we may even feel better about it.

jesse

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10605>

Dr. K. Progress is not made without taking risk. Usually that risk is evaluated as minimal by the person taking the risk (because that person has deep insight or uncommon knowledge--as a surgeon would) while those who are not fully cognizant of what is truly going on or those that may see danger everwhere will perceive lots of risks, and usually based on CW. I find no fault with your approach. In fact, progress is also not made without courage. Congratulations!

I am my own N=1 experiment, and have seen quantifiable results after just 8 weeks of CTing--better sleep, loss of fat weight, quicker recovery after HIIT, more muscular definition. Naysayers (like those at PH) should just try it for themselves IMHO.

Can't wait to read your book(s). Keep on keeping on!

### QuantumRadical — 2012-04-03T04:25:27+0300

AmyK, no disagreement. But facing the dragon is ultimately all about the inner self and it is very lonely standing there. Jack picked his poison and his method of application. He would either be a Hero or a Zero. I applaud his courage. And I think he knew pretty well what he was doing. And why he chose not to be inclusive-- too many reasons to say no, and only he could say yes ultimately. My two cents.

### Glamazon — 2012-04-03T07:22:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10695>

Probably best to review about MRSA before passing judgment. [http://www.mayoclinic.com/health/mrsa/DS00735](http://http://www.mayoclinic.com/health/mrsa/DS00735)

In hospitals all patients are on standard precautions. This includes good handwashing, no contact with body fluids. I believe Dr. K said he injected himself with MRSA, not smear or cover himself with it. It's hard to catch MRSA unless you are immune compromised. A lot of people have MRSA natively because we all have staph aureus on our skin. It's part of our normal skin and nose flora.

As a former ICU nurse I would bank on Dr. K. that he did the right thing. If you have never worked with a surgeon you have no idea how demanding they are of following protocol. The good ones always are obsessed with safety and the well being of their patients and their crew.

I have worked with 100s of them in my 30 year nursing career. Nurses know fairly well who is good and who is not. Nurses know who to go to for treatment and who to run from in the other direction. No doctor can be reckless and get away with it in a good facility. After observing Dr K on the blog and what he's doing I would say I have a high degree of confidence in his ethics and expertise.

I believe he would not have done anything to jeopardize his family or those he came in contact with. To do so would not be congruent with what he has displayed so far in his personality and personal ethics.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-03T15:38:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10739>

Two things - he did smear himself. And SA is usually different from MRSA. I wouldn't think having SA in our noses necessarily means we have MRSA natively, no?

Still, he knows the extent to which his experiment would or would not have risked the staff, and the staff seem not to be bothered, which means more than my speculation. Whenever I or others have second-guessed him in the past, it has turned out there was something we didn't know that made sense of it all, and till I have reason to think otherwise, I have no reason to believe this isn't the case here once again.

My biggest concern is the "driving himself home" part, although I can't exactly remember if he said he did that, or just that his wife didn't take him home.

### Glamazon — 2012-04-03T16:40:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-mrsa-experiment-how-do-we-get-dr-kruse-nominated-for-a-nobel.587/post-10766>

> **MamaGrok;10808 wrote:** Two things - he did smear himself. And SA is usually different from MRSA. I wouldn't think having SA in our noses necessarily means we have MRSA natively, no?
>
>
>
> Still, he knows the extent to which his experiment would or would not have risked the staff, and the staff seem not to be bothered, which means more than my speculation. Whenever I or others have second-guessed him in the past, it has turned out there was something we didn't know that made sense of it all, and till I have reason to think otherwise, I have no reason to believe this isn't the case here once again.
>
>
>
> My biggest concern is the "driving himself home" part, although I can't exactly remember if he said he did that, or just that his wife didn't take him home.

No, about 1% of the population have MRSA but they tend to concentrate in hospitals. I see several a month in my patient load who test positive with MRSA by nasal swab. They are most often the elderly with chronic co-morbidities who are admitted from a care facility and the immune compromised. Contact isolation is the precaution.

Sorry, I did miss the smear thing. And, it is still difficult to contact MRSA which is why injecting is really a strong measure to insure he was inoculated. I would be surprised if they didnt betadine scrub or other prep for his torso first before doing surgery. I wasnt there so I can't say. None of us can.

I can understand concern about him driving home.

I cringed when I heard his account because I knew how dangerous what he was doing unless he was so confident in his data he could proceed.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-03T17:43:17+0300

By the way, I had a far-too-long-postponed thorough dental exam today. My suspicions about that root canal turn out to be correct -- seems like a low-grade chronic infection is there, just above the ends of the gutta percha. There are other problems, such as shelves from messy oversized fillings which have been there nearly forever, trapping food and making flossing more difficult. I started talking about lotsa pianos -- here's a five-piano tooth, oh, dear, that is probably a ten-piano tooth. But my gums (which aren't that bad, considering) should be happier, and my immune system will eventually be able to turn its attention to things other than protecting me from my own bad habits, neglect, a childhood diet far too full of sweets and cheap starches -- and some less-than-perfect dental work in the past.

He first presented his findings in 1983. They were dismissed. ["One prominent gastroenterologist dismissed him as 'a crazy guy saying crazy things'.](http://http://www.achievement.org/autodoc/page/mar1bio-1)" Sound familiar??
>
>
>
> In 1985, to the horror of his colleagues, he deliberately infected himself with H.pylori - and sure enough, got seriously ill. Despite all he'd done, it took another ten years before his findings made it into clinical practice - now peptic ulcers are routinely treated with antibiotics. In 2005, he and colleague Dr Robin Warren were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. The Big One.
>
>
>
> Yeah, it only took 20 years!! But compared to MRSA, H.pylori and peptic ulcers are a "nothing" disease. MRSA is an out-of-control, sometimes lethal superbug that costs billions of dollars a year just to TRY to contain. It has evolved resistance to almost all current antibiotics. And now Jack has shown how to totally defeat it. And that's just ONE affliction the Kruse protocol deals with.
>
>
>
> So, who do you know? How can we get this out there?

# Thread: temp of your baths?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-01T23:28:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-9958>

Hi, please let me know how you started your baths and at what temps? what is the lowest temp of a bath?

when you add ice, does it go down to the 40s? and is that desirable? or do we keep the water at 50-55F and no lower?

Today was my first bath at 63F.

### Chrisgraff — 2012-04-01T23:42:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-9969>

1\. Face dunks (50ÂºF) and swimming pool (45m @ 69ÂºF) for a week

2\. Bath, no ice (30m @ 64ÂºF) for a week

3\. Bath (30m @ 60ÂºF) + bag of ice on chest w/T-shirt for three or four days

4\. Bath (30m @ 56ÂºF) + ice on chest (no shirt).

working my way down...

Dr. K made a comment last week that, "50Âº water is perfect". I don't plan on going lower than that.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-02T01:21:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10019>

I get my water down to 54 w/all the ice my fridge can make in a day and two ice blankets, one below and on my back and one on top of me.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T01:33:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10023>

45F from the tap, I add some warm to bring it to 50F, right now, 30 minutes into a CT it is 50.1F but skin is 49F

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-02T01:34:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10024>

got it, so stay above 50F

### mistigi — 2012-04-02T03:03:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10073>

My bath is between 51-55F but it takes around 30lbs of ice to take it there. I use one solid frozen block of ice (4 gal of water) which is not ideal as it does not fully dissolve throughout my bath (60 min).

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-02T03:31:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10083>

I have at times only been able to get my bath down to high 50s when ice has been at a premium in my house (I'm the only one who uses it-hahaha). For me there seems to be a huge difference between 54 and 59. I noticed today that although I was cold I got numb quicker at 54 and the col went away much faster. I didn't shiver at all. I've been doing this for 6 weeks so hopefully most of the shivering will go away by now. So I am going to try to keep it temp below 55 for maximum comfort!

### WestmorelandGrape — 2012-04-02T03:58:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10106>

I am blessed with 52 degree Michigan well water. Works for me without adding any ice. Once the tub is full, I let the faucet trickle to keep the tub full and to keep the water cool. I am up to 45 minutes a day and seeing changes! Good luck, everyone!

### optimeyes — 2012-04-02T04:17:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10129>

I'm in Florida, the water is coming out of the tap at 70-80 degrees and when I add ice (face bath) it melts and gets as cold as 60. LOL!!! Our pool is abt 85 degrees unheated. The CT presents quite a challenge here  I don't care for the face bath, I might just try cold baths and try to work the temp down.

### Mikey7 — 2012-04-02T05:26:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10152>

My trash can? I say been 50s of late since the ambient temp been around that temp, but I still add ice.

### ste colombe — 2012-04-02T05:46:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10160>

Im in Hong Kong so the temperature here is nearly always tropical (its still winter and 26C ....). I fill up huge plastic bottles that I cut open and freeze. I have about 6 of those, the water gets down to 10c and then I have to add a bottle every so often during the bath to maintain the cold. Being a girl, I cover my boobs with my hands as I dont want them to disappear and lay in the bath mainly covered, except for my upper chest, i stay there for about 30 mins, sometimes more depending if I can keep the water tempeature cold. Its going to get worse soon, so I'll be using even more ice. I have always taken cold showers, so I just jumped into the ice bath first off and started at 10 minutes and worked my way up from there....

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T11:19:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10241>

My tap water is around 45 - 50F at the moment - I doubt it will get much warmer! So I don't add ice or anything to it, but still haven't managed to get my skin temperature down below 55.9F. In the depths of winter, my tap water tends to be about 3 - 4C/39F, so I guess I'll have to add some hot water then to bring the temperature up, unless I'm cold adapted by then!

### Qodesh — 2012-04-03T23:02:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10938>

I started with a couple face dunks at what I thought was cold. (70 degrees here in S. Florida.) and cold (same temp) showers.

I spot ice in between.

My baths are about 2-3x /week. started at 70, then got it down to 65 for and hour. My feet took a long time to warm up.

My last bath was 58, feet in for 30 minutes. No prob! Not even the feet. I even felt warm until the final 5 minutes.

I plan to take it down slowly. I'll see if I can get enough ice to bring it down to 55-60 for a couple more times while I bring the time to an hour.

Then down in temp again. 50 for an hour.

This is my plan

### hellojtm — 2012-04-04T00:29:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-of-your-baths.588/post-10998>

my tap water is currently 52-53f. i've added ice to get it down to 48f but doesn't seem worth it to me. although i agree with a previous poster that there may be some benefits to \_even\_ colder if it numbs you quicker

Hi everyone.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-02T01:00:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-is-dropping-bombs-on-fbook-about-underwater-ct.589/post-10013>

I read in this book: [http://www.amazon.com/The-Einstein-Factor-Increasing-Intelligence/dp/076150186X](http://http://www.amazon.com/The-Einstein-Factor-Increasing-Intelligence/dp/076150186X) (I'm 90% it was that book anyway) that we think better while holding our breath. The was an example of some famous guy that I don't remember who would do all his brainstorming in the pool underwater. He even developed some sort of way of writing all his amazing thoughts down under the water.

My other thought is about how much harder it is to hold your breath under cold water. I'm guessing that extending the time you can hold your breath at the same time as CTing could really have a huge impact on lung capacity/performance and all sorts of others things I don't know anything about that are effected in our bodies by our cells oxygen levels.

Soooo, what's he up to???

_Yoshiro Nakamatsu, 83, described his "creativity process", which includes listening to music and concludes with diving underwater, where he says he comes up with his best ideas and records them while underwater. Nakamatsu claims to benefit from lack of oxygen to the brain, making inventions "0.5 seconds before death"._

I have friends who are scuba divers who say that slow, controlled breathing is critical to being able to scuba dive and they are physically exhillarated for 3 days after diving...somehow I think there is a connection to the benefits of underwater CT.

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-03T22:26:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-is-dropping-bombs-on-fbook-about-underwater-ct.589/post-10913>

Years ago now I went diving 3x/day for 2 weeks in the British Virgin Islands. Water is like, bathtub warm (80) and at first you can just jump in, in your skin... as the days progressed and my body temp dropped I ended up adding a dive skin. It's still colder than body temp and I could feel it. I came home 12 lbs lighter, my appetite was smashed. This was while eating SAD. I can only imagine immersion in colder water, eating a ketogenic paleo diet! (Well, I can MORE than imagine it... I am starting to DO IT!)

Seriously though hubby and I talked about breaking out our scuba tanks and sitting at the bottom of the 604 degree pool fully submerged once we are better adapted....!! Can't wait to hear the next step. I only wish I started this earlier!!

### Optigal — 2012-04-04T00:20:32+0300

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-is-dropping-bombs-on-fbook-about-underwater-ct.589/post-11000>

On another note, Dr. K mentioned in his post-talk chit-chat that the dive reflex is why babies born in water don't drown.

# Thread: CT and Aspberger's (one of the autism spectrums)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-aspbergers-one-of-the-autism-spectrums.590/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-04-01T23:56:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-aspbergers-one-of-the-autism-spectrums.590/post-9981>

I'm thinking CT + keto diet would be beneficial to those with Autism and it's spectrums. My 21 yo son has Aspberger's (discovered when he was about 2yo) and over the years he has had many health issues from unusual weight gain, hashimoto's, egg allergy, lethargy and learning disorders. He eats primarily low carb, but does have things that aren't good for him occasionally. He's started taking cooler showers and says he likes the feeling afterward. My question is, could CT fix the issue of Aspberger's syndrome? I'm not pushing the optimal plan...I just talk about it a lot  and I'm hoping he will follow one day.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-01T23:58:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-aspbergers-one-of-the-autism-spectrums.590/post-9983>

yes it would be.........

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T00:09:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-aspbergers-one-of-the-autism-spectrums.590/post-9988>

> **Jack Kruse;10046 wrote:** yes it would be.........

Music to my ears  Seriously.. thank you for sharing what you've learned.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-02T00:51:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-aspbergers-one-of-the-autism-spectrums.590/post-10009>

How long has your son been eating paleo and have you done severe gut healing for him? My daughter was autistic, but aggressive gut healing and a very, very strict paleo diet have erased it almost entirely.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T02:18:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-aspbergers-one-of-the-autism-spectrums.590/post-10047>

> **PaleoMom;10072 wrote:** How long has your son been eating paleo and have you done severe gut healing for him? My daughter was autistic, but aggressive gut healing and a very, very strict paleo diet have erased it almost entirely.

He has done a lot of work on healing his gut.. detoxing, doctor prescribed shakes for healing the gut and pretty close to a paleo diet.. no grains, but he is bit stubborn on giving up potatoes and cane sugar...but is improving on that. Being he is 21, he has to make his own choices, but we work on it together. Happy to hear about your daughter. Yay! How old is she?

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-02T02:55:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-aspbergers-one-of-the-autism-spectrums.590/post-10069>

She is 8 now, but we started all this for her around age 3 or 4. For what it's worth giving up / bringing back potatoes doesn't make any difference for my daughter. Sugar however is right up there with grains.

Have you done anything for heavy metals? That was big for us too. I tried many things (on myself first) that turned out really horrible, but then I found a paleo dentist in my area and he has an amazing chelation protocol that I can't recommend enough [http://idahonaturaldentist.com/mercury-cleanse-program-2](http://http://idahonaturaldentist.com/mercury-cleanse-program-2) It helped my kids with their heavy metals and also my own. Unrelated to autism, but amazing for me was a life-long mystery with my heart that no doctor could figure out... after only 3 weeks on the chelation protocol it was gone! I'm finally able to be active now.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T05:48:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-aspbergers-one-of-the-autism-spectrums.590/post-10161>

> **PaleoMom;10132 wrote:** She is 8 now, but we started all this for her around age 3 or 4. For what it's worth giving up / bringing back potatoes doesn't make any difference for my daughter. Sugar however is right up there with grains.
>
>
>
> Have you done anything for heavy metals? That was big for us too. I tried many things (on myself first) that turned out really horrible, but then I found a paleo dentist in my area and he has an amazing chelation protocol that I can't recommend enough [http://idahonaturaldentist.com/mercury-cleanse-program-2](http://http://idahonaturaldentist.com/mercury-cleanse-program-2) It helped my kids with their heavy metals and also my own. Unrelated to autism, but amazing for me was a life-long mystery with my heart that no doctor could figure out... after only 3 weeks on the chelation protocol it was gone! I'm finally able to be active now.

Thank you for the link.. I've been reading through some of the information.. quite interesting. I plan to order the ebook.. my son doesn't have any mercury fillings, but I do and probably passed onto him during pregnancy. Wish I had a paleo dentist in my area... yours looks awesome.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-02T06:51:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-aspbergers-one-of-the-autism-spectrums.590/post-10204>

I don't have any fillings either, nor do my kids. They are also completely unvaccinated, yet when tested we all three were high in mercury, lead and copper. I mentioned it because I think most autisic people have heavy metals.

# Thread: Getting senior loved ones to become optimal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-04-02T00:01:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-9985>

Does anyone have ideas on how to get older folk to drop the margarine and pick up the butter? To lose the flour and sugar?

Many folk have had their gall bladders removed, and so going to a high fat diet, if they were to try it, can be difficult.

### .lina — 2012-04-03T18:39:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-10808>

maaan, do i ever sympathize with this! i have been a living example of how amazing you can

feel with this kind of eating, embracing butter and shunning carbs, and STILL last week, my

dear mother calls me and gleefully says my dad has signed up for a course which entails

him exercising rigorously and drinking nothing but liquids for weeks. he's almost 60, i kind

of fear for his health :/

but any kind of weightloss will be good for him of course... and my parents have both cut

down on carbs so my hopes are that once he finishes this, he comes into full-on lowcarbing

and not being butter-shy.

does anyone know how it can affect a body to make such a transition though? VLCD to a

low carb and high fat diet -- will there be any positive results on his weight or will it take

longer than had he gone from SAD (well, swedish...) to lowcarb? i don't want him to be

discouraged and quit it before it starts being awesome, and many seem to drop off it at

first sign of ketosis discomfort kicking in.

best of luck to you and your oldies

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T11:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-11655>

I hate this: reading about 'senior loved ones' and then read "almost 60'!

Might I suggest 55-75 might be 'middle aged loved ones.' (75-85= I'll let you know when I get there, in 14 years)

Lots of love, Localad. xxx

### .lina — 2012-04-05T12:23:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-11661>

haha so sorry! let's just say i define it by him as being MY senior?  but you are right of course,

he's but a spring chicken compared to his potential. it just becomes much more evident with

age making people appear much older when there's poor health involved. he's visiting me tomorrow

for the whole easter weekend and is allowed to eat one solid meal per day which me & the bf cooks,

and we're gonna try and really sell in lowcarb with all the deliciousness and healthinessssss

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T13:43:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-11670>

Took me a year of 'paleo' "baking" etc, (you know, all the almondy, cauliflowery, grated zucchini stuff) to get unhooked from a lifetime of grainy 'crusty goodness'. (I don't bother with 'paleo baking' these days.) Take it steady with your Dad, it's worth it!

[](http://http://s70.photobucket.com/albums/i97/nmhsu/?action=view&current=SWS_George_Phillips-3.jpg)

60, not out.

### DarleenMB — 2012-04-06T03:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-12021>

thanks. At 65 I highly resent references to "elders in their 60's". I am NOT there YET.

As to your folks... give 'em a copy of Wheat belly. I snuck a copy on to hubby's Kindle and he actually read it. That was in December. He announced he would give up wheat but not until the first of the year because "it's the holidays."

He was true to his word. I also have several paleo and low-carb cookbooks lying about.

Today he got on the scale and he has EFFORTLESSLY dropped 12 pounds. He was SO proud of himself announcing he "thought" he might have lost some because his jeans felt loose.

### Marsha — 2012-04-06T04:41:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-12039>

I'm with you, DarleenMB and localad!

I'm 65 and must admit that I have felt, at times during this K/Paleo and CT journey, that it's probably too late for me. I mean, how much longer will my telomeres grow with this very late start??!! Man, I wish I'd known about all of this before the vegetarianism, RAW, whole grains and low fat, as well as, decades of SAD before that!

But I _didn't know before and I _have started now__ __- so move over you 'young whipper - snappers' and make room for your so called 'elders'! My mind and heart feel like I'm a more wise 35 - 40 year old!

Today I had an awesome CT session - whole hour in 53-54 degree water rockin' out to _Florence and the Machine_, Young the Giant,_ as well as, The Stones and Led Zeppelin_! (Nearly dropped the I-Pad in the tub yesterday, so reading is out and the i-pod is in!)

That being said - be an example for your parents/grandparents. Let them know how good your life is becoming - presenting wonderful dishes made with love and butter will help, too!

It's amazing how things can change when one knows that another cares enough to say the truth and follow through. I wish my kids had known about this way of living and convinced me by their example - I would have been won over! Now it's the other way around - I have to set an example for them!

### Petra — 2012-04-12T12:39:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-senior-loved-ones-to-become-optimal.591/post-14163>

Everybody has it's own ways of taking care.

For the people I love, the best thing I can do for them, is be the best ME.

What really annoys me, are those folks for whom their diet is like a religion that needs to be forced upon me.

Show me a student, I will be the teacher. I go with that.

Petra

### Pardus — 2012-04-02T06:06:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10172>

I think the problem is that a lot of the people on paleohacks are young, fit, and athletic, and were fortunate enough to be born without serious diseases of civilization such as autoimmune conditions, susceptibility to diabetes or easy weight gain. These are the kinds of people will improve greatly and feel like rockstars on plain ol' paleo. No need for a leptin reset, leaky gut RX or CT.

Since they feel so great on regular paleo, they somehow think everyone else should feel great too. They don't understand that there are some of us out there who are so deranged from the poor pre-natal nutrition, a lifetime of eating the SAD, not being breastfed, being put on anti-biotics, etc....that regular paleo won't cut it.

It's kind of odd, there are newbies coming to paleohacks all the time asking for advice because standard paleo isn't working for them and they need a 'hack'... but all they get is the advice to "add more carbs".. Or they get jumped and criticized for "not being strict enough". What's the point of paleohacks if nobody is hacking anyone, and they all recommend the same solution: "add more carbs!" That's not hacking, that's just dogma.... And it's definitely not fair to people like ourselves who really need that "something extra" to become optimal.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-02T17:56:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10317>

This is a 2fold problem -

ppl are skeptics regarding something different from their accepted paradigm b/c its change from their dogma. when you challenges someone's beliefs, especially w/science - it can shake them to their core - this will either cause action or inaction. Sometimes the action is positive, and sometimes its negative - as the reaction on paleo hacks.

Sometimes the action spurs the desire - to change society - but lets be honest we can't change societal mind think. Change occurs at the individual level. I have a friend on FB who thinks we have to change society - well imo most of society won't change unless its easy - so they will continue to devolve and die. this is where the discussion on Factor X comes in - we either adapt and change to realign our neolithic to the paleolithic or we die, from preventable diseases. Society wants the easy fix, not the final solution, which requires work. WE've become a lazy species.. so instead of fixing ourselves - we think that if we fix society it will fix us.

grr... Total Bullshit IMO.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T22:48:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10474>

Funny. I was just thinking about Dr Atkins being dissed when I came upon your post. And I agree with you that Dr. Kruse will be treated the same way when his book comes out; you are seeing the beginnings of that now.

But I think this is more than just about not liking change. I figured out several years ago that nutrition as practiced in this country is more like a religion than scientifically backed. Nutrition becomes a belief system.

People like Dr Atkins and Dr Kruse aren't just promoting change, they are challenging people's belief systems. That's why they are vilified.

An interesting side note, even up to the early/mid 1960s, if your doctor told you to lose weight, he/she would have given you a low carb plan similar to Atkins. Then the belief system changed to low fat, which I think is one of the worst diet changes ever.

### optimeyes — 2012-04-03T02:01:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10568>

I had labs in Dec (pre-Kruse) and labs in March. I was already doing a low cal, low carb most of the time, paleo

diet. My rt3 in Dec was 577; my rt3 in March was 45. No one else has a cure for high rt3, except some other

fringe people who say to take a lot of t3 till you "burn off" the reverse. These people do this for a long time,

from what I gather, with a lot of symptoms.

The only negative I had on my labs was cholesterol up to 219, and my homocysteine actually went down.

(Better indicator of heart disease.) My weight has not gone anywhere, and I am off thyroid meds all together.

My "hack" is that I am going to stop eggs and bacon for my BAB (bacon was uncured but still pork, so Omega 6 I guess)

and just do grass fed beef, wild salmon and whey as my protein (I do still use dairy)

Anyway, FOR ME the proof is in the puddin' and I have labs showing I am healing myself, thanks to Jack. I don't care if he tells me to start talking backwards to cure these whack things about me. I will give it a try ;-)

I am only just starting CT... those labs were just from a lackadaisical LR (I say that b/c it was not strictly low carb 100% of the time and I had a couple grain infractions) Basically I was strict only about the BAB, and 95% adhered to early bedtime and no late eating; So I am excited to go further on this and see what happens.

### Guest — 2012-04-03T02:32:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10580>

> **hazyjane;10292 wrote:** They have the disease of academia brain
>
> To me, there is a huge difference between theorizing and reading books/research papers vs. practical application and experience. Not only has Jack come under attack, but I've also seen some snarkiness about Nora Gedgaudas. I'm sorry, but Jack and Nora are experienced clinicians who test the heck out of their patients. I'm going to lean toward their experience and knowledge vs. a bunch of people who want to sound smart, yet don't run labs on, or treat patients and who, themselves, do not have a metabolic or autoimmune disease.

I'm a member of Paleohacks, albeit more of a lurker than a poster. I agree that it can sometimes get nasty, but Jack and Nora aren't the only paleo gurus who have come under fire. I've seen Chris Kresser, Kurt Harris, Stephan Guyenet, and Paul Jaminet questioned and judged as well. I actually don't think it's a bad thing for Paleohackers to dissect the arguments of Jack and others. Forums and blogs provide a sort of peer review process for people who are operating outside of academia. If a scientist can't listen to and respond cogently to criticism of his or her ideas, how much is the science worth? I think the last thing the paleo world needs is acolytes unquestioningly following a charismatic leader. That's a cult of personality, not science.

### Birdy — 2012-04-03T02:55:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10589>

Apow, I agree with your viewpoint conceptually, but that is not really what happens at PH. It is really more of a play-pen or the gong show than a peer-review process. Jack gets attacked much more frequently and personally than any other person combined (except perhaps vegans). If I was at a peer-review at work and some of this stuff was said, I would work to get them fired. Too much immaturity and creepy sexual innuendo and misogynist comments. Casual visitors may not notice this as much, but several of the regulars drop nasty notes and questions which are later removed for whatever reason. They do show up on the 9000-plus fb pages before they are edited out. Meanwhile, the perpetrators of this foolishness claim innocence, "Wha?....li'l ole me????" after the drive-by shootings have been cleaned up by other moderators. What kind of creepy cult is that? At least Kruse HAS a personality.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-03T03:20:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10608>

> **APow;10649 wrote:** I'm a member of Paleohacks, albeit more of a lurker than a poster. I agree that it can sometimes get nasty, but Jack and Nora aren't the only paleo gurus who have come under fire. I've seen Chris Kresser, Kurt Harris, Stephan Guyenet, and Paul Jaminet questioned and judged as well. I actually don't think it's a bad thing for Paleohackers to dissect the arguments of Jack and others. Forums and blogs provide a sort of peer review process for people who are operating outside of academia. If a scientist can't listen to and respond cogently to criticism of his or her ideas, how much is the science worth? I think the last thing the paleo world needs is acolytes unquestioningly following a charismatic leader. That's a cult of personality, not science.

Hi, APow! I have no problems with intelligent discourse and skepticism. What I take issue with is the level of snarkiness that I've seen around the blogopshere, even from some people that I've known a long time and like very much. I can tell the difference between disagreeing with someone's theories and a silly personal rivalry, and, like Birdy mentioned, I do see Dr. Kruse being singled out quite a lot right now in rather petty ways. It serves no real purpose.

I also see a whole lot of very erroneous statements being made about what his philosophies actually are: that he's VLC all the time, that he pushes supplements, that he calls himself "Paleo" (he doesn't.)

I also take issue with the insinuation I see around that those of us who are testing Dr. Kruse's theories are unquestioningly following him. You're welcome to spend time around us (I say that sincerely) and you'll see that we constantly pick apart his theories and ask him and each other annoying questions. We compare labs and results ad nauseum. We get annoyed when Dr. Kruse gives us vague or sometimes incomprehensible answers (or at least I do )

He def. makes us work to understand his ideas and he outright asks us to question him and not follow blindly. I can't speak for everyone here, but I don't follow gurus. You can ask Melissa from Hunt Gather Love, who I know well from back when she and I were both raw foodists on a raw food forum (and both challenged the dogma there). There are ideas where Dr. Kruse and I depart on consensus, but I have found a lot of value and real-life results from his methods and I'm really glad that I have. But, if at some point I feel like he's sorely mistaken or I'm not getting results, I have no problem trying other things.

I hope that you can see that what he and his "followers" have been depicted as doesn't totally line up with the reality of the situation.

### Guest — 2012-04-03T04:35:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10660>

> **hazyjane;10705 wrote:** Good point! Clinicians should review work being done by other clinicians. Art critics don't review economic policy and theater critics don't review food...

Unfortunately, it sometimes seems like Jack is saying that he is beyond peer review. He claimed (somewhere on his blog, I think), "I am about the only person who thinks this way in everything I do now." He has responded very defensively to criticism on several Paleohacks threads. You might argue that he responded that way because of the snarky nature of the criticism. But I do think he reacts in a way that shuts down discussion, denigrates the critic, and undermines his argument. So I guess I wonder who his peers would be. If often seems that the majority of bigwigs in the ancestral health movement--clinicians, scientists, trainers, highly educated bloggers--are highly critical of Jack. So I just wonder at what point we all start listening to them a little more carefully.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-03T05:00:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10665>

> **APow;10729 wrote:** Unfortunately, it sometimes seems like Jack is saying that he is beyond peer review. He claimed (somewhere on his blog, I think), "I am about the only person who thinks this way in everything I do now." He has responded very defensively to criticism on several Paleohacks threads. You might argue that he responded that way because of the snarky nature of the criticism. But I do think he reacts in a way that shuts down discussion, denigrates the critic, and undermines his argument. So I guess I wonder who his peers would be. If often seems that the majority of bigwigs in the ancestral health movement--clinicians, scientists, trainers, highly educated bloggers--are highly critical of Jack. So I just wonder at what point we all start listening to them a little more carefully.

And a lot of them have defended him, so it's rather like 6 of one and half a dozen of the other. He's not good at communicating via writing (no offense, Jack!) and when you hear him talk, he's a lot more understandable and comes across as having less bravado. He can come across as arrogant, but I've seen Kurt Harris do the same and sometimes worse (smart guy, but major attitude sometimes!)

From my own experience, I have listened to them and done the "right" Paleo/Primal/safe starch thing and my body still needed something more. I'm getting some remarkable results that I haven't had previous to trying cold thermogenesis. I was already familiar with cold thermogenesis via having read about Kneipp Therapy, Tim Ferriss, classical naturopathy, etc, so to me, it isn't some crazy, silly idea but something that's been around a long time. Dr. Kruse just put it into a different context for me. He hardly invented it.

His clinical experience with severely "broken" patients is something I find very valuable. I also find Nora's clinical experience valuable. I also love Chris Kresser's input because he works with patients. I get tired of people who do not do clinical work with actual patients being so nitpicky and derrogatory toward those that do. Again, I think a lot of this comes down to simple human competitiveness and I find that silly and immature.

So, I'd really just leave off with: don't knock it until you try it.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-03T05:09:28+0300

### Guest — 2012-04-03T08:16:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10698>

I'm not sure how I've become the great defender on Paleohacks here, but I find it ironic that people are lambasting Paleohackers for being too critical . . . while calling them paranoid, uncivil, pathetic, etc. And the charge that Paleohackers are, by and large, young, fit, and athletic is a huge assumption without supporting evidence. I don't know the personal stories of all the people on there, but a quick look at the top posters reveals multiple people diagnosed with serious illnesses, and many others who have had long struggles with obesity.

"Criticism is an indirect form of self-boasting" is a nice, pithy quotation (by Emmit Fox, not Jack Kruse), but it's going to be awfully hard for Dr. Kruse to disseminate his ideas if he doesn't care what his critics think.

See I disagree........when you help people things change. See Ghandi. He did not care what his critics thought. MLK too........to get the change we seek we only need to remain focused on the prize. Focusing in on the small minds is not the prize I am shooting for.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-03T15:45:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10743>

> **Jack Kruse;10799 wrote:** See I disagree........when you help people things change. See Ghandi. He did not care what his critics thought. MLK too........to get the change we seek we only need to remain focused on the prize. Focusing in on the small minds is not the prize I am shooting for.

We're also not going to change society, until society wants to change. so let them rail. I can only worry about me, and what is MINE. As much as I'd love to get DH fully on board - he's not ready for change yet. I can't make him change. whats interesting, is the kids, especially the 4yo has jumped on board - she's asking for cold baths at night so she won't get sick, she's refusing to wear coats when going out side... now when she says she's cold - and we ask if she wants a sweater/coat etc - she says no - I want the cold to keep me healthy. example, inspires change. Why does every woman want to look like Jennifer Annistion? B/c she's got an effing HOT body at +40 (can't say if she's really healthy) - but ppl see her and are inspired to look like her

affect those you can by being the example of optimal - ppl will see what you're doing - ask you what you're doing, and then engage change for themselves. you can't force it. Just like you can't force an alcoholic to get help if they don't want it. Change requires the individual to reach the point of no return... like Ivy said earlier in this thread - the 20-somethings, who don't have any disease and can xfit me around the block and back - don't need this now...but in 10-15yrs You can bet they'll be lining up for DrK's info.

I've read paleohacks - and other forums where "expert bashing" goes on in full force. And I choose not to engage - b/c it's frustrating. I will say there have been very good discussions - where bashing did not occur, and the analytical data actually convinced some ppl to serious look at Kruse - one person who was anti-Kruse when he first came out even tried the LR - don't know about the individuals success/failure b/c they weren't a regular poster...but when the individual decided to do the LR - they had reached the tipping point - they couldn't go back to what they were doing b/c it wasn't working...so why not try something else... took this individual close to 8mths to get to this point...

I truly embrace the Ayn Rand concept of self before others... If I take care of me... I can take care of others, and teach them what I did for myself...and they can do the same, and pass it on... In all honesty I'm a selfish -bitch. but the prioritization of me = a longer heather life, so I can be around for the duration of my kids lives, and those of my grandchildren

### hazyjane — 2012-04-03T19:05:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paranoia-at-paleohacks.592/post-10830>

> **APow;10767 wrote:** I'm not sure how I've become the great defender on Paleohacks here, but I find it ironic that people are lambasting Paleohackers for being too critical . . . while calling them paranoid, uncivil, pathetic, etc. And the charge that Paleohackers are, by and large, young, fit, and athletic is a huge assumption without supporting evidence. It isn't Paleohacks that I have a problem with. Forums with be forums and I hardly go on Paleohacks. It's some of the bloggers that are an issue because they are the public face of Paleo/Primal.

There's a way to dissect and critique someone's ideas in a rational and neutral way and then there are personal attacks. I'm seeing the latter. Kevin Cottrell actually called out these people for being immature and snarky and even Robb Wolf mentioned the behavior as being inappropriate.

Whenever I see personal attacks (like when Matt Stone used to single out other bloggers to make himself look good at their expense), I assume that person is insecure, frustrated or has a rivalry. If that's not the case, than they need to realize the image they're creating for themselves!

As an example, read the tags on a blog post about Nora Gedgaudas: "bullshit, carbs, coprolites, did you even read it?, seriously?, wtf" and on this one about Dr. Kruse: "bullshit, decline, fall, rant, woo".

Hmmm... could that blogger be letting their emotions get in the way of rational discussion? Is their self-described "rivalry" taking precedence over professionalism? Is this behavior making the "Paleosphere" look immature and petty?

# Thread: What do we know about brown adipose tissue
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/>

### AKMan — 2012-04-04T21:44:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-11421>

Just read this on [www.physorg.com](http://http://www.physorg.com)

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-01-brown-fat-calories-adult-humans.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-01-brown-fat-calories-adult-humans.html)

Brown adipose tissue (often known as brown fat) is a specialized tissue that burns calories to generate body heat in rodents and newborn humans, neither of which shiver.

Recently, adult humans have also been found to possess brown fat. This fact piqued the interest of researchers seeking to combat the obesity epidemic, the thought being that if they could develop ways to increase the amount of brown fat a person has that person will become slimmer. One hitch to this idea is it has never actually been shown definitively that brown fat in adult humans can burn energy.

Now, a team of researchers — led by AndrÃ© C. Carpentier, at UniversitÃ© de Sherbrooke, Sherbrooke, Quebec; and Denis Richard, at UniversitÃ© Laval, Quebec City, Quebec — has provided this evidence, showing that when healthy adult men are exposed to cold their brown fat burns energy to generate body heat. However, it did not burn energy at warm temperatures.

As Barbara Cannon and Jan Nedergaard, at Stockholm University, Sweden, discuss in an accompanying commentary, these data have significant implications for the human obesity epidemic. In particular, they note that the data generated by Carpentier, Richard, and colleagues indicates that developing ways to increase the amount of brown fat a person has is unlikely to make that person slimmer, what is needed is a way to make sure that the brown fat is active and burns calories.

### AKMan — 2012-04-04T21:52:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-11423>

I can't get enough of these! Here is another one: [http://www.jci.org/articles/view/60433?key=5e3684aee3d55b74adc8](http://http://www.jci.org/articles/view/60433?key=5e3684aee3d55b74adc8)

This is the main point to me: Your CT activities should be done without shivering. Shivering stops BAT activity, as found here:

In summary, the present study demonstrates that a cold-exposure stimulus designed to minimize muscle-mediated shivering thermogenesis enhances BAT oxidative metabolism as well as glucose and NEFA uptake in adult humans. The enhanced BAT activity was associated with a 1.8-fold increase in whole-body energy expenditure. We found a significant inverse relationship between BAT volume of activity and shivering and a significant increase in BAT radio density within 3 hours of cold exposure, indicating rapid reduction in BAT triglyceride content. The present results demonstrate that BAT is undoubtedly involved in nonshivering thermogenesis in humans.

Note: This study was done with 18 deg C (65 deg F) water.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T02:16:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-16397>

[http://www.mendeley.com/research/effects-diet-cold-acclimation-lipid-composition-rat-interscapular-brown-adipose-tissue/](http://http://www.mendeley.com/research/effects-diet-cold-acclimation-lipid-composition-rat-interscapular-brown-adipose-tissue/)

**

Effects of diet and cold acclimation on lipid composition of rat interscapular brown adipose tissue.**

The effects of diet and of cold acclimation on lipid composition of interscapular brown adipose tissue (BAT) were studied in rats. Three groups of animals were acclimated for 20 weeks to 28 degrees C (controls), 5 degrees C (CA) or to a daily fluctuating temperature (Cy). They received either normal fat (5 p. 100 lipids) NF or high fat (26 p. 100) HF diets. Cold acclimation increased the tissue weight and its protein, phospholipid, free fatty acid and cholesterol content. It decreased the total lipid proportion in the tissue. HF diet led to an increase in lipid content and to a decrease in the protein content only in control and Cy groups. The mean chain length of triglyceride or phospholipid fatty acids was increased by HF diet and cold acclimation. The proportion of unsaturated acids was not changed in cold acclimated rats, but the amount of arachidonic acid bound to phospholipid was significantly increased. It is concluded that the total amount of lipid in the diet acts on the synthesis of BAT lipids. The cold dependant changes in lipid composition which are similar but less important in cyclic cold acclimation than in constant cold adaptation seem to be proportional to the increase in the tissue energy metabolism.

I'm still thinking fat's perty good for a cold adapted gal.

BTW There are tons of cold adapted rat studies from the 60's on. Everything changes in the cold. I am a coarse communicator, but there is some good stuff on epididimys fat and growth hormone.

### chocolate — 2012-05-08T19:19:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-22280>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20557470](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20557470)

Significance and application of melatonin in the regulation of brown adipose tissue metabolism: relation to human obesity.

Tan DX, Manchester LC, Fuentes-Broto L, Paredes SD, Reiter RJ.

Source

Department of Cellular and Structural Biology, the University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio, San Antonio, TX 78229, USA.

Abstract

A worldwide increase in the incidence of obesity indicates the unsuccessful battle against this disorder. Obesity and the associated health problems urgently require effective strategies of treatment. The new discovery that a substantial amount of functional brown adipose tissue (BAT) is retained in adult humans provides a potential target for treatment of human obesity. BAT is active metabolically and disposes of extra energy via generation of heat through uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria. The physiology of BAT is readily regulated by melatonin, which not only increases recruitment of brown adipocytes but also elevates their metabolic activity in mammals. It is speculated that the hypertrophic effect and functional activation of BAT induced by melatonin may likely apply to the human. **Thus, melatonin, a naturally occurring substance with no reported toxicity, may serve as a novel approach for treatment of obesity. Conversely, because of the availability of artificial light sources, excessive light exposure after darkness onset in modern societies should be considered a potential contributory factor to human obesity as light at night dramatically reduces endogenous melatonin production. In the current article, the potential associations of melatonin, BAT, obesity and the medical implications are discussed.**

I saw the full-text of this study recently. It's implications are huge.

It demonstrates quite readily that melatonin regulates BAT activation. Unfortunately, most humans are melatonin-deranged due to light abuse, that is getting doses of artificial light that interfere with normal circadioan rythms for melatonin production.

This study implies that BAT growth is more tied to light cycles and normal melatonin secretion than to cold. It makes sense, when the light cycle is shorter, as in Winter, more melatonin is produced, activating more BAT.

This study also goes into detail about BAT as a mechanism for 'overeating thermogenesis'. When we take in too many calories, BAT activates to burn these calories as heat regardless of temperature (cold thermogenesis).

When I first read this study, I thought--cool, I'll start popping melatonin pills before bed, but I don't think it will be that simple. The melatonin that the body produces is done in a certain sequence, starting off slowly, increasing around midnight, then tapering til morning. Melatonin supplements provide a huge slug of melatonin that is mostly gone by midnight. You'd need a super-formulated time-release type pill to mirror mother nature.

### MJ* — 2012-05-10T19:03:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-22881>

N~Doro that first post was interesting, could peppermint soap do the trick?:

Saturday, May 5, 2012

Dr. Oz, Pain, Hot/Cold Receptors

The production of endorphins in tissues in response to nerve stimulation relieves inflammation and pain. Dr. Oz and the medical community seem to forget that hot and cold receptors in the skin can be readily triggered by natural products in foods, to stimulate the release of endorphins and treat inflammation in nearby tissues.

Hot and Cold Don't Easily Penetrate the Skin

Athletic trainers commonly apply hot and cold packs to avoid swelling and inflammation from injuries, and they are always discussing the virtues of various hot/cold regimens. They ignore the extraordinary efficiency of the circulatory system in regulating tissue temperatures and avoiding temperature changes. They also ignore the fact that chemical "hot and cold" salves and ointments are effective without actually changing the temperature of the skin. The essential observation is that triggering hot and cold sensing nerves is more important than changing the temperature of the damaged tissues.

Common Food Molecules Activate Hot/Cold Nerve Receptors

Peppers are hot, because they contain capsaicin that binds to protein receptors on nerves in the skin, which results in the brain sensation of heat. Camphor and castor oil bind to the same receptors. Menthol binds to corresponding cold receptors. Vicks Vaporub has both menthol and camphor, and therefore stimulates both hot and cold sensors. Vicks is also an effective treatment for tissue inflammation.

Vicks and Castor Oil are Effective Treatments for Pain and Inflammation

A bee sting or a burn on a finger will produce reddening, swelling and pain, that can be quickly alleviated by applying Vicks to the wrist. The hot and cold sensors of the wrist would be stimulated and the returning nerve signals would be generally detected in the whole hand and produce endorphins that would calm the inflammation and sooth the injured finger. In a similar way, an inflamed joint can be treated by topical menthol and castor oil, and lower abdominal discomfort can be alleviated by castor oil applied to the belly.

Tendonitis can be Treated with Peppermint Soap

I have treated a persistent tendonitis in my shoulder by applying Dr. Bronner's Peppermint Soap liberally to my shoulder and waiting a few minutes before continuing my shower. This gentle, persistent treatment produced relief within a week. This was a cure for this persistent inflammation and pain. It also works on joints.

What Dr. Oz needs to communicate is that there are simple ways to stimulate hot/cold receptors that have nothing to do with changing the temperature of deeper tissues, but these treatments are very effective in stimulating general endorphin production that reduces troublesome inflammation and pain. As an addendum, vagal stimulation, i.e. through yoga postures such as shavasana or the Valsalva maneuver, can produce a reduction in general inflammation.

### chocolate — 2012-05-13T21:00:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-we-know-about-brown-adipose-tissue.593/post-23724>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21820402](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21820402)

Characteristics of the turnover of uncoupling protein 3 by the ubiquitin proteasome system in isolated mitochondria.

Mookerjee SA, Brand MD.

Abstract

Uncoupling protein 3 (UCP3) is implicated in mild uncoupling and the regulation of mitochondrial ROS production. We previously showed that **UCP3 turns over rapidly in C2C12 myoblasts, with a half-life of 0.5-4h, and that turnover can be reconstituted in vitro. We show here that **rapid degradation of UCP3 in vitro in isolated brown adipose tissue mitochondria required the 26S proteasome, ubiquitin, ATP, succinate to generate a high membrane potential, and a pH of 7.4 or less.** _Ubiquitin containing lysine-48_ was both necessary and sufficient to support UCP3 degradation, implying a requirement for polyubiquitylation at this residue. The 20S proteasome did not support degradation. UCP3 degradation was prevented by simultaneously blocking matrix ATP generation and import, showing that ATP in the mitochondrial matrix was required. Degradation did not appear to require a transmembrane pH gradient, but was very sensitive to membrane potential: degradation was halved when membrane potential decreased 10-20mV from its resting value, and was not significant below about 120mV. We propose that matrix ATP and a high membrane potential are needed for UCP3 to be polyubiquitylated through lysine-48 of ubiquitin and exported to the cytosolic 26S proteasome, where it is de-ubiquitylated and degraded.**

lysine 48 is a player in the iron reactions also [http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/ja209297n](http://http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/ja209297n)

Control of the Evolution of Iron Peroxide Intermediate in Superoxide Reductase from Desulfoarculus baarsii. Involvement of Lysine 48 in Protonation
