### chocolate — 2012-04-29T16:16:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-many-talents-of-cytokine-il-6.594/post-19768>

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interleukin\_6 Il 6 interacts with this...

Characteristics

The members of the IL-6 receptor family all complex with gp130 for signal transduction. For example, IL-6 binds to the IL-6 Receptor. The complex of these two proteins then associates with gp130. This complex of 3 proteins then homodimerizes to form a hexameric complex which can produce downstream signals.\[3\] There are many other proteins which associate with gp130, such as cardiotrophin 1 (CT-1), leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF), ciliary neurotrophic factor (CNTF), oncostatin M (OSM), and IL-11.\[4\] There are also several other proteins which have structural similarity to gp130 and contain the WSXWS motif and preserved cysteine residues. Members of this group include LIF-R, OSM-R, and G-CSF-R.

Loss of gp130

gp130 is an important part of many different types of signaling complexes. Inactivation of gp130 is lethal to mice.\[5\] Homozygous mice who are born show a number of defects including impaired development of the ventricular myocardium. Haematopoietic effects included reduced numbers of stem cells in the spleen and liver.

Signal transduction

gp130 has no intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity. Instead, it is phosphorylated on tyrosine residues after complexing with other proteins. The phosphorylation leads to association with JAK/Tyk tyrosine kinases and STAT protein transcription factors.\[6\] In particular, STAT-3 is activated which leads to the activation of many downstream genes. Other pathways activated include RAS and MAPK signaling.

### chocolate — 2012-05-05T20:48:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-many-talents-of-cytokine-il-6.594/post-21651>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16702321](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16702321)

Specific amino acids increase mucin synthesis and microbiota in dextran sulfate sodium-treated rats.

Faure M, Mettraux C, Moennoz D, Godin JP, Vuichoud J, Rochat F, BreuillÃ© D, Obled C, CorthÃ©sy-Theulaz I.

Source

NestlÃ© Research Center, Nutrition and Health Department, Switzerland. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

Abstract

During the anabolic response associated with inflammation, mucin synthesis and colonic protection may be compromised by the limited availability of specific amino acids. We therefore determined the effect of dietary amino acid supplementation on the microbiota, mucin status, and mucosal damage in dextran sulfate sodium (DSS)-treated rats. F**rom 8 d before to 28 d after colitis induction, male Sprague-Dawley rats (10 mo old, n = 8/group) were fed a control diet supplemented or not with 2 different doses of an amino acid cocktail containing L-threonine, L-serine, L-proline, and L-cysteine. All diets were isonitrogenous (adjusted with L-alanine). The higher dose of amino acids increased the number of Muc2-containing goblet cells in the surface epithelium of the ulcerated area, stimulated mucin production in the colon, and restored the mucin amino acid composition and mucosal content to healthy, control values. The colonic mucin synthesis rate was specifically stimulated by 95%, whereas the protein turnover was unchanged. All bacterial populations, markedly altered by the DSS treatment, were promoted. In conclusion, in inflammatory situations, an increase in threonine, serine, proline, and cysteine dietary supply can promote mucin synthesis, reequilibrate the gut microbiota, and thus favor colonic protection** and mucosal healing.

I'm taking the MSM to balance the iron. Maybe it's being held back because of a sulfur deficiency. I know it isn't the ideal, but there are no carbs this way and I want my gut right before I reintroduce.

### chocolate — 2012-05-05T20:51:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-many-talents-of-cytokine-il-6.594/post-21652>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1856798/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1856798/)

Comparative study of the intestinal mucus barrier in normal and inflamed colon

Aim

To study the role of mucus in the spatial separation of intestinal bacteria from mucosa.

Patients and methods

Mucus barrier characteristics were evaluated using histological material obtained by biopsy from purged colon, colon prepared with enema and material from untreated appendices fixed with non‐aqueous Carnoy solution. Bacteria were evaluated using fluorescence in situ hybridization, with bacterial 16S RNA probes and related to the periodic acid Schiff alcian blue stain. Biopsies from controls (n = 20), patients with self‐limiting colitis (SLC; n = 20), ulcerative colitis (n = 20) and 60 randomly selected appendices were investigated.

Results

The mucosal surface beneath the mucus layer was free of bacteria in \[gt-or-equal, slanted\]80% of the normal appendices and biopsies from controls. The thickness of the mucus layer and its spread decreased with increasing severity of the inflammation; the epithelial surface showed bacterial adherence, epithelial tissue defects and deep mucosal infiltration with bacteria and leucocytes. Bacteria and leucocytes were found within mucus in all biopsy specimens from patients with ulcerative colitis, SLC, and acute appendicitis. The concentration of bacteria within mucus was inversely correlated to the numbers of leucocytes.

**Conclusions

The large bowel mucus layer effectively prevents contact between the highly concentrated luminal bacteria and the epithelial cells in all parts of the normal colon. Colonic inflammation is always accompanied by breaks in the mucus barrier. Although the inflammatory response gradually reduces the number of bacteria in mucus and faeces, the inflammation itself is not capable of preventing bacterial migration, adherence to and invasion of the mucosa.

**

TRPM8 senses cold and the body makes IL-6.

Traditionally, doesn't IL-6 lead to TH17? And isn't TH17 found in high concentrations in all kinds of autoimmune disorders - leads to inflammation? So I'm just trying to match this up with CT shutting down inflammation. Anyone help me, please?

### shilohman — 2012-08-31T18:02:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-many-talents-of-cytokine-il-6.594/post-48580>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;49041 wrote:** I'm still having a hard time grasping the cascade here.
>
> TRPM8 senses cold and the body makes IL-6.
>
>
>
> Traditionally, doesn't IL-6 lead to TH17? And isn't TH17 found in high concentrations in all kinds of autoimmune disorders - leads to inflammation? So I'm just trying to match this up with CT shutting down inflammation. Anyone help me, please?

CT increases DHEA, DHEA reduces 'excess' IL-6. IL-6 is only harmful when in excess. Its like TNFa, they both are anti-inflammatory until there is a prolonged excess. Everything is context.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-09-06T17:32:31+0300

# Thread: Campfire stories - Enkidu's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/campfire-stories-enkidus-journal.597/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Enkidu — 2012-04-02T04:16:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/campfire-stories-enkidus-journal.597/post-10127>

"Midway upon the journey of our life

I found myself within a forest dark,

For the straightforward pathway had been lost." - Dante Alighieri

I'd say I've been lost a bit longer than Dante. And at 30 everyone tells me I'm not midway through life, but with my current state of health, I believe I may be or even more-so, but hey lets not dwell on the angst. You came here for stories yes?

"Shall we begin like 'David Copperfield' I am born... I grew up. Or shall we begin when I was born to darkness."

Oh right, yeah we were going to try to avoid angst... guess channeling Anne Rice's Louis de Pointe du Lac probably isn't the best way to begin.

Ok, so I was bit by a radioactive cave-man.... yeah, I don't believe me either.

I've been stalked, as we all have, by the great beast mortality. But my slow speed, particular waddle, and size make me an appealing target. I had pretty much resigned myself to an early grave. It's then that Mark Sisson threw me a spear! Then urged me to follow some fellow named Grok. Now,to understand exactly how useful this was to me, know this, I have never been a hunter. I spend all my time in libraries. On occasion I'll fancy myself a bard and write a bit of poetry or perform a bit of magic, but that's about the limit of my creative and physical exertion. Sure I have very dear friends that wander around the forest protecting the world from evil, but one can't borrow skill as one borrows a cup of coconut flour. Anyway! So there I was with a spear and the undefeatable beast. And I had no idea what I was doing...so I ran, or rather waddled quickly to Grok. I figured Mark meant me to learn from him how to use the spear against the beast. But, that's not what Grok had in mind. Instead, he showed me how to hunt and gather my food. I started to eat as he did. I felt better and grew more proficient. I spent a bit less time in my library, and started to learn to enjoy being outside (yes I lost a bit of weight too, but I still waddled.) The beast lagged behind just a little, giving me a bit more space. I discovered Robb Wolf and Matt Lalonde, they reinforced what I had been doing. Sean Croxton offered support and encouragement. And then I also came across Dr. Kruse's writings; so now I make my way to a land of ice and snow, to the midnight sun where the hot springs blow.

Oh... you wanted numbers and stuff... umm ok.

SW: 480

CW: 440

Went primal January 19th.

Started CT ~3 weeks ago with cold showers, (my tap comes out ~60 right now) I've taken one bath with ice so far. It was 30 minutes, but I could only get the temperature down to the upper 50s. As for Leptin Rx, I'm not exactly doing it. I eat a BAB within 30 minutes of waking and eat dinner 4-6 hours before bed, but my shift is noon to nine pm, so I get up at 9am instead of around dawn. When I get home at night I do institute an electronics and artificial light curfew at 11pm. Shortly after that I'm in bed.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-02T04:20:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/campfire-stories-enkidus-journal.597/post-10131>

love it dude and welcome to the road to optimal.

### Enkidu — 2012-04-10T01:05:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/campfire-stories-enkidus-journal.597/post-13155>

This is a story of temptation, and of cultural norms overcoming momentum. To understand exactly what happened, we must speak of Thalia. Thalia is a sweet goddess, one whose attention is usually welcome, she is the goddess whose domain includes Banquets. She and I have a long friendship, often I invoke her when I host seasonal poetry gatherings. Anyway, that's a bit of a digression. Recently though, she's gotten a bit annoyed that I don't engage in her style of celebration. She'll try to tempt me by taking possession of a co-worker and offering homemade baked goods, or she'll arrange for my work to cater lunch or breakfast, but I'm mostly good at resisting her charms. But yesterday, yesterday she got the best of me. It started with a call to watch the roads quiet early... (my day job is in the service to Hermes and Asclepius. I keep Hermes' road clear of obstructions and bandits so Asclepius can use it to perform his duties,) Anyway, one of my co-workers had requested the day off so she could spend the holiday with her family, and I stepped in to cover her shift. So, I would need to wake up four hours earlier than usual. This wasn't aided by a party I attend Saturday. So, I ended up with two hours sleep. This had a bit of an impact on my judgement. The shift was also 12 hours, when I'm usually work 8, this also worked to weaken my resolve. So, when Thalia took possession of my wife and encouraged me to feast in celebration of the holiday, I eagerly accepted. We went to a restaurant and I eagerly ate mounds of poison (breaded-fried fish, shrimp, claims, french fries, and brownie topped with ice cream, whip cream, and syrup) I feasted gloriously. Today, I am bloated, stricken with gas and cramps, cranky, and not at all pleasant to be around. So I beg Thalia, if you are to present me with a feast, please make it filled with meat that has not been coated in flour and deep fried in vegetable oil. And further, I make offerings to Ganesha to clear away Satan's most devious of tools, the french fry, from my path.

### Lyndra — 2012-04-10T05:08:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/campfire-stories-enkidus-journal.597/post-13234>

Serving the goddess should be noble, no? As you say, let us hope next time she'll tempt you with a feast you can truly enjoy without regret. In the mean time, regroup and keep moving forward one step at a time.

### Enkidu — 2012-04-10T18:46:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/campfire-stories-enkidus-journal.597/post-13381>

Thanks for the advice; that's exactly my plan of action. Accept where I am and move forward, regain the ground I've lost and keep going.

But I respectfully disagree about serving of goddess and gods as being noble. They've their own agendas, and putting them on a pedestal gives them a bit of a power trip. They're really a fickle lot, and one's place in their plans may not be to one's benefit. After all, we can't all be chosen heros, and even those of us who are should be considered unlucky.

The enemies of a hero's patron become the hero's enemies. How difficult a life did Hercules have because of Hera? Odysseus was frequently challenged by Poseidon. And then there's Aenus who was victim so many schemes by Juno. No, heroing is not something in which I wish to engage.

If I am to work with the gods, I'll follow the example of Moses who argued with Jehovah and won. Or Jacob who wrestled with an Angel. I'll not be meek and submit as Job did, That's what got me into the mess in the first place.

But, if I am to make offerings, or gain the favor of a goddess. Let it be Athena under the name Liberty, or perhaps Hecate who could lead me to secrets and rebirth, or maybe my CT will win me a bit of favor with The Moirae who will add back a bit of string to my telomeres. I'm not so shallow as to devote myself to a goddess of parties and feasting.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-02T05:50:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scleroderma-lupus.598/post-10163>

I have a very dear friend who is in her mid-30's, I've known her for 12 years and has had a multitude of health problems in that time. Being very overweight she had gastric bypass surgery and lost over 100 pounds. She mad many complications following the surgery. She was later diagnosed with lupus. Most recently she starting having more digestive issues and went to her gastroenterologist who blew her off saying "you chose to rearrange your insides with the bypass surgery, now you have to deal with the consequences." He refused to do anything for her. She continued to get sicker and sicker and went to see another doctor who put her in the hospital and she was diagnosed with scleroderma effecting the digestive system. I know there are now only certain foods she can eat and keep down and she gets much of her nutrition via IV each night. She is seeing specialists, but it seems that all they can do is keep trying different meds to help her symptoms. Is there any chance CT could help her? I guess it couldn't hurt to try anyhow if I could convince her of it. I doubt she could pull of the ketogenic diet just because I know she has a lot of food restrictions. Any thoughts or advice?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-02T06:10:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scleroderma-lupus.598/post-10176>

SHE needs Terry Wahl's version of the Paleo diet and the CT protocol.......stat.

I can't wait to start seeing some positive improvements in myself, though I don't have any major debilitating problems. I need to lose weight and I've been hypothyroid. When others see those changes in me, I know it will be so much easier for them to see what I'm doing has merit! There are so many other sick people I"d like to speak to, but this particular friends has just had things so hard that I don't want her to wait!

# Thread: Dietary Menthol as Cold Thermogenesis / Cold Adaptation Supplement?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gambolputty — 2012-04-02T17:48:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-10312>

I came across this study and found it quite interesting so I thought I would share it here. I am presently at the face dunking stage of the CT protocol. I would love to think that adding menthol to my diet could help speed the CT process. Peppermint oil is roughly 30-50% menthol. It is available in capsules, but often blended with other oils for help with gastric issues. It would be great to find a source for 100% Peppermint Oil capsules.

Anyway, I would love to hear feed back from more knowledgeable members. I posted this info at MDA and it attracted a gaggle of Trolls.

Activation of the cold-sensing TRPM8 channel triggers UCP1-dependent thermogenesis and prevents obesity

Abstract

Brown adipose tissue (BAT) is an energy-expending organ that produces heat. Expansion or activation of BAT prevents obesity and diabetes. Chronic cold exposure enhances thermogenesis in BAT through uncoupling protein 1 (UCP1) activation triggered via a β-adrenergic pathway. Here, we report that the cold-sensing transient receptor potential melastatin 8 (TRPM8) is functionally present in mouse BAT. Challenging brown adipocytes with menthol, a TRPM8 agonist, up-regulates UCP1 expression and requires protein kinase A activation. Upon mimicking long-term cold exposure with chronic dietary menthol application, menthol significantly increased the core temperatures and locomotor activity in wild-type mice; these effects were absent in both TRPM8−/− and UCP1−/− mice. Dietary obesity and glucose abnormalities were also prevented by menthol treatment. Our results reveal a previously unrecognized role for TRPM8, suggesting that stimulation of this channel mediates BAT thermogenesis, which could constitute a promising way to treat obesity.

Link:

[http://jmcb.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2012/02/17/jmcb.mjs001.full](http://http://jmcb.oxfordjournals.org/content/early/2012/02/17/jmcb.mjs001.full)

A note on safety: While menthol is GRAS for human consumption, there are limits. Please note: Ingested pure menthol can be poisonous - as little as a teaspoonful (1 gram per kilogram of body weight) can be fatal ([http://www.rx.com/reference/natural/Peppermint.jhtml](http://http://www.rx.com/reference/natural/Peppermint.jhtml)). The estimated acceptable daily intake is up to 200 micrograms per kilogram of body weight (Martindale, The Extra Pharmacopoeia, 1993). Dr. James Duke at the USDA Agricultural Research Center estimates that the LD50 (dose at which half the animals die) for menthol is higher than coffee, oral LD50 in rats of 192 mg/kg (CRC Handbook of Medicinal Herbs, 1987).

### primat — 2012-04-02T18:03:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-10323>

So that's why the menthol cigarettes kept me skinny back in the days

### janagram — 2012-04-21T02:25:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17268>

Oh, man, those Kools!

### MJ* — 2012-04-21T16:15:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17381>

Gambolputty first welcome to this forum, I see this is your first post. I don't think you'll find trolls here. Everyone's here because they want to be optimal! I like this theory, it's really interesting. I'd like to hear what Dr. Kruse thinks too. I wonder also is there menthol in peppermints? My father lived to be almost 90 and he ate a lot of cake and bad stuff and he ate peppermint patties everyday, and he never had a weight problem!

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T16:59:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17408>

I took some pure little tiny balls of peppermint that are supposed to be an anti-parasitic. I got them a few years back.

### AKMan — 2012-04-21T19:23:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17473>

I think the idea has merit, but keep in mind this was a study on mice. You can also find studies with capsinoids, the non-heat part of hot-peppers that also activate thermogenesis. I try to add a little cayenne to about everything I eat, not sure if it helps, but I like it so I do it. Same could be said for menthol. I doubt it is the magic bullet, but if it fits in your diet and taste, give it a try.

I'd be willing to bet that study got the attention of a drug manufacturer and they are scrambling to isolate compounds they can market for obesity.

Thanks for posting!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-21T20:19:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17488>

I've used spirit of peppermint (in water) now and then for years. Wish it meant I was not fat!

You can grow peppermint and tincture it, or use it in cooking. It is frequently used in Middle Eastern cuisine.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T23:54:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17550>

[http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Spearmint/fulltext%285%29.pdf](http://http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Spearmint/fulltext%285%29.pdf)

Something jumped into my head about this.....it reduces testosterone, which is good for the ladies and the facial hair....I am finding that these things are very rarely ac/dc. Just like the berries are good for male Parkinson's and no improvement for females.

A little excerpt:

**A significant decrease in triglyceride levels was found

after the consumption of spearmint tea in hirsute

women. Women with endogenous hyperandrogenism

often have high triglyceride levels associated with insulin

resistance and obesity. Partial androgen suppression

or the pure antiandrogen treatment with flutamide in

PCOS results in no change in insulin resistance or

lipoprotein levels, indicating that lipoprotein abnormalities

are due to insulin resistance and not to the androgen

levels (Vrbikova et al., 2004; Wild et al., 1992).**

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-22T00:12:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17556>

Very interesting, Chocolate -- thank you.

Note that the study uses spearmint tea instead of peppermint. Might not have quite the same effect.

One can grow spearmint too, of course.

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T00:33:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17564>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15941008](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15941008)

Toxicol Ind Health. 2004 Sep;20(6-10):119-22.

**Effect of Mentha piperita (Labiatae) and Mentha spicata (Labiatae) on iron absorption in rats.**

Akdogan M, Gultekin F, Yontem M.

Source

Department of Biochemistry, Suleyman Demirel University, Faculty of Medicine, Isparta, Turkey. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])

Abstract

AIM:

The effect of Mentha piperita (Labiatae) and Mentha spicata (Labiatae) teas, which contain different phenol compounds, on iron metabolism was studied. These teas grow in different areas of the city of Isparta, Turkey. These herbals were given to the rats in tea.

METHODS:

Forty-eight male Wistar albino rats weighing 200-250 g were used for this study. The rats were divided into four groups of 12 animals: Group I received no herbal tea (control group); Group II received 20 g/L M. piperita tea; Group III received 20 g/L M. spicata tea; Group IV received 40 g/L M. spicata tea. Herbal teas were prepared daily and provided at all times to the rats over 30 days as drinking water.

RESULTS:

M. piperita tea caused a decrease in serum iron and ferritin levels (P < 0.05), and caused an increase in unsaturated iron-binding capacity (UIBC) (P < 0.01). M. spicata tea caused no significant change in serum iron, ferritin levels and UIBC (P > 0.05).

CONCLUSION:

Both herbal teas inhibited Fe absorption. Inhibition caused by M. spicata tea was dose dependent. Therefore, when drinking these teas, their effect should be considered, especially for children and anemic patients.

PMID:

15941008

\[PubMed - indexed for MEDLINE\]

### Tizawee — 2012-04-22T00:38:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17567>

Peppermint oil capsules are already in use for relief from IBS symptoms. Not sure how well they work: I just googled peppermint and found it.

I have been using peppermint oil orally for about 18 months. Ordered it from Amazon. I use it to brush my teeth, to rinse afterwards and as a breath freshener throughout the day. I don't swallow the morning routine stuff but do swallow the breath freshener. I was under the impression that one has to be careful with how much is ingested but can't find the reference that told me. I have been thinking about mixing it with almond oil and trying it for oil pulling.

For anyone thinking about using straight peppermint oil orally, be aware that it's v-e-r-y strong. Until you get used to it, put one drop on your tongue and swish it around your whole mouth. If you swallow it, do so in little tiny amounts at a time. You'll know if you got too much: it burns your mouth like eating too many of those hot cinnamon candies and opens up your sinuses like a dose of fresh horseradish. Once you get used to it, you can use a few drops at a time. Oh, it makes you burp, too.

It's also good topically because it kills germs instantly.

Hope this helps,

Elin

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T00:41:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17570>

> **piano-doctor-lady;17627 wrote:** Very interesting, Chocolate -- thank you.
>
>
>
> Note that the study uses spearmint tea instead of peppermint. Might not have quite the same effect.
>
>
>
> One can grow spearmint too, of course.

[http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Spearmint/fulltext%285%29.pdf](http://http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Spearmint/fulltext%285%29.pdf)

Before planning this study, it was observed that

some male patients who live in Yeni8arbademli town

were admitted to hospitals with complaints of diminished

libido after daily consumption of four cups of

tea steeped with M. spicata or M. piperita. Therefore

this study investigated the effect of spearmint

tea on the androgen level in female patients with

hirsutism and a significant decrease in FT levels was found.

Maybe they have different effects but they inspired the test , and the peppermint and spearmint both seem to help the hirsutism and free testosterone. I'm loving that it slows iron absorption. I think its a go for gals.

### MJ* — 2012-04-22T17:04:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dietary-menthol-as-cold-thermogenesis-cold-adaptation-supplement.602/post-17732>

According to this [article,](http://http://www.nutrition-and-you.com/spearmint.html) peppermint has a much higher content of menthol than spearmint:

"The leaves and herb parts contain essential oil menthol. However, unlike in peppermint, spearmint does not contain high amounts of menthol (0.5% compared to the 40% in peppermint), making it least pungent and subtly fragrant herb in mint family. "

Winter: VLC, CT, animal fats, cold, dark

Summer: moderate primal carbs, heat, CO, sunny

Transition gradually in between. Be warm only when it's supposed to be warm, not warm-adapted 24/7/365.

# Thread: OMG! I just dunked my face a few times, it actually HURT, now I feel wonderful!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-i-just-dunked-my-face-a-few-times-it-actually-hurt-now-i-feel-wonderful.610/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Aili — 2012-04-02T21:44:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-i-just-dunked-my-face-a-few-times-it-actually-hurt-now-i-feel-wonderful.610/post-10444>

The long title says it all. In the middle of my workday (home office) I decided to try CT just on my face. I have been trying to learn about it because it seems like total torture and I am too much a newbie to get all the intricacies, BUT from online forums like MDA and all you folks, I've learned more about divergent thinking and wisdom, and health. I trust that if CT works for people and some talk about the exhilaration of it, it's worth a try. OMG!!!

So this morning I took a large cooking pot and put it under the tub tap and let the water run for a few minutes until it is ground cold (some where in the 40s F, I think). Oh yeah, we have a well in the country. Then I first washed my face with cold water, and then used the cooking pot. I held my breath and plunged my face into the water 4 times. It actually HURT! Then, I thought to not do it anymore, checked my face which was real red, went back to work and feel wide awake and great! My skin is smooth and shiny!

BUT I still cannot imagine how so many people in this forum can actually have a whole shower, baths, etc. with ICE!I grew up with the Finnish sauna (being Finnish-Canadian) and we used snow or cold water (e.g. hole in the ice on the lake) as part of the bath. I preferred myself to be outside in -40 degree weather for a few minutes rather than the shock of cold water or snow, so time will tell if I manage CT like all you folks.

I do recall the exhilaration of swimming in cold water though; after a few minutes the water did not feel as cold...but then there's the ice people ADD!

### Birdy — 2012-04-03T02:16:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-i-just-dunked-my-face-a-few-times-it-actually-hurt-now-i-feel-wonderful.610/post-10575>

ha ha, love your title. Same thing here. Isn't the face dunk wonderful? It's like a spa in a bowl.

### caroline — 2012-04-03T03:04:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-i-just-dunked-my-face-a-few-times-it-actually-hurt-now-i-feel-wonderful.610/post-10595>

> **Birdy;10644 wrote:** ha ha, love your title. Same thing here. Isn't the face dunk wonderful? It's like a spa in a bowl.

It does feel sooooo good. My skin is tighter and I think I have less wrinkles. That last bit might be wishful thinking!! lol

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-03T03:14:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-i-just-dunked-my-face-a-few-times-it-actually-hurt-now-i-feel-wonderful.610/post-10602>

### ASHO — 2012-04-03T19:56:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10847>

> **usmile777;10847 wrote:** I already found my answer, if anybody else is interested - 8 table spoons max.

Where did you find this information of 8 table spoons max?

Thank you.

### quelsen — 2012-04-03T20:33:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10870>

too much CO is when you cramp and pass out. ( as I have done)

Too much CO is when you begin to gain weight in a ketogenic situation all other macro-nutrients being strictly controlled.

In short there is no such value as too much unless you meet the first statement.

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-03T20:46:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10873>

> **usmile777;10523 wrote:** I am doing Leptin Reset RX since day 1 of P3, now is day 8.
>
> I am having 2 tablespoons of cocoa crack with every meal plus all the cooking with CO, total 6-8 table spoons per day. Is it too much? 30% of my daily calories come from CO. Just want to make sure I don't overdo it.
>
> Thank you!

I do not know what your total caloric count is but if you are keeping it low, you are getting way more then 30%

1 tablespoon is approx 126 calories x 8 is 1008 calories of CO ( 8 tablespoons is 112 grams of fat x 9 calories ). So unless you are eating 3000 plus calories a day, you are getting a much higher percentage of fat in your diet.

### usmile777 — 2012-04-03T21:01:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10878>

Quelsen - interesting theory about CO.

Continuum Fitness - what is the formula - Fat-Protein-Carbs? I know I have to keep under 50g of carbs per day. Is it pretty much 70% from protein and 30% from fat during Leptin Reset Rx? My calorie consumption is around 1700 a day.

Thank you for your help!

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-03T21:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-coconut-oil-is-considered-too-much.611/post-10884>

> **usmile777;10947 wrote:** Quelsen - interesting theory about CO.
>
> Continuum Fitness - what is the formula - Fat-Protein-Carbs? I know I have to keep under 50g of carbs per day. Is it pretty much 70% from protein and 30% from fat during Leptin Reset Rx? My calorie consumption is around 1700 a day.
>
> Thank you for your help!

I personally try to keep a 50% fat, 40% protein, 10% carb. Now I am not resetting but even when I did I kept in this range.

so based off your 1700 calories

850 calories of fat or 94 grams

680 calories of protein or 170 grams

170 calories of carbs or 42.5 grams

using your numbers of 70% protein 1700x 70% = 1190 calories or 297.5 grams of protein

1700 x 30% of fat is 510 calories or 56 grams of fat. So if you are eating 8 table spoons of CO, well see my numbers above. your calculations are way off. you are getting 60% of your fat from CO or 1008 calories/1700 plus the amount of fat mixed in with your animal protein.

### usmile777 — 2012-04-03T21:35:51+0300

I am figuring things out, still having hunger issues after being on 500 cal/day for 28 days, and it seems like fat is really helping me to keep my hunger under control.

Would you adjust the ration of 50% fat, 40% protein, 10% carb if your client was a woman? Appreciate your help.

I was wondering if anybody has similar problems like me introducing to their diet CO.

Iâ€™ve been doing LR for 2 weeks and CT. On the 4th day I started eating CO. and then I had a sharp pain in my tummy and later on I got bloated, I was disappointed as Dr Jack said itâ€™s good for us specially if one has Leaky Gut like me.

My health is not as bad as it used to be 5 years ago, slowly Iâ€™ve been healing my body first Parkinson, Rheumatoid arthritis, depression, lack of energy, food allergies (I still got some like not been able to eat onions, leaks, chillies and CO) I stop taking CO on Sunday as I get bloated every time I eat.

I take Armour, adrenal, cortisone and vitamin C.

Any clues as why is this happening?

### quelsen — 2012-04-02T22:19:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/c-o.612/post-10462>

Yes.

CO may cause cramping.

I dont know your situation but here is my recommendation / suggestion

1\. Find your bowel tolerance\* level for Vitamin C

2\. Start cleaning your gut with Magnesium Citrate at bowel tolerance levels do this for 8 weeks minimum. This will eliminate excess waste in the track.

3\. Take 1000 mg less than BT of Vitamin C daily.

4\. Take a strong Probiotic such as Standard process ProSynBiotic or Ultimate Flora Super Critical 200 Billion both available. Take from amazon i think. You want to take a very large dose with Fage or other greek ( clean ) yogurt ( unless dairy intolerant) for 4 weeks then drop down to half that for 4 weeks. ( large dose = 10 caps of ProSynbiotoc daily)

5\. Consume 1 can of high quality coconut milk daily for 2 weeks.

In two weeks you should be able to stomach 1 tablespoon of CO straight. slowly increase that to a max of 8

happy huunting

Bowel Tolerance means take enough that you need to have a bowel movement.

# Thread: Recipe for mashed eggs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipe-for-mashed-eggs.613/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rojo — 2012-04-02T23:44:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipe-for-mashed-eggs.613/post-10503>

Hi all,

I thought I would share an easy recipe I enjoy. It's from the Primal Kitchen: A Family Grokumentary blog:

Ingredients

\* Four eggs

\* 2 tablespoons butter, or to taste (I used salted Kerrygold)

\* Pepper (I used freshly ground)

Directions

Boil eggs in a rolling boil for 15 minutes. Remove with a slotted

spoon and plunge immediately into bowl of ice water (this turns your

yolks the pretty yellow instead of sulfuric grey). Shell the eggs

while they are still warm, place in a bowl, mash with butter, garnish

with pepper, and serve.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-03T00:13:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipe-for-mashed-eggs.613/post-10512>

### Kiera2 — 2012-04-03T00:43:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipe-for-mashed-eggs.613/post-10531>

# Thread: Help me out here - I am a miserable mess today.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### differentstory — 2012-04-03T00:07:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/post-10511>

I have been battling depression for a long time and have been on anti-depressants for 5+ years (Wellbutrin). On Wellbutrin, I have been AMAZING - but slowly it started to not be as effective at handling my blues and I could NOT eat healthy when on medication - I found myself eating a ridiculous amount of sweets throughout the day.

Fast forward 5 years, my menstrual cycle became very sporadic to the point that it stopped for over 6 months and my depression kicked in to high gear and couldn't be controlled by Wellbutrin anymore. I wound up in the dr's office and she handed me a new anti-depressant (Vibranyce I think??) and when I asked about it she said she "heard" it was "quite similar." Well I get home, start doing the research and realize it is SO NOT SIMILAR! I was pretty upset that my doctor would just hand me something like that without even really knowing anything about it - especially after everything I read about the side effects (from others that had just started taking it)!

Needless to say, at that moment, while researching the new medication, I decided that I really needed to take my life back. So now I am here, 2 months later.

I started primal eating almost 30 days ago. I CT - I'm having good progress with it. Have had lots of labs - still working to understand the results (I paid for them myself). I am supplementing. Increasing the vegetables (my goal is to get to 9 cups a day - currently I am at 5 cups a day).

I stopped my anti-depressants with good success in the beginning. I weigh 196. I've lost 6.6 pounds! My menstrual cycle came back this weekend (YAY ME!) and I have a feeling that it will be a regular cycle from now on as long as I continue eating primal (based on my history). My blood pressure is down. My temp is getting definitely moving around acting like it wants to stabilize.

But things are still rough.  I am 34 (although I feel like I am 75). I am really lacking in the ENERGY department. My mood and anxiety are a million times better - where I was probably a 2 out of 10, I am now a 5.5 out of 10. But I don't have any motivation to work. All I want to do is sit and read or sit and play on the computer. I can barely do laundry or clean the kitchen. I am FAKING it every day at work and at home.  I am sleeping 10-11 hours a day too.

Today is a REALLY bad day for me as I just feel sad, down, wanting to cry. . .I am on my period - I know that is part of it. But, I've gone through this for FOUR WEEKS thinking it is going to get better but it is not getting better.

2 months ago when I started this journey, I had finally had it and started googling like crazy. I have symptoms of hyperthyroidism and marched into my dr. wanting to be tested - I just KNEW that was my problem. All of the tests came back "fine" though (she did do the T3 and T4). I don't have the numbers with me at the moment.

I think my problems are all hormone related (although isn't everything?). But I am not sure where to turn to next.

Also, just wanted to say that I did have my gallbladder removed when I was 17. I'm not sure if that has anything to do with my lack of energy.

Please please please help me figure out what to do next.

### sandy — 2012-04-03T00:14:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/post-10514>

HI

I just wanted to say, I know how you feel. I have been suffering from depression to varying degrees for many years. It got so bad a couple of years ago that I went to the doctor. He gave me lexapro which made me sleep for 12 hours! I got off of that and now take magnesium malate instead. It really helps me from feeling like I am going crazy.

I still have the same things as you as far as motivation- don't feel like doing anything, don't feel like cleaning, I would stay in bed most of the day if I could get away with it.

I don't have any answers for you. I am hoping the leptin reset and CT will help me. I also plan on going to a doctor who is willing to work on my hormone levels and andrenals and things like that.

I hope we both find the answer! hugs

### NotAnother30 — 2012-04-03T00:17:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/post-10515>

I understand how you are feeling. I too was on a similar journey but was able to correct things one at a time. The final one with me is weight. I was just wondering if you consumed soy by any chance?

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-03T00:51:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/post-10535>

> **differentstory;10580 wrote:** I have been battling depression for a long time and have been on anti-depressants for 5+ years (Wellbutrin). On Wellbutrin, I have been AMAZING - but slowly it started to not be as effective at handling my blues and I could NOT eat healthy when on medication - I found myself eating a ridiculous amount of sweets throughout the day.
>

>
> Fast forward 5 years, my menstrual cycle became very sporadic to the point that it stopped for over 6 months and my depression kicked in to high gear and couldn't be controlled by Wellbutrin anymore. I wound up in the dr's office and she handed me a new anti-depressant (Vibranyce I think??) and when I asked about it she said she "heard" it was "quite similar." Well I get home, start doing the research and realize it is SO NOT SIMILAR! I was pretty upset that my doctor would just hand me something like that without even really knowing anything about it - especially after everything I read about the side effects (from others that had just started taking it)!

My menstrual cycle came back this weekend (YAY ME!) and I have a feeling that it will be a regular cycle from now on as long as I continue eating primal (based on my history). My blood pressure is down. My temp is getting definitely moving around acting like it wants to stabilize.

My mood and anxiety are a million times better - where I was probably a 2 out of 10, I am now a 5.5 out of 10. But I don't have any motivation to work. All I want to do is sit and read or sit and play on the computer. I can barely do laundry or clean the kitchen. I am FAKING it every day at work and at home.  I am sleeping 10-11 hours a day too.
>
>
>
> Today is a REALLY bad day for me as I just feel sad, down, wanting to cry. . .I am on my period - I know that is part of it. But, I've gone through this for FOUR WEEKS thinking it is going to get better but it is not getting better.
>
>
>
> 2 months ago when I started this journey, I had finally had it and started googling like crazy. I have symptoms of hyperthyroidism and marched into my dr. wanting to be tested - I just KNEW that was my problem. All of the tests came back "fine" though (she did do the T3 and T4). I don't have the numbers with me at the moment.
>
>
>
> I think my problems are all hormone related (although isn't everything?). But I am not sure where to turn to next.
>
>

> Also, just wanted to say that I did have my gallbladder removed when I was 17. I'm not sure if that has anything to do with my lack of energy.
>
>
>

here's my N=1 [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?95-N-1-My-journey-on-the-Leptin-Reset](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?95-N-1-My-journey-on-the-Leptin-Reset)

I've got PCOS, bipolar, fibromaylgia, chronic fatigue. at some point we've all been where you are... it took me years of struggling to discover MDA, and Dr K... I wouldn't be where I am today.

take one day at a time... and remember your thoughts can change your DNA. Think about what you want to be tomorrow, or the next hour and it will happen.

>

Thank you so much for your reply. It makes me angry sometimes that I am a woman.  My husband is overweight (although getting better) and it totally emotionally stable!!! ARGH.

I did start taking 5-HPT about 6 weeks ago and that has helped some. Enough where I quit taking my AD. But then I thought it was not doing much (the 5-HPT) so 3 weeks later I stopped taking it, and then things whent REALLY downhill. So I added it back and I'm definitely better. Still not near 100% though. I would say I am more like 50%.

I feel like I'm going to get fired from my job if I don't do something.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-03T00:58:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-out-here-i-am-a-miserable-mess-today.614/post-10541>

One thing I know about depression is that it is an inflammatory condition. Years ago I went to a talk by a biochemist who began by asking, "what do prozac and St John's Wort have in common?" They are both anti-inflammatories. If you are doing CT and making changes, you are likely in the process of major detox. I know I am - it began from the start, but after about a month of CT it is really ramping up. It is both emotional and physical detoxing. I would up your anti-inflammatory protocol (I take at least a teaspoon of tumeric every day, eat 100% cocoa for the resveratrol, for example), eat lots of fish, take a krill oil supplement (I believe Dr K recommends 500g 3x per day), get some B6/B12 on board to clear toxins and maybe some DIM too.

Also, realise that you are healing/reversing a long-standing pattern of disease. This means (at least for my n of 1) that the overall pattern will move toward health while the day-to-day can be VERY up and down. For example, during a big detox day I'll feel like shit and have very low energy. I remember Dr K commenting before that he took lots of naps early on. Nurture yourself. You may have to 'fake it' at work with colleagues but you don't have to fake it with yourself.

Also, make sure you are eating enough fat. Lots of CO and ghee and animal fat. Fat seems to be the most nourishing thing for our adrenals (stress) and helps a lot with mood and hunger too.

Hang in there. (You also have permission to throw away your scale. I did - great move!)

Are you supplementing with vitamin D3 at all? I won't bore you with my whole life story, but suffice it to say that I'm about your age, and I spent five years on and off Wellbutrin, Effexor, Lexapro, plus some Valium thrown in for good measure when I was having panic attacks. I finally got weaned off of everything, including the Wellbutrin, and started taking a high dose of Omega-3's along with vitamin D3 (I take 8000mg a day), and within about 6 weeks I started to feel like a whole new person. It hasn't fixed every mood swing or PMS meltdown, by any means...but it's made a HUGE difference.

Good luck!

I don't have the numbers with me at the moment.**

**

Hi differentstory,

My heart goes out to you. I can feel your suffering. I tend to by a very happy person but after the birth of my second child I learned about postpartum depression. I found a few things helped me:

1\. Everyday, I would go for very long, slow walks with my daughter in a backpack.

2\. I found a support group and talked about my struggles.

3\. I read a little red book called Awakening Lovingkindness** by Pera Chodron. Every time I started to feel down I would read some of it:

[http://www.amazon.com/Awakening-Loving-Kindness-Shambhala-Pocket-Classics/dp/1570622590/ref=sr\_1\_15?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1333644015&sr=1-15](http://http://www.amazon.com/Awakening-Loving-Kindness-Shambhala-Pocket-Classics/dp/1570622590/ref=sr_1_15?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1333644015&sr=1-15)

4\. On my long walks I would wish "lovingkindness" to all the people I passed on my walk. I found this practice changed my world.

If you are already eating a strict paleo diet I would suggest you try to watch how much sweet foods you are eating. This may be the root to your problem. Eat a lot more saturated fats. You may be feeding "bad" gut bacteria that is producing endotoxins that are being reabsorbed by your bowel and adding to your neurological problems:

[http://www.eatkamloops.org/what-is-a-healthy-gut/](http://http://www.eatkamloops.org/what-is-a-healthy-gut/)

My husband used GABA when he came off his anti-depression medication a number of years ago. Now when my husband had a bout of blues/anxiety, he takes large doses of fermented cod liver oil and his mood improves within hours. You could look at the book **The Mood Cure** by Julia Ross. That is where I learned about GABA which helped get my husband off depression medication:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-Mood-Cure-Program-Emotions--Today/dp/0142003646/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1333644677&sr=1-1](http://http://www.amazon.com/The-Mood-Cure-Program-Emotions--Today/dp/0142003646/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1333644677&sr=1-1)

I hope that helps some. Do find someone to talk with. Find a support group if you are concerned about wearying family members.

# Thread: Make ahead dinner ideas!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-04-03T00:30:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-10524>

Those of you with young children know that sometimes the days just get crazy, and at least for me sometimes I end up having to scramble around to figure out dinner. I used to do make ahead freezer dinners, but I've found that hard to do having my whole family on a low carb diet! Gone are the pasta dishes and casseroles. So... any ideas for make ahead dinners? Something easy to pull out of a freezer when you don't have time to mess with anything? Or maybe your fave crock pot dishes that you can throw in first thing in the morning and not think about it till dinner? Let's share recipes!

### Casey — 2012-04-03T02:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-10579>

Great thread, I need ideas too! Quiche/egg pies etc can be frozen and put straight in the oven when needed. Doesn't help me though because we can't eat eggs! Soup is pretty much the only meal I freeze ahead of time. I make bolar roast in my slow cooker/crockpot, no recipe but usually put in broth, splash of wine and herbs.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-03T21:54:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-10898>

I think this looks like a great recipe! Make a huge pot and freeze some for another meal!

[http://www.mommypotamus.com/ultimate-beef-liver-chili/](http://http://www.mommypotamus.com/ultimate-beef-liver-chili/)

### PaleoSue — 2012-04-04T00:27:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-10995>

Sooo much can be done with a CrockPot. You can generally prepare the pot the night before (but be careful about mixing veggies with raw meat ahead of time), and then just turn it on in the morning.

Or you could cook just meat in a broth in the CrockPot, and prepare veggies at night.

### thyme — 2012-04-04T14:14:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-11212>

I double or triple every recipe and freeze it. I've got 4-person servings of cooked beef rendang, oxtail stew, halibut curry, and beef heart kebabs in the freezer now...no microwave, but no young children, either, so I can usually remember to stick the frozen dinners in the fridge the night before and then stick them in the oven come night to eat.

### vlynnb — 2012-04-05T05:00:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-11598>

thyme, Are those your own recipes or out of Dr. K's book? I haven't purchased the book yet as I am waiting for a new computer and this one is tempermental if I add anything new. But, if they are your recipes can you share?

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-04-05T06:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-11633>

Here's some make ahead items that I do for the kids (and for hubby's lunches)

1\. quiche with bacon crust - I line mini quiche pan with either bacon or proscuitto and then add the quiche mix and bake. These can be frozen until you want to reheat

2\. Some cooked ribs (I pressure cook) and then just season and throw in the oven to reheat and broil to crisp up. I've had luck sticking the frozen (but pre-pressure cooked) in the oven. We love all types of "meat on a stick" around here... well I take that back ... no corndogs.

3\. One of my favorite foods is rouladen... it's a german food my grandma taught me. It's a slice of beef with bacon, onions, black pepper, grainy mustard on top and then rolled up and secured with tooth picks. I brown it in rendered pork fat and then pressure cook. Some people put pickle inside but I prefer to have a home fermented pickle on the side so as to not kill all the cultures. Kids think it's cool because its a roll. They're good reheated or the rolls can be made ahead of time.

4\. Roast beef slices..... the kids love rolling stuff in them and dipping them, etc.

5\. homemade sausage is a big hit around here - easy to have cooked and then reheat. ditto with meatballs. And these are great ways to hide the liver and such

6\. daughter goes wild over pulled pork.... i buy smoked pork picnic shoulder and throw it in the crockpot until it falls apart. it's great reheated too.

### Mud Flinger — 2012-04-11T05:59:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-13636>

I've made some fun meat balls with hard boiled eggs in the middle. Basically mix any ground meat with spices, etc that you like, make a patty and wrap it around a peeled hard boiled egg and bake. I called them dinosaur eggs - my 4 yr old son called them dragon eggs.

### thyme — 2012-04-11T19:08:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-13796>

vlynnb, they're my recipes and I'm happy to share--let me type them up over the weekend.

### thyme — 2012-04-11T19:22:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-13806>

[Beef heart kebabs, or Anticuchos](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?852-Anticuchos-peruvian-beef-heart-kebabs-%28-meat-on-a-stick-for-kids%29-has-nightshades&p=13873#post13873). I usually just cook the whole heart and freeze the already-cooked second dinner worth and the sauce.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-11T21:19:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-13854>

would love your recipes too!

### gemstar — 2012-04-12T06:41:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-14100>

You can freeze just about anything, especially proteins and reheat. I often reheat on the stove burner, adding a drop of boiling water, keeping the gas flame very low. I usually try to make leftovers to freeze, so that I always have something ready to eat. I do not use the microwave. I also always freeze in portions. BTW, BBQ'd meat is also great frozen and reheated. People do not know the difference from fresh grilled meats!!!

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-12T17:05:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/make-ahead-dinner-ideas.615/post-14223>

the crock pot saves us on the nights I have class, or we'll make a big roast - and eat it on those nights. Easter Sunday dinner of a 6lb leg of lamb - covered dinner for 3 nights this week... and there may be enough for my HAB tomorrow morning...

# Thread: Optimal Fitness for Children
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-03T00:48:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-10533>

For parents implementing these ideas with children (and other fitness gurus out there)... do you think the guidelines are the same for children, regardless of age? I've read some of Mark Sisson's writing on weight lifting for kids and most everything I read says the HIIT and weight lifting protocols are good for children... and of course a core of natural movement and play. However, I'm interested in the other side of the coin: are the CW ideas about fitness just as bad for children as they are for us? Do children experience the same rise in cortisol with sustained cardio? I want to challenge my children's school fitness regimen (currently training all the 11-13 year olds to run long-distance races in a few months by doing daily runs that get progressively longer in length and time), but I'd like some specific info to do this. Anyone?

### Dead River Sailor — 2012-04-03T03:07:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-10599>

> **KiwiLauren;10602 wrote:** For parents implementing these ideas with children (and other fitness gurus out there)... do you think the guidelines are the same for children, regardless of age? I've read some of Mark Sisson's writing on weight lifting for kids and most everything I read says the HIIT and weight lifting protocols are good for children... and of course a core of natural movement and play. However, I'm interested in the other side of the coin: are the CW ideas about fitness just as bad for children as they are for us? Do children experience the same rise in cortisol with sustained cardio? I want to challenge my children's school fitness regimen (currently training all the 11-13 year olds to run long-distance races in a few months by doing daily runs that get progressively longer in length and time), but I'd like some specific info to do this. Anyone?

I am unaware of any studies regarding cortisol, aerobics and children. However, I am acutely aware of the huge issue of orthopedic trauma for people doing long aerobic training. Young people (teenagers) certainly have major orthopedic consequences when they do "long aerobics" (read running). If the trouble does not show up immediately, it often shows up later in obvious ways such as damaged feet.

I was training for long distance running at 12 years old, I had no obvious trauma but somehow I managed to have severely shortened legs (stuck at the same length as when I was 12 BTW) for my body proportions......could have been something else...but it is really way out of range. Growth plate damage in children is not reported with strength training according to Wayne Wescott.

Orthopedic trauma alone makes long aerobics a major caution.

Best DRS

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-03T03:10:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-10600>

swimming in cool water.....

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-05T15:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-11694>

> **Jack Kruse;10669 wrote:** swimming in cool water.....

The kids have been in swimming lessons since they were 6mths! can't keep 'em out of the water...would love it if they followed mommy into the competitive pool.... but we'll see!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-05T15:54:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-11706>

I wouldn't tolerate forcing my children to do long-distance running. Period.

Move slowly a lot. Lift heavy things. Sprint once in a while.

Get them to have the children play a lot of basketball, soccer, volleyball, softball, etc. Sprinting, slow movement, and explosive bursts are part of those sports. If they whine that not everyone has ball co-ordination, true. Balance it with short-distance hurdle races, 100m dashes, rope climbing, rock climbing (cheap to install on one gym wall), etc.

At home, we hike a LOT. Canoe, bike, etc., but especially hiking. On the hikes, we jump boulders, swing from trees, dash after squirrels, jump in the river, whatever.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-05T16:10:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-11710>

one thing I've been encouraging the girl to do is walk on her hands & feet... helps her core and builds her coordination.. Also encouraging her to practice her balancing and climbing skills!

### chocolate — 2012-04-05T18:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-11755>

There is a race horse training method based on an Army study of men that played high school basketball. Its the best. Its about straining the bone and calcification overlays. You stress the bone, let it rest and heal. Then again. I forgot the name of the method. Prevents injuries. The high school basket ball players had fewer breaks and denser bones. You can add a year of age to the bone with the method. But people aren't like animals (allegedly).

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T20:39:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-11819>

I'd say no to the daily runs. I can't imagine my daugher being forced to run, she HATES it. What is their "point" with the races? Community? Team spirit? Accomplishment? Aren't there other ways to achieve that? I bet the program designer is a runner LOL

The DD, 15 yo, loves to dance, has been doing so since 2. Rides her bike or walks just about everywhere. Loves to flip tires and lift weights, but only sporadically. She take coaching better from her stepdad than from me, so he trains her with the barbell. he trained her for a powerlifting meet in Nov, we did it together, it was great fun.

Her JV tennis "practice" consisted of sitting on the bleachers and watching the varsity tennis girls games. 4 days of 5. The 5th day they went out on the courts. She want to make varsity this summer, so she is starting today to work with DH in the gym. Knows she needs to be stronger and faster.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-10T06:59:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-13265>

Thanks everyone. I totally agree. And I'm appalled at their 'mandatory' stance on the running. What is their point? Not sure - fitness achievement I'd think. The thing is, my kids are very active. They play sports, they walk (or scooter) to school, they cycle in the neighborhood. And, as we move into autumn, we've been doing family beach swims (water temp is 60 degrees at the moment) in the ocean! The kids love it.

I just wish I had a way to convince the schools that the long-distance running is a no-go. (Clearly, I can set a limit for my children and they must oblige. I was just hoping for something more global.)

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-06-28T16:34:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-fitness-for-children.616/post-34154>

See Barry and his opinion on Fasted State Training. If one is a fat burner, it seems to be much much better and way less (in fact improves) endurance training. So probably the same for kids. So if the school insists on long distance training, fat adapt your kids. Also, it's OK to give them BP coffee in the morning. There's no bad effects of coffee/caffeine on kids. (Obviously get good coffee b/c the same problems remain the same for kids as with adults). Maybe for kids...you can give slightly less of the coffee, and add a bit more butter or perhaps some raw-grass fed cream (if tolerated). Also for kids, you can put some raw sugar in their drink too.

Let me know how this goes. I know with other kids around with junk food pizza etc., fat adapting might be hard for kids...sigh.

# Thread: Labs Please Help ... Hack Away!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-03T00:52:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-please-help-hack-away.617/post-10536>

Just got the BFs labs back and need a little help. Can anyone confirm if this is too much testosterone? All the others sex steroid hormones look low ..... so where is that high testosterone coming from?

Anyone can hack away on anything on these labs. All comments and contributions are welcome. Quelson, JanSz I need help as I don't understand the "man thing" as well as you guys do.

46 yr old male

No meds

6'1"

160#

SAD diet most of his life. Completely organic for last 10 yrs. Grass-fed meat, gluten free for a year. Grain free since Jan 1. Leptin Rx for 2 months due to high rT3 (low ratio).

Cholesterol 260 (100-199)

Triglycerides 41 (0-149)

HDL 90 (>39)

VLDL Calc 8 (5-40)

LDL Calc 162 (0-99)

These ranges are from LabCorp

Omega 06:03 ratio is 2.1:1

Pregnenolone 113 (180-200)

DHEAs 230 (500-640)

Progesterone apparently wasn't measured

Testosterone 848 (650-827)

Testosterone, Free 14 (6.8-21.5)

Estradiol, sensitive 15 (20) \*really bad

Prior to these numbers his thyroid borders on hyper. Graves disease runs in his family. Son has Graves.

CRPhs .33 (.0-3.0)

IGF-1 157 (94-252)

PSA .9 (3-70)

Iron, serum 106 (40-155)

Iron Saturation 46% (15-55)

TBIC 229 (250-450)

Ferritin 283 (30-400)

HA1c 5.4 (4.8-5.6)

Fasting Insulin 6.2 (2.6-24.9)

2 Hour postprandial Insulin 5.8 (2.6-24.9)

LH 7.2 (1.7-8.6)

FSH 5.8 (1.5-12.4)

GGT 14 (0-65)

Thank you!!!

### Enkidu — 2012-04-03T02:59:35+0300

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-03T17:43:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-please-help-hack-away.617/post-10788>

### mambo — 2012-04-29T23:14:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-please-help-hack-away.617/post-19936>

# Thread: Doctor, Diabetes, Leptin Rx - Oh my!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-diabetes-leptin-rx-oh-my.618/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 19

### CrystalM — 2012-04-03T01:05:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-diabetes-leptin-rx-oh-my.618/post-10544>

Hi All - I'm looking for some advice (or maybe a place to vent...lol). I just got back from my doctor and am very frustrated. I can't seem to get her to understand that I'm looking to get RID of my diabetes and not just treat it. Here's a little back story:

In Sept. 2011 I was told by another doctor that I was diabetic. My sugar was over 275! ... We immediately went into triage mode, as I wanted to do everything I could to get better and NOT be put on meds. He introduced me to the Paleo Diet and Dr. Kruse's Leptin RX blog. I couldn't be more grateful to him for that! It changed my life. However, due to all the changes in healthcare my then doctor changed his practice to a membership, which unfortunately I couldn't afford. So starting in November I had to see a nurse practitioner in his office. Ever since that 1st visit with her, she's been trying to put me on Metformin. She doesn't understand that since September I had been working extremely hard to lower my weight and blood sugar. I've been successful too! She did a blood test in December and all my numbers were better than from September. She even said I was not Diabetic, but insulin resistant.

Now forward 3-4 months later and she's trying to put me on Metformin AGAIN and my blood sugar levels are great! ... Am I missing something? Or should I just switch doctors?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-03T01:24:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-diabetes-leptin-rx-oh-my.618/post-10551>

metformin wont hurt you......it activates the pathway........Read CT 8. Its in there. You wont need to be on it long. But Ct is what you need. Get Some.

### Birdy — 2012-04-03T01:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-diabetes-leptin-rx-oh-my.618/post-10559>

doesn't metformin wreck your stomach if you use it a long time?

### CrystalM — 2012-04-03T01:34:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-diabetes-leptin-rx-oh-my.618/post-10560>

Dr. Kruse - I had a VAP done today and go back in 2 weeks. I told her I'd give Metformin serious thought between now and then. I've been doing some spot CT, and will soon try full immersion. Honestly, I haven't lost "weight" but I fit into pants I haven't worn in 5 years!  I know you hear "thank you" all the time, but I sincerely mean it! You've helped me change my life!

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T01:22:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-diabetes-leptin-rx-oh-my.618/post-11527>

> **Jack Kruse;10620 wrote:** metformin wont hurt you......it activates the pathway........Read CT 8. Its in there. You wont need to be on it long. But Ct is what you need. Get Some.

I'm T2D, on 2000mg Metformin.

Was hoping it would make me lose weight, as some claim it does for them, but mine didn't budge.

Then, one month on Leptin Rx, into my third week of baths, I'm down about 15 pounds, blood sugar down further. I've had 20-30 point drops from 40 min baths. I'm sure my doc would chalk it all up to the Metformin, but I don't buy it.

Wondering how long to go before reducing the Metformin? At goal weight? "Normal" blood sugar readings?

Any suggestions?

Dave

### debbiemikus — 2012-04-05T03:10:10+0300

Ok, this makes me curious. I was on Metformin before I was diagnosed with Crohns and I took myself off it and started on Glipizidexr, of course with my doc at the times approval. I felt that the metformin was too hard on my gut, but maybe that wasn't it at all. Dr Kruse, do you think I should give it another try?

### Momma28 — 2012-04-09T23:44:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-diabetes-leptin-rx-oh-my.618/post-13138>

> **Jack Kruse;10620 wrote:** metformin wont hurt you......it activates the pathway........Read CT 8. Its in there. You wont need to be on it long. But Ct is what you need. Get Some.

Hi Dr. Kruse,

I, too, have been experimeting on my own health and that of my family's, which includes Hubby and eight children. Hubby and I have been working at lowering our fasted blood glucose levels through various means. We both started with levels above 85...mine running 95 to 100 and his 115 to 120. Our goal is to bring those levels down to the 70-85 range with the understanding that it is the elevated glycation that ages the body. And we are both noticing the macular degeneration going on. We are both at pretty much optimal weight and both 48 years old. Hubby is an Air Traffic Controller and I, of course, care for and educate kids all day, so we both have what I would consider, average amounts of stress. He exercises 4 to 5 days a week and I when I can, but the laundry is a workout all by itself.

I have studied health and nutrition for 20 years now, so our diet has been 80% organic, "Nourishing Traditions" style, less the grains, for several years. Even with weeks on LeptinRx I have not consistently had a normal blood sugar in the morning, so I reasoned that the liver must still be in overdrive mode. Understanding that all secretory organs need Iodine for optimal functioning, we started on large doses of Lugols Solution, 50mg daily, to see what affect this would have. Our numbers came down, but not consistently. There are still other factors affecting this we have yet to pinpoint. Are we eating too late, say after 5 pm? Are we not hydrated enough from the day before? I'm looking at all of these as possible causes. It has to be age related, however, because despite any of these circumstances the children's levels are consistently below 85. The question is....can we return our liver to functioning as we did while younger and is Metformin the answer to the restoration?

I am now about to start modulating my body's ph to see if this affects anything. Since optimal ph is 7.35 to 7.45, which corresponds to a voltage of -20 to -25 millivolts (per the work of Dr. Jerry Tennant), with an increase to -50 millivolts for any healing to take place, I want to see if monitoring my ph to bring it within this range will work. I plan to use Calcium Sulfate Solution in intervals bringing my a.m. urine ph to a range of 6.4 to 6.6, which corresponds to a blood and tissue ph of the 7.35 to 7.45. I will let you know how this method works. I just can't take the thought of CT right now. My body temp has always run 96.something so I hate the cold. However, the Lugols has it up to 97.7 to 97.9 most mornings now.

One other question...is the glycation process directly tied to hormone levels? I have just recently started on bio-identicals, so I am anxiously waiting to see if this changes the numbers. It was just a few years ago that my blood sugars were consistently in the 70-85 range no matter what I ate or did, so there must be a reason for the change and, hence, a way to change it back.

Thanks, Christina

### nonchalant — 2012-04-09T23:55:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-diabetes-leptin-rx-oh-my.618/post-13142>

> **Momma28;13207 wrote:** I just can't take the thought of CT right now. My body temp has always run 96.something so I hate the cold.

Hi Christina, I think your hating the cold is a big clue.

If you're interested, you could try washing your face with a cool damp cloth, see if you can take that.

I used to be afraid of being cold, much like I used to be afraid of getting hungry/hypoglycemic/shakey, but no longer. I am confident in the ability of my body to adjust to cold, and to adjust to hunger seamlessly.

### chocolate — 2012-04-15T17:23:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctor-diabetes-leptin-rx-oh-my.618/post-15299>

[http://lowcarbdiets.about.com/od/prediabetesanddiabetes/a/insulinresistan.htm](http://http://lowcarbdiets.about.com/od/prediabetesanddiabetes/a/insulinresistan.htm)

I was looking up a study on fermentation last night and tripped over a study that now (hmm) has a broken link. All of the fermented foods have been studied for preventing insulin resistance or increasing the sensitivity and they all seem to do it. One study in particular, isolated the lactic whatever bacteria and it was the bacteria itself that prevented the resistance. I can't find it now. Anyway, I'm thinking a water fast is not the water to go, a fermented veggie juice fast would be. Then I found a really good study on vinegar. That prevents insulin resistance also.
