# Thread: I have a friend that had breast cancer, was on tamoxifin,
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-a-friend-that-had-breast-cancer-was-on-tamoxifin.649/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-04T17:01:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-a-friend-that-had-breast-cancer-was-on-tamoxifin.649/post-11265>

and was told it wasn't stopping the hormones. Now she needs a hysterectomy with no hormone replacement. I think she is misunderstanding or didn't have something explained to her. She has chronicled her entire journey on facebook. The tamoxifen made her gain tons and she wasn't small in the first place. I am so concerned that she hasn't been told that the liver is what controls SGHB, and that she has estrogen in all of that fat and will make it in her intestines and that cancer needs fructose for cell division. I sent her a note about the liver and SBHG, and that we were trying to prevent a repetition of under thirty breast cancer death in our gene pool and wished her the best. I feel like I'm watching a train wreck of uninformation. Its the worst.

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2007/11/071109171610.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2007/11/071109171610.htm)

I was looking at that blog with comments that was saying how chaotic this blog is, and honestly while it is chaotic, everything overlaps or goes with everything....the body is not such a straight line with all the mismatches. It seems like this article should have been shouted from the rooftop and passed out with pink ribbons.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/pmc2756324/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/pmc2756324/)

Metformin selectively targets breast cancer stem cells

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CDUQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.freerepublic.com%2Ffocus%2Ff-news%2F2354967%2Fposts&ei=OpaBT839GeGQiQKcpsG1Aw&usg=AFQjCNGMssvf1l88KJO1SfExXYPyp1S8UQ&sig2=ZDoyO4GXjFLhCXUVG9xaOg](http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CDUQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.freerepublic.com%2Ffocus%2Ff-news%2F2354967%2Fposts&ei=OpaBT839GeGQiQKcpsG1Aw&usg=AFQjCNGMssvf1l88KJO1SfExXYPyp1S8UQ&sig2=ZDoyO4GXjFLhCXUVG9xaOg) There is article mentioning that diabetic women on metformin get less breast cancer than those not. I would think that would carry over to pcos or any others with metabolic problems. I hope the off-label use isn't "off-label". If it prevents the manufacture of glucose and that is what feed the stems cells...help me out here....and fructose is what makes the cancer cells divide..... and grain/sugar/fructose/dairy is what stops shbg from being produced.....

I guess we need to figure out when the body makes the stuff on its own, and what the specific targets would be. We can stop eating the stuff. If any you are Dr Pepper/Snapple drinkers west of the Mississippi.... if it matter where they get their water, ask me. (Waste water plant adjacent is the hint.)

# Thread: HCG diet and trying to get pregnant?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### justducky — 2012-04-04T17:20:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-11274>

Hello all. I am new here. I have been reading (a little) about the HCG diet/protocol.

During my last pregnancy, I lost lots of weight. It was almost magical. I now wonder whether this was caused by the higher levels of HCG during the pregnancy.

To anyone's knowledge, does this diet affect one's ability to become pregnant?

Thank you,

Ducky

### colleencoble — 2012-04-04T17:41:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-11289>

Over the years we have seen women who had infertility issues become pregnant while on hcg or shortly after.

### justducky — 2012-04-04T18:43:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-11334>

Thank you. That almost seems counter-intuitive. I would think that elevated hcg levels would signal to the body that a pregnancy was already underway. Any idea how or why it works?

### colleencoble — 2012-04-04T19:04:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-11341>

I personally think some underlying hormonal issues are fixed and it allows the woman to finally get pregnant. We know for sure thyroid levels are elevated and normalized. And I always have to cut back on my progesterone and estrogen while on the protocol.

### Claudia — 2012-04-19T05:26:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-16481>

> **colleencoble;11410 wrote:** I personally think some underlying hormonal issues are fixed and it allows the woman to finally get pregnant. We know for sure thyroid levels are elevated and normalized. And I always have to cut back on my progesterone and estrogen while on the protocol.

Do you then add the progesterone and estrogen when you finish protocol and are in maintenance? Do you test first to see where you are? I need to test sex hormones but don't want to do it right after a round..not sure when it would be a good time after doing a round to test. I have been estrogen dominant in the past and used progesterone cream so was thinking I might do this again. I think hcg affected me and maybe made me more estrogen dominant than before hcg. Need to test though.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-19T16:50:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-16584>

No, I add them back in. I have no ovaries so I will always need to have them. And yes, it can make you more estrogen dominant for a short time, as can any diet when fat is released. Your fat cells store estrogen.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-19T17:33:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-16595>

August, that's what hcg DOES though. It protects the baby when the mom is too nauseated to eat by releasing the calories in that stored fat. So if you conceive on hcg, even MORE hcg will be coursing through your body and the baby is fine.  Now I wouldn't continue on the diet once you knew you were pregnant, but you're not harming the little one.

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-04-21T19:20:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-17470>

> **colleencoble;16654 wrote:** No, I add them back in. I have no ovaries so I will always need to have them. And yes, it can make you more estrogen dominant for a short time, as can any diet when fat is released. Your fat cells store estrogen.

interesting colleen! maybe that is why i'm experiencing a bit more bloating water retention since starting hcg? i'm peeing all the time -also on progesterone so that may be way - wondering if this might just be a transitory sympton as things balance out.

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-04-21T19:21:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-17471>

> **August;16660 wrote:** I have the impression that the HCG Protocol might increase the odds of conceiving . . I have nagging thoughts in the back of my mind though that if you are on a a VLCD or perhaps even soon after, it might not be the optimal time to conceive (epigenetics-wise - like for the baby) . . . I'm a worrier . .

VLCD are not bad for babies in fact you could argue that the avoiding grains sugars starches are more beneficial bc you are avoiding an insulin spiked roller coaster, inflammation of the gut (which releases allergens in the bloodstream) etc. i would not shy away from VLCD and pregnancy

edited to say: BUT i'm nowhere near ready for a baby yet! so my opinion is pretty much worthless in this arena, hehe

### colleencoble — 2012-04-21T19:25:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-17476>

Some of the weight lost the first week is water weight for most, but those are usually people who used to eat a lot of carbs. Progesterone can help you dump excess water though. But the bloating isn't usual. What have you been eating?

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-04-21T23:02:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-17538>

pretty much grilled chicken and fish with veggies to equal 500 calories (mostly spinach, but had some fermented saurekraut mixed in- only once though )...seasoned with S&P very simple...I remember you said simple is best. BUT i have heard some people react to progesterone differently....however I'm on a super small dose. my body tends to be sensitive to things though. i hope its transitory. i did lower the dose to .2 bc the .4 was making me super sleepy. what is the reasoning behind the morning dose suggestion of progesterone? would it still work for me if i took it at night (Thinking the sleepiness then would be a bonus!)

i updated dzugan so hoping they get back to me next week with some suggestions

### colleencoble — 2012-04-22T00:22:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-17560>

You're actually best to take progesterone 2x a day. The dose is pretty much gone after 12 hours.

### nuttmegs17 — 2012-04-22T18:34:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-diet-and-trying-to-get-pregnant.650/post-17772>

> **colleencoble;17631 wrote:** You're actually best to take progesterone 2x a day. The dose is pretty much gone after 12 hours.

hmmm interesting...perhaps i should do .2 in the am and .2 in the pm to equal the .4 recommendation..i'll be curious as to what they suggest. i think i'd rather have the topical gel so am anxious for it to get to me. next week...

### colleencoble — 2012-04-22T19:38:18+0300

# Thread: Daily torpor is associated with telomere length change over winter in Djungarian hams
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daily-torpor-is-associated-with-telomere-length-change-over-winter-in-djungarian-hams.651/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hellojtm — 2012-04-04T18:29:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daily-torpor-is-associated-with-telomere-length-change-over-winter-in-djungarian-hams.651/post-11324>

[http://rsbl.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/8/2/304.full](http://http://rsbl.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/8/2/304.full)

### Dan in Utah — 2012-04-04T19:17:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daily-torpor-is-associated-with-telomere-length-change-over-winter-in-djungarian-hams.651/post-11354>

So, is daily torpor in the cold the new WOD?

### hellojtm — 2012-04-04T20:35:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daily-torpor-is-associated-with-telomere-length-change-over-winter-in-djungarian-hams.651/post-11390>

Some interesting notes: I added bold. and put my comments in _italics

RTL = relative telomere length

torpor = lethargy/inactivity (i had to look it up )

\- Twenty-five mature virgin female hamsters (aged: three to eight months) were switched from a long-day (16 L : 8 D) to a short-day photoperiod (8 L : 16 D, lights on: 07.00–15.00 h) and exposed to either a warm (20.0Â°C Â± 0.86Â°C s.d.) or cold **(9.2Â°C Â± 3.7Â°C) air temperature over 180 days. Food and water were provided ad libitum. (_not very cold air, I get this about 100 days out of the year in San Francisco, so dressing lightly is going to be significant)

\- **Initial RTL did not differ between the groups (RTL on day 0: 1.21 Â± 0.14; t = 0.34, p = 0.73). Over 180 days on a short-day photoperiod, the change in RTL differed significantly between the cold and warm groups (t = 3.17, p = 0.005). **Eleven out of 12 hamsters kept in the cold experienced an increase in their RTL (average change in RTL: 0.25 Â± 0.19), whereas RTL decreased or increased approximately equally among hamsters in the warm group** (average change: 0.03 Â± 0.14)

\- An effect of torpor was not associated with a reduction in metabolic energy expenditure: food intake was approximately 50 per cent greater in cold-exposed hamsters, regardless of torpor use**, yet neither group nor food intake was important in explaining the change in RTL _(death to cron theory?)_ (death to MFTHR and IGF-1 mTOR pathway theory?)_

\- Inferring from this set of models, the difference in body mass and initial RTL were the most important parameters (relative importance: 0.89 and 0.85, respectively) explaining the difference in RTL from day 0 to 180, whereas food intake was rather unimportant (relative importance value of 0.35). (I wasn't able to tell if it was starting, ending or changing BMI/TL**_**)

\- In this experiment, the use of daily torpor was associated with an increase in the length of telomeres in hamsters exposed to a short-day photoperiod. Our results support the hypothesis that, in addition to reducing energy expenditure, the use of torpor might also serve to retard somatic ageing during periods of unfavourable environmental conditions**.

jesse

### Jim — 2012-04-04T20:44:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/daily-torpor-is-associated-with-telomere-length-change-over-winter-in-djungarian-hams.651/post-11395>

So Jesse, to run this test in humans I suppose we would need 25 mature virgin females too right?

# Thread: What has been your biggest setback on the road to optimal? How did you overcome it?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 32
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dan in Utah — 2012-04-04T19:04:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11343>

Injury? Funky labs? Hormones? Ridicule from the peanut gallery? Neolithic thoughts messing with your paleolithic genes? Please share...

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-04T19:06:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11346>

> **Dan in Utah;11412 wrote:** Injury? Funky labs? Hormones? Ridicule from the peanut gallery? Neolithic thoughts messing with your paleolithic genes? Please share...

Besides my own Stupidity and refusing to do labs the first 3mths, and then not doing the most impt lab for me (GI FX) ... that would have to parasites... they've been holding me back for about 10yrs!

### MartiD — 2012-04-04T19:11:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11349>

Mine has been traveling. I started HCG in June 2011 and after second round and starting LeptinRX I was in NYC for a long weekend with family so timing of food and BAB was messed up. I gained 3 lbs but had it off in 3 days after picking up on reset. We just returned from Mexico last week and I indulged in a few desserts and few margaritas and came back with an extra 6 lbs (mostly fluid) but already down 4 of those 6. So for me I think I am glad to be able to have those setbacks and overcome them so quickly. I certainly couldn't have done that a year before!

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-04T21:58:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11426>

Traveling makes me gain, too, but I have learned to pack my george foreman, some frozen meat, coconut oil, etc. in my carry-on bag, and that helps me get BAB in when I'm on the move.

The biggest things for me have been...

1\. Mourning the loss of food (carrot cake and bavarian cream donuts for some reason...)

2\. Dealing with negative, sometimes hostile and always judgemental, feedback from family and friends.

3\. Unveiling thyroid/adrenal/hormone issues that are taking FOREVER to resolve because of CW doctors that waste my time/money (Slowly but surely getting there through labs and my own n=1 experiment).

4\. Getting over the scale and listening to my body.

5\. Pushing through my own dogma to do things like CT and shutting down the lights at night.

However, I LOVE, LOVE, LOVE my diet of animal fats and proteins. It doesn't get any better, and this way of life hardly feels like a diet or a sacrifice. Who can miss bread when there is bacon and t-bones on your plate?

### colleencoble — 2012-04-04T22:01:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11428>

Mandie, that negative feedback is all in the attitude. Don't be defensive. If someone comes at you with negative comments, LAUGH at them. Lift your eyebrow and smile and say "You just don't know as much about it as I do. I've studied all this extensively and I'm taking charge of my health. You should do the same." THAT will shut them up. LOL

I get no negative feedback because I'M the expert and I make sure they know it by my attitude. Really, attitude about this is everything. Try it!

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-04T22:21:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11440>

Thanks, Grammy. Yes, I've learned to be more assertive in how I live my life. It was bad last Aug. at the state fair, and I have had friends make comments here and there...but really I've decided I'm in charge of me. End of story. I used to talk about it a lot, but now I kind of keep to myself unless someone asks. I really believe the old adage, "my body is a temple," and nobody is forcing me to eat anything I don't want to and nobody is allowed to stress me out or make me feel inferior without my consent.

I love the paleo lifestyle though, and it always makes me happy when I can have friends/family over and they can feast on all the meat I serve up. It makes going to other people's houses lame because their meat servings are like 3 oz. and then they cram themselves FULL of starches and garbage. My, how things change!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-04T22:30:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11442>

Bingeing. Cravings.

I tried to stick to a low fat, high whole grain, low sugar diet for a LOOOOONG time, and did, in between the daily binges.

I embraced fat, protein, and traditional foods, & tried to stick to WAP for three years, and did, in between the several-times-a-week binges.

I tried to kick grains & stick to primal, and did, in between the weekly binges.

I tried to double my breakfast size & stick to the LR, and did, in between the monthly binges. Then one day, a few months in, it stuck. I haven't binged since. No cravings. Miracle.

Each of those changes increased fat, increased morning protein, and decreased wheat & sugar. Each of them made a big dent in my cravings at first. But over time, the cravings would spin out of control again, and I'd be wallowing again. The last change is the only one that has \*decreased\* cravings \*over time\*, and finally stopped the bingeing, with no sign of increasing problems again over time.

Biggest thing I can say for those whose obstacle to sticking to the diet is a disease that prevents you from sticking to the diet is to eat the BAB. Don't hold out. Increase it if the binges are not dead yet. Increase the fat, too. Just keep doing it, just keep dusting off and doing it again and again ... and eventually, you will win. You will be able to stick to the diet, and start reaping all the benefits of it!

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-04T22:38:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11446>

Oh, and excuses. RR helped me a lot with that. At first there was a lot of excuse-making.

\- I will NEVER cook more than one meal/day.

\- I will NEVER cook seven days a week.

\- I can't have breakfast ready w/i 30m; I must shower/pray/meditate/get my quiet time/etc.

\- I can't get 5hrs between bkfst & lunch; i have XYZ excuse.

\- I can't stop eating 4hrs before dinner; I will starve.

\- I will be bored to death of eggs & sausage.

\- I have too many children / too much to do / too little energy to cook my meals.

blah blah blah blah blah

Question is, do I want optimal, or not? I will re-arrange my schedule, and no, these changes (most of them) won't be forever. I can re-evaluate in a month/year if needed.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-04-05T04:52:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11596>

The last month been the worst for me on the road to optimal. Prior to that it seemed that every week I'd have a major win. So for 5 months I had solid progress that improved my life and health. I've been running a low grade fever, anxious, irritable (with anger boiling up ever day), exhausted, dark thoughts, bad sleep, overall "look" went downhill, craved the worst of the worst carb foods. I'd end up in pits of self pity and I cried often.

I dealt with it best I could in getting help getting all the household stuff done and did as much as I had in my toolbox to support my body with supplements and hormones through the wild detox. At the end of the day though what really kept me going is that there is a lot of life left in front of me to enjoy and going through some struggle and darkness will make the other side that much more sweet.

It is about discovering your own fears and kicking their ass.

It is about taking a hard look at priorities.

It is about renewal.

"The road is always better than the inn" - Cervantes.

Oh, and it is definitely about perspective too. It isn't like I injected MRSA and surgery without anesthesia.

### PaleoSue — 2012-04-05T05:12:23+0300

### ColdBren — 2012-04-05T07:37:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11639>

I think it is probaby a toxin dump, but I feel emotionally unwell and palpitations all the time. I am so frustrated by it because it stops me from moving forward and following the protocol. Panic attacks are what led me to this blog in the first place, if I am panicky....I am immobile....it is not a mental thing, it is a misfiring of my adrenaline....usually after I fall asleep. Any suggestions would be so appreciated (except the "just relax" ones lol )

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-05T08:34:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-11650>

I thought I was having setbacks then I realized my mistakes. Like many things in life I try to go fast and just get there. Now, I enjoy the ride. If if don't feel like CTing one day, I don't push myself. Invariably, I find myself continuing down the path and progressing, adapting, and improving. My lifestyle changes are completely sustainable, enjoyable, and enveloping.

Recovering our health is beautiful. The journey is like painting a canvas. No point in rushing that. Like the sunrise and sunset, take time to enjoy because it will be there. We all have cloudy days. We just need to remember the sun will come out again.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-05T20:48:46+0300

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-04-06T02:41:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-has-been-your-biggest-setback-on-the-road-to-optimal-how-did-you-overcome-it.652/post-12008>

> **ColdBren;11870 wrote:** RiverN, yes, its not good...my cortisol is too low in the morning and too high the rest of the time

First go read comment 153 on this blog (no I don't have a brain index of Dr.K's posts... this one is imprinted in my brain because it was a headdesk moment for me  and seems it could apply for you ). [http://jackkruse.com/hormone-cascade-101/](http://http://jackkruse.com/hormone-cascade-101/)

Also lots of good advice in this blog: [http://jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/](http://http://jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/)

Ask Shijin13 about her experience using PS to fix the high night time cortisol. Also go see her post #14807 here : [https://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread32345-1481.html](http://https://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread32345-1481.html)

I can tell you that working on my circadian rhythm by paying attention to light cycles was bringing my morning levels up last time I tested. Even though doc says that the cold will override the SCN and light cycles I'm continuing to get as many sunrises and sunsets as possible while I cold adapt. ([http://jackkruse.com/primal-cortisol-response/](http://http://jackkruse.com/primal-cortisol-response/))

Now here's the thing... I wish I had listened to Colleen and Dr. K on the virtues of bioidentical hormones months ago. I think the HCG forum folks call Colleen grammy but in my view she's more like a big sister. Anyway, after 6 months of piddling around and not being able to commit to going to this antiaging doc (I'm only 34!) or that naturopath I finally got over what was holding me back (related to the headdesk moment) and am trying out a doc. The hormones are really helping in the way I feel even though I had horrible detox symptoms for a few weeks. Now I wish I'd made more of an effort to deal with the hormones earlier (I had tested and knew they were a mess but failed to go to a Dr. who could and would help me).

I am planning on doing another saliva test this month to see where things are at.

HOpe some of these links give you some good ideas on new places to go on your journey to optimal!

### Jim — 2012-04-04T19:06:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/word-of-the-day.653/post-11345>

Here is the word of the day from Dictionary.com. I thought it was very appropriate for this forum.

April 3, 2012

zeitgeber TSAHYT-gey-ber, noun:

An environmental cue, as the length of daylight, that helps to regulate the cycles of an organism's biological clock.

The lightâ€“dark transition Zeitgeber is widely used by plants to set internal clocks not just for leaf movement but for many other activities as well.

\-- John King, Reaching for the Sun

The most prominent zeitgeber in humans is the light/dark cycle.

\-- Harold R. Smith, Cynthia Comella, Birgit HÃ¶gl, Sleep Medicine

### Jim — 2012-04-06T14:35:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/word-of-the-day.653/post-12085>

grokGRAHK

verb : to understand profoundly and intuitively

The novel's protagonists are driven by a desire to grok their place in the grand scheme of the universe.

"Both of these views completely fail to grok both the documented benefits of America's leadership in space and the font of inspiration for visionaries … who have aspired to send people there." — From an article by Greg Autry in Political Machine, February 2, 2012

### quelsen — 2012-04-06T16:43:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/word-of-the-day.653/post-12119>

To grok (play /ˈɡrɒk/) is to intimately and completely share the same reality or line of thinking with another physical or conceptual entity. Author Robert A. Heinlein coined the term in his best-selling 1961 book Stranger in a Strange Land. In Heinlein's view, grokking is the intermingling of intelligence that necessarily affects both the observer and the observed. From the novel:

Grok means to understand **so thoroughly that the observer becomes a part of the observed—to merge, blend, intermarry, lose identity in group experience.** It means almost everything that we mean by religion, philosophy, and science—and it means as little to us (because of our Earthling assumptions) as color means to a blind man.

The Oxford English Dictionary defines grok as "to understand intuitively or by empathy; to establish rapport with" and "to empathise or communicate sympathetically (with); also, to experience enjoyment". Other forms of the word include groks (present third person singular), grokked (past participle) and grokking (present participle).

In an ideological context, a grokked concept becomes part of the person who contributes to its evolution by improving the doctrine, perpetuating the myth, espousing the belief, adding detail to the social plan, refining the idea or proving the theory.

### Jim — 2012-04-08T18:40:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/word-of-the-day.653/post-12718>

Today's Word of the Day

**phosphorylation /phosÂ·phorÂ·yÂ·laÂ·tion/ (fos-for″ĭ-laÂ´shun) the metabolic process of introducing a phosphate group into an organic molecule.

\- oxidative phosphorylation the formation of high-energy phosphate bonds by phosphorylation of ADP to ATP coupled to the transfer of electrons from reduced coenzymes to molecular oxygen via the electron transport chain; it occurs in the mitochondria.

\- substrate-level phosphorylation the formation of high-energy phosphate bonds by phosphorylation of ADP to ATP (or GDP to GTP) coupled to cleavage of a high-energy metabolic intermediate.

As used in Dr K's Quilt [http://jackkruse.com/the-quilthow-to-beat-agin/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-quilthow-to-beat-agin/)

Levee 7:

ROS generation. ROS stands for Reactive Oxygen Species generation. We all know life is based upon energy production from the use of oxygen. This process is called oxidative phosphorylation**. Examples of ROS include oxygen ions and peroxides. Oxygen species are highly reactive due to the presence of unpaired outer shell electrons. ROS forms as a natural byproduct of the normal metabolism of oxygen and has important roles in cell signaling and homeostasis. This process occurs in the mitochondria. When we generate energy packages, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), from nutrients, we also generate free radicals of oxygen that leak from the cytochromes of our mitochondria. Cytochromes are the furnaces in mitochondria where ATP is made. This “leakiness” is critical to life and to the death programs inside our cell. The more “leaky” our mitochondria the more ROS we generate. Generally ROS is a bad omen for the cell but it can also signal the cell to lengthen its telomeres at times or to recruit the formation of new mitochondria to make energy. ROS generation is a measure of cellular stress, and that stress can be so great that is can damage cellular structures so that they no longer work properly. Examples are UV exposure or radiation exposure.

In general, lower ROS is better for longevity of the cell and for it to function optimally. A simple example of this fact is coenzyme Q10. Most people have heard of this cofactor, and this protein is used up to a great degree when you are making energy or the cell is stressed. Statin drugs cause depletion of this protein because of their mode of action on enzymes in blocking the building blocks of cholesterol, so they cause the cell to be more inefficient making energy as the CoEnQ10 is depleted. It is also used up when we are under stress due to long term insulin or cortisol secretion. Depletion of CoEq10 is why people can get cognitive change and muscle cramping when they are on statins too long or the dose becomes too high. The cause is because the cells in that part of the body can’t make the energy they require because of the side effect of the drug. Therefore many patients are told to add CoEnQ10 to their supplement list daily if they are on a statin.

### David — 2012-04-10T13:01:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/word-of-the-day.653/post-13290>

I nominate: _Zakalyatsa: Russian cold adaptation as submitted in the CT thread by **PaleoDentist**, born in northern Russia; immigrated to the US when 9. The post says that:

ZAKALYATSA basically means cold adapted.

The description is very compelling:

I went to my parents house for Passover dinner on Saturday. there were a bunch of their friends, all Russian, ranging in ages form 55-75. I asked them about the meaning of the word and the the process. The general consensus was this: zakatyatsa is done to improve the immune system and people who practice this never get sick in winter. basically it is people who tolerate the cold or are able to withstand the cold. they spoke about taking cold showers, splashing themselves with cold water in the morning, swimming in cold water. they said the epitome of this practice are people who swim in ice cold water in the winter. they are called "seals". In the US, we call this "the polar bear club"._

### Jim — 2012-04-28T13:48:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/word-of-the-day.653/post-19556>

littoral LIT-er-uhl, adjective:

1\. Pertaining to the shore of a lake, sea, or ocean.

2\. (On ocean shores) of or pertaining to the biogeographic region between the sublittoral zone and the high-water line and sometimes including the supralittoral zone above the high-water line.

3\. Of or pertaining to the region of freshwater lake beds from the sublittoral zone up to and including damp areas on shore.

noun:

1\. A littoral region.

The extensive artificialization of lake shorelines reduces the native littoral vegetation in quantity and quality.

\-- Alex CÃ³rdoba-Aguilar, Dragonflies and Damselflies

There was an exuberant fierceness in the littoral here, a vital competition for existence.

\-- John Steinbeck, The Grapes of Wrath

Littoral stems from the Latin word lītus which meant "shore." It was replaced by the Old English word shore but is still used by scientists.

# Thread: Lurker....coming out..lol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lurker-coming-out-lol.654/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cantweight — 2012-04-04T19:08:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lurker-coming-out-lol.654/post-11347>

Three very short years ago I thought it may just be the end for me.

Over the years I had been diagnosed with Fibromyalgia, CFS, LUPUS, Hasimotos, BiPolar Disorder, PCOS, and laundry list of other ailments. Surgeries and ER trips were commonplace. At the ripe old age of 35 I felt I was dying.

Pain was so severe I crawled up the stairs at night to tuck my kids into bed, I was losing my ability to speak properly. Couldn't spell my own name at times and was unable to comprehend simple instructions. I ran unexplained fevers of 104 degrees for 2 years straight. Multiple bouts of shingles plagued me. Migraines and arthritis made everyday unbearable. Exhaustion that left me in tears was all I knew. Who wouldn't be depressed with all that?? I often wondered how long I would be with my family physically and mentally. I had never been so scared.

I was on so much medication....wellbutrin, vicodin, diclophenac, plaquenil, synthroid, .....a list so long I can not even remember!!

In the winter of 2009 after 10 years of this insane decline I had had enough. I went off all meds and hauled ass to a holistic doc as my last resort. All the finest docs in the DC metro area had failed to help me. Yet a few visits with this doc had me on adrenal support, natural thyroid, and finally a diagnosis...Lyme disease.

3 years later I am a new person. Curing myself was more of an exorcism than a healing process, but one I'd do again in an instant to have regained such a wonderful quality of life.

Throughout his healing process I have run the gamut from GF to Vegan to HCG to Juicing to Paleo. My whole family has not had fast food in 3 years. No dyes, nitrates, HFCS, .....all that crap....gone.

However I am still walking around at 240lbs. How this was possible was beyond me!

Enter Dr Jack. Wk 5 of Leptin Rx....I feel great have lost 8lbs, stomach getting flatter by the day. Second week of CT up to 30 min baths at 58 degrees. (my tap doesn't get any colder)

Besides the stomach flattening the biggest change I have noticed is my ability to go up and down stairs effortlessly. Sounds silly, but often I would go step by step in the early morning or late evening. My body was tired and maybe a little sore, now I get a little giddy when I am practically skipping up and down the steps any time of day.

I am thrilled about this and the possibilities of what lies ahead. The knowledge and camaraderie shared on this forum is invaluable.

Cheers to you all!

To a life filled with bacon and sex

### David — 2012-04-04T21:54:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11425>

The procedures are relatively easy once you get going; but finding your way can be a bit complex at first. You need to do a lot of homework on this website and try to read everything (which is no small feat!)

**Chrisgraff (3-23-2012) wrote a list of things to do to get started (I copied the following):

1\. Sleep- turn down lights 3-4 hours before bed, reduce/eliminate computer & TV after sunset (alternatively...wear sunglasses). Ideal bedtime no later than 10pm. {ed: You must get in touch with your _circadian rhythm_.}

2\. 50g of protein for breakfast. - A typical breakfast for me is 4 eggs, 4 slices (no sugar) bacon, 2oz Turkey breast, 1-2 oz raw green vegetable (spinach or baby romaine lettuce usually). This will wipe out afternoon cravings.

3\. Eliminate sugar/sweeteners/starches - fruit included. (liquid stevia ok **if you must**).

4\. Eliminate Dairy, except for grass-fed, clarified butter. - I use Kerrygold brand (Silver package is unsalted).

5\. Eliminate Omega-six fats/increase Omega-three. - Most folks have way too much Omega-six stored in their body. Proper ratio should be 4:1. No olive oil/canola oil. Use coconut/palm/Macadamia oil instead.

Constipation? An issue for many. Dr. Kruse recommends Magnesium Malate two hours before bed. I have also had good luck with Chiro Klenz tea from time to time.

Your goal is less than 50g/carbs per day (25g if you have more than 30 lbs to lose).

**

If you have an iPhone, use an app like livestrong to keep track of what you're eating. GREAT app!

### colleencoble — 2012-04-04T21:58:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11427>

Art, the key for you is going to be a HUGE protein breakfast. Even now, if I don't eat protein, I get hungry. Stuff that food down first thing and start the icing. Both will give you a huge turnaround.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-04T22:06:54+0300

I echo the part about just getting started. It can be super easy to get bogged down with all the reading, but I guarantee you if you follow the basic rules for now, and tweak as you go, you will see AMAZING results!

Best wishes!

**

Thank you all for your support, **I'm gonna go for it. My quality of life is so low now and I'm in so much pain, it can't get much worse and there is truly little at risk.

I have a couple of things to bring up.....

First, I take low dose aspirin, fish oil and flax supplement. The supplements are for O6 to O3 ratio fats, which I've researched pretty thoroughly already. I noticed Dr. J wasn't thrilled with flax supplements, but the ratio of 06 to 03 is far better in flax than in any other type of oil by far-so I'm slightly at a loss. I know flax tastes bad, but for those who have to force oil by the spoonful to do the BAB, I would think a brief bad taste would be a minor issue since flax has so much more O3.

The info about ox bile is good info for me. If the gallbladder is the mechanism to break down fat, then a missing gallbladder seems pretty significant (medically speaking). Glad to know about the alternative and that the missing gallbladder isn't a deal breaker.

With regard to the gallbladder failure mode.....is it possible that the gallbladder originally became dysfunctional due to the interference from blood glucose? I wonder, if I had been on a Paleo type diet years ago, is it more likely that my gallbladder might have not failed at all?**

Should I begin using GB Digestion from Pure Encapsulations now rather than beginning to use it later??? Or, should I wait until the LR is completed?

**

Despite my really marginal BAB this am, I did notice I wasn't impossibly hungry by lunchtime-which is a vast improvement. I should also note that I've already been fructose free gluten free for the past week-4 days before I found LR and Paleo (I was able to figure out these were no good and just stopped them even though I didn't have diet plan/strategy yet).

I am taking insulin now (started March 15), and I'm in the process of slowly ramping up the dose. I intend to test BG often since the LR process might radically and abruptly make my BG lower.

Again, ty to you all. I'll keep you posted. I can't wait to convert from a sugar burner to a fat burner!!!

Regards,

Art

### Jim — 2012-04-05T05:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11622>

Art,

Good on ya for cutting out the gluten and fructose. Assuming your pancreas is still working correctly then you should be able to digest fat. The problem is that it only produces digestive enzymes at a certain somewhat continuous rate. Your gallbladders function was to store these enzymes for when you eat a large meal. Think your big breakfast. Consuming all that fat at one meal could quickly overwhelm your pancreas. You therefore might have a problem digesting the fat in a large meal. The digestive supplement should help prevent any issues.

I would dump the flax and go with coconut oil. It's pretty much a wonder fat. I eat it right out of the jar. You can use olive oil to make salad dressings etc.

Ideally you will not snack at all. However while you are transitioning, if you get an uncontrollable urge either eat some protein or a spoonful of coconut oil. This will keep your carbs low and tamp down the hunger until the next meal.

Hang in there.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-05T06:03:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11626>

Welcome, Art. It can be really overwhelming starting all this - all the info, the science, the worries. But bottom line: eat your BAB (and make sure it has at least 50g of protein (ideally, meat and eggs, not chicken) and lots of fat. Coconut oil will serve you really well. It burns fat, it satiates hunger and it's great for you. Do NOT worry about eating too much. When you're trying to lose weight and cravings we are often so used to trying to stop ourselves from eating and holding back that it's engrained (at least it was for me). The freedom in this is that you get to health (and weight loss) by not worrying. Just eat the good foods - clean diet as has been suggested - and don't snack if you can help it.

Hang in there.

### gemstar — 2012-04-05T07:19:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11635>

Hey Art,

Welcome to the first days of the rest of your healthier life. I've been playing with low carb for many many years. First experience was our old buddie Dr. Atkins. I even did the CALP (carb addicts life plan) for a while a number of years ago. They all worked wonderfully, but what I didn't know then was how important it is to eat enough FATS in order to not get hungry. So at one point my yearning for carbs became larger than my willpower (duh!).

I live in a country where grass fed animal products are not available. Our green pastures are only green for a few months a year. Agribusiness has taken over here in Israel on many levels. Fortunately our produce mentality is based in locally and seasonally grown items. But I still eat low carb high fat and can only brag about our own home grown olive oil and the wonderful date syrup we can sub for your maple syrup.

Eating low carb high fat is better than a diet based in grains and legumes, even if it's not organic and grass-fed. I also have lost quite a bit weight without all the exercising so many people seem to do. So you even don't need to do anything like that to start losing the weight.

BTW, do make sure that the coconut oil you buy is cold pressed only and not refined in any way. It seems that in some countries that produce and export CO, there is a demand for them to refine the CO so that it stays hard even in high temps. I found ORGANIC CO in our HFS and it stayed hard during our very hot summers, this led me to learn about the oils. Cold pressed CO will have the distinct aroma of coconut. Any other kind will not!! Same goes for palm oil. I have yet to find cold pressed non damaged palm oil here in Israel, and I have a feeling it's not imported.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-05T20:23:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11812>

TY all.

I have a brief update, with some news that is not great.

First, the update. This is day 2 of the LR.

I had a sub level protein BAB yesterday (Day 1 of the LR) and a modest plate of raw green veggies for dinner. I did have some nuts, brazil and cashews. During the day yesterday, I didn't get horribly hungry until well past noon. I did not sleep well the night before due to stress, so I went to bed earlier than I should have and I simply had to take a 1 hour nap in the afternoon-I set my alarm so that I wouldn't sleep longer than 1 hour.

Today, I woke up at 5, but didn't get out of bed until 8:30-I just felt horrible in general and couldn't get myself to move.

I made the BAB, this time with 2 large slices of ham. Here's where the bad news comes...... After I made and ate the BAB, I looked at the package the ham came in. One of the ingredients was 'dextrose', which I know is a sugar! I was so bummed and I thought I had been sugar free these last few days!!! Clearly it's not the end of the world, but it is definitely a setback and a newbie mistake. SO, I learned something else....ALWAYS LOOK AT THE LABEL, without exception.

I pulled a frozen turkey out and began thawing it in the fridge shortly after breakfast. I looked at the label this time. It said there was 'sugar' in it!!! I was so damned disgusted. There's no need to add sugar to a whole turkey!!!!

I did read the leaky gut and the gaps diet FAQ-wow!!! It sure seems to me that all this crap the food vendors add to the food has created many of the medical issues we have today!!! Maybe all/most of the ailments and medical issues are man-made by the food packaging industry.

Saturday, my wife and I plan to drive 50 miles to a food coop known for organic and homegrown foods. We have a local Amish clan nearby that supplies some of the food there, and they are known to be wholesome. Hopefully I can buy meat there that isn't raised on grain.

I have a question about butter. I keep seeing 'pastured' butter mentioned in the forum. Here in Maine, we have many dairy producers. We can buy pure butter in the supermarket and it is all local, and it's all raised with hay for feed (no grains). It's my understanding that I need to buy only pastured and clarified butter. Is the locally produced butter the type I need, or do I need to seek out other sources of 'specialty' butter?

Again, TY to u all.

Art

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-05T20:54:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11827>

Hi Art! The butter many of us on the forum use is Kerrygold brand butter. It's carried in Whole Foods or Trader Joes if you have either one. If you know of a small local dairy that pastures the cows during the summer months and feeds them hay during winter it's probably OK. You really don't want the grain or anything from cows that have been fed hormones (to keep the milk coming) or antibiotics.

Also I don't think anyone mentioned that you can, if you need to, eat up to 75 gm of protein in the morning for your BAB.

I buy most of my meat online. Check eatwild.com for sources/ranches near you. I buy from a producer in the northern part of the state and bits and pieces from uswellness meats like liverwurst...YUM! I have a slice every morning with b/fast. Also be sure to make sure that the beef is 100% grass fed and not grass fed and gran finished.

We're glad to see you here and welcome! My best advice is to enjoy this and have fun! It's such an easy way to eat and live.

### ealachan — 2012-04-05T20:59:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11832>

Art, if you're in Maine you might have Hannaford grocery stores near you? We have those down here in Vermont, and they carry the Kerrygold butter that everyone here is so fond of. I was quite happy to find that out.

### ineedanLR — 2012-04-05T21:14:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11836>

### nonchalant — 2012-04-05T21:52:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/intro-new-to-dr-j-and-paleo.655/post-11848>

Welcome Art!

Locally produced butter from cows fed hay is good. When the cows start eating Spring grass, that is the best butter.

Around here in Texas, my local dairy feeds them grass year around, but gives them grains while they're being milked. So just a small amount of grains.

I had to work myself up to the BAB. Took me a while to be able to digest all the fat and protein.

So glad you and your family are working towards optimal!

\-Sally

# Thread: Leptin in the news
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-in-the-news.656/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Adria — 2012-04-04T20:34:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-in-the-news.656/post-11389>

On the news last night they spoke of obesity and how to measure it more accruately using leptin blood test:

[http://www.latimes.com/health/la-he-obesity-20120403,0,3807756.story](http://http://www.latimes.com/health/la-he-obesity-20120403,0,3807756.story)

**We may be fatter than we think, researchers report

A new study finds that the widely used body mass index, or BMI, may be understating obesity in many people.

The study also found that a measure of leptin, an appetite-boosting hormone secreted by the body's fat deposits, generally tracks with an individual's level of fatness as measured by DXA. Testing leptin levels may be a simpler, cheaper way to reliably distinguish people with healthy levels of body fat from those carrying too much, the researchers wrote.**

### shilohman — 2012-04-04T21:22:04+0300

\* The Paleo Extreme Reality show, where people will compete to see how long they can stay in an ice bath.

\* Dr. Oz or someone like him will march Oprah out with a permanent Barbie doll figure, of course Dr. Oz will have to 'modify' the diet so

he can market his own superior version.

\* Tony Horton from P90X fame will have 'Paleo 90X' DVDs

\* The grain companies will come out with 'Paleo safe starch' chips and snacks (They will lie as usual)

\* A documentary will be made about Dr. K and all of his pioneering efforts with leptin and CT.

\* The models on the covers of magazines/commercials will be Photoshoped even thinner than they are now.

\* Other doctors/hucksters will come out of the wood work and have to modify the diet to make it safe for the masses

\* The Army will have an elite core of very, very thin, but super strong warriors who will become invisible when marching sideways

\* Breakfast cereals will be fortified with grass fed protein and each box of cereal will have a super strong leptin sensitive action figure

.

.

.

If paleo ever goes main stream, it will be sad at what happens, oh well.

# Thread: Heat Intolerance
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-04T23:03:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11455>

I know it has been said several times that once we become cold adapted our bodies can still adapt to heat as well and maintain a normal temp. However, I'm finding the opposite to be happening and I can't even say that I'm fully cold adapted yet either.

This doesn't surprise me at all given that my body tends to always respond to things differently . . . apparently I'm a bit of a complex case!

I'm really struggling when I'm in warm environments. My office is very warm and stuffy and I assumed it was more the stuffy air that was getting to me. When I'm in there my cheeks get flushed and super hot. I start to feel extremely weak all over and sometimes even get nauseous. This week I'm staying with a friend in South Carolina and we're having beautiful weather. I'm from New Jersey, so this 80 degree weather is new for me right now! I've been trying to get outside and soak up some vitamin D, but the same thing is happening to me that happens in my office . . . nausea, weakness, fatigue.

Any ideas why my body is reacting this way?

### nonchalant — 2012-04-04T23:16:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11463>

Hi BK, how long have you been CTing? I wonder if it might be toxins. I'm finding the opposite--others complain about the 80F+ heat before I notice it.

### Guest — 2012-04-04T23:20:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11464>

There is a guy in my building that is cold adapted and he said heat does not bother him. He wore nothing but shorts and a t shirt outside all winter long. I am thinking that the healtheir we get the better we are able to tolerate all temps.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-04T23:30:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11471>

> **Nonchalant;11532 wrote:** Hi BK, how long have you been CTing? I wonder if it might be toxins. I'm finding the opposite--others complain about the 80F+ heat before I notice it.

I've been CTing for 6 weeks total - 1 week of face dunks, 2 weeks of ice pack spotting and 3 weeks of baths.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-04T23:32:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11472>

Kevin Cottrell posted some remarkable things on FB that I would tell you to ask him about......he is the leader of Paleo fx. Friend him.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-04T23:45:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11486>

> **Jack Kruse;11541 wrote:** Kevin Cottrell posted some remarkable things on FB that I would tell you to ask him about......he is the leader of Paleo fx. Friend him.

Just sent him a friend request. Thanks Dr. K!

### Adriana — 2012-04-05T15:23:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11691>

The health of your liver also influences your ability to tolerate heat, as I found out after I got Hepatitis A. You might consider taking milk thistle supplements.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-05T15:51:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11704>

Hormonal "hot flashes" from detox or hormone re-arrangement?

I'm loving starting the day on my deck while it's still sub-60 and spending all day out there homeschooling and eating lunch till it's 80+, then leaving the door open and returning to the deck for dinner as the sun sets and we return to the 60s. All in tank top & shorts.

### Sherry — 2012-04-05T16:33:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-11716>

> **BJK77;11524 wrote:** I know it has been said several times that once we become cold adapted our bodies can still adapt to heat as well and maintain a normal temp. However, I'm finding the opposite to be happening and I can't even say that I'm fully cold adapted yet either.

When I'm in there my cheeks get flushed and super hot. I start to feel extremely weak all over and sometimes even get nauseous. This week I'm staying with a friend in South Carolina and we're having beautiful weather. I'm from New Jersey, so this 80 degree weather is new for me right now! I've been trying to get outside and soak up some vitamin D, but the same thing is happening to me that happens in my office . . . nausea, weakness, fatigue.
>
>
>
> Any ideas why my body is reacting this way?

This is exactly what has happened to me over the last few years. And I live in the Florida Keys so summers were most of the year with the high humidity. I started escaping to the high elevations of Colorado for the worst of the hurricane season. What I found there is that I could better tolerate the heat because the humidity level is so low. However, I've been doing the leptin Rx for over 3 months and CT for two months and I'm finding it so much easier to tolerate the heat. It's not perfect yet. I still have bouts of intense uncomfortableness with the heat but they are diminishing in intensity, length of time and frequency. I haven't changed anything else so it is definitely the Kruse protocol. I'm sure you'll start noticing an improvement.

### Birdy — 2012-04-07T05:13:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-12368>

I had been sleeping and waiting in the morning for things to warm up before going outside. Now I do the opposite. I do outdoor things early in the day to get the cold. I have found that after a few weeks, I am not too hot when it is hot. I think it also might be that I don't wear as many clothes, and am usually in a constant state of just a little bit of cold, so I have some upwards flexibility if I need it.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-05-04T16:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heat-intolerance.658/post-21307>

So at least on the first day, I found hot weather to be much worse than prior to cold adaptation.

# Thread: Ice/Cold post exercise - Blunts Adaptations
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 49
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Barry — 2012-04-04T23:43:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11483>

I've been looking at cold water/contrast baths/cryotherapy for recovery post exercise for a while now.

If you look at all the studies, and there are plenty of meta-analysis to go by, the conclusions are as follows:

\- Ice baths do numb the pain

\- But they blunt adaptations

Basically, ice/cold therapy inhibits the acute inflammation. This prevents the body from adapting to the stress. This is not what you want to happen. You want to adapt so that after subsequent training sessions you don't cause as much damage and can thus train harder.

So the take home message for me has been:

\- only use ice baths during competition or after extreme exercise

\- but the rest of time, let the body adapt while you train to improve adaptations

So I'm not sure why Dr K is recommending "icing" after all exercise ?

... question everything ;-)

### Glamorama — 2012-04-05T05:04:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11599>

I exercise and do my LHT hours prior to CT. So gym at 10 am, CT at 5 pm, the walk around 6 pm. So far I've had nothing but gains in the gym.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-05T15:42:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11700>

I train 3 x week from 4-5:30, dinner then CT after, for an hour. CT on days off from training as well. My technique coach finally picked up a calculating error I put into my program. For six weeks I have been doing a MENS program, forgot to adjust for women, I have been doing 5# jumps on worksets of sq and dl, 2.5 on bench, should have been 2.5/1.25 lbs. ha ha, oops. Also I am "masters" so I should have factored in a lighter recovery day, and I didn't. I still feel like I could keep making these jumps too. My powerlifting meet is in less than two weeks, I have put on 12.5# on my single squat which is significant gain in 6 weeks, from 220 to 232.5

I don't feel my adaptation is "blunted". I would have stalled in the 3rd week. AFter the meet, I have a la few months, won't be interrupted with tapering for/recovery from a meet. I should be able to see much longer linear progression. Yay

### hellojtm — 2012-04-05T21:28:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11842>

> **Barry;11552 wrote:** So the take home message for me has been:
>
>
>
> \- only use ice baths during competition or after extreme exercise
>
> \- but the rest of time, let the body adapt while you train to improve adaptations
>
>
>
> So I'm not sure why Dr K is recommending "icing" after all exercise ?
>
>
>
> ... question everything ;-)

Good post Barry. I would like to hear Dr. Kruse's thoughts on it as well. The acute inflammation from exercise is valuable as long as your chronic inflammation is low/non-existent. So I always wonder about the context of the advice given. Is icing after all exercise optimal for someone with low inflammation and a good acute inflammation response? Seems like it is not. But, then again, I don't think we can control for the diet and lifestyle of all the people in your meta-analysys, and most likely they had some level of chronic inflammation as the norm (it's normal), so perhaps it isn't even of benefit then!

jesse

### Barry — 2012-04-06T01:32:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-11980>

> **hellojtm;11911 wrote:** Good post Barry. I would like to hear Dr. Kruse's thoughts on it as well. The acute inflammation from exercise is valuable as long as your chronic inflammation is low/non-existent. So I always wonder about the context of the advice given. Is icing after all exercise optimal for someone with low inflammation and a good acute inflammation response? Seems like it is not. But, then again, I don't think we can control for the diet and lifestyle of all the people in your meta-analysys, and most likely they had some level of chronic inflammation as the norm (it's normal), so perhaps it isn't even of benefit then!
>
>
>
> jesse

Jesse

most studies never control "everything". However, the general consensus is that constant ice treatment post exercise inhibits the acute inflammation signalling that is beneficial to adaptations. Thats what the science says and its pretty well documented at this stage.

There is obviously some timing considerations to take into account i.e. immediate icing is counter productive, but perhaps a time delay between cessation of training and icing is beneficial, like what Glamaroma and Teenie Leek do above.

Don't get me wrong, I'm not saying CT doesn't work in the Leptin Signalling context etc. I'm purely talking about the athlete, who trains regularly and uses ice baths after every session for recovery. The evidence shows that this is wrong and its pretty conclusive and makes sense.

Performance Enhancement = exercise + acute stress + acute inflammation + adaptation x repeat.

NOT

exercise + acute stress + anti/inhibitory inflamation = poor adaptation

### SimonM — 2012-04-06T10:10:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12071>

I'd like to see the references you are looking at, as I find it very hard to believe that ice baths a) prevent all adaptation and that b) anyone can possibly prove that long term.

By adaptation you are talking about everything from improvements in V02 max, lactate tolerance, muscle enzymes, quantity and quality of mitochondria, new capillarisation, cardiac muscle changes leading to improved stroke volume and on and on and on... so, really? Ice baths stop all that? You're laying a lot of adaptation at the feet of acute inflammation.

Then I want to see a study or two that quantifies and balances the loss of "adaptation" when set against the benefits of faster recovery and the ability to train more and/or harder than would be the case without ice baths. Many of the elites I know use ice baths to the legs to enable them to train twice a day.

Also bear in mind that, for athletes, adaptation sometimes takes a back seat to recovery: think Tour de France cyclists with another stage in the morning', or a track and field athlete (especially a masters' athlete) with championship heats and finals giving him or her five races in seven days to get through. In those situations ice baths are performance enhancing.

### eva — 2012-04-06T11:52:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12077>

plus.. is it the case that these studies are done on warmadapted people. so maybe its not the case for coldadapted. plus its a choice - what is more important - sacrificing a bit of proteinsynthesis - gain a lot of HGH and testo. i know my choice. and once you are oldadapted - maybe the effect will be different alltogether. i understand that inflammation is concidered necessary for muscle growth - but is it? im not so certain anymore...

### SimonM — 2012-04-07T00:08:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12287>

> **Kath;12235 wrote: Barry's post is confusing me. I've been trying to advise my teenage weightlifting sons re CT, and now I'm less sure that I understand it correctly. I told them to work on getting cold adapted and at the very least take a cold shower after a workout. They don't eat paleo yet, but are getting closer. Is the timing of CT after a workout addressed somewhere in Dr. K's blogs? Back to checking CT 6...
>
>
> I think that's because the science is nowhere near as conclusive as Barry is suggesting and that the "science" (such as it is) directly conflicts with real-life experience. I know it is a pain, but you really do have to experiment with your boys and see what works for them long-term. I say long-term because the overwhelmingly majority of sports "science" experiments are short-term. There is also currently no placebo control for an ice bath known to man
>
>
>
> That said, a cold shower after training is NOT going to interfere with inflammation and/or adaptation, so you are perfectly safe recommending that! And you can also get them to follow the protocol Glamorama and Teenie Leek are using - they are both seeing improved performance with cold treatments - timed away from training sessions.
>
>
>
> Dr Alex Hutchinson, who writes the Sweat Science blog now a permanent feature on runnersworld.com, [reviewed the "ice bath" research at the end of 2011](http://http://sweatscience.com/ice-baths-lab-fatigue-vs-real-fatigue/) in the wake of what he described as a "a major meta-analysis in the British Journal of Sports Medicine that adds a couple of interesting insights. The analysis covers 14 different studies with a total of 239 athletes." (Not much of a sample and I'd love to know how many of the subjects were highly trained elite athletes compared to the usual untrained volunteers - makes a huge difference to the response.)
>
>
>
> He quotes those researchers as concluding (my emphasis added):
>
>
>
>
> > "It has, however, been suggested that the inflammatory response is critical for optimal repair of damaged tissue. Although the mechanisms of training adaptation are not fully understood, it may be detrimental to reduce the commonly accepted damage-repair-adaptation model by diminishing the inflammatory response; however, there is a lack of evidence to support this.
>
> **
>
> > This raises the question of whether frequent or habitual use of strategies designed to reduce inflammatory responses can be detrimental for elite athlete adaptation to training."
>
>
>
> Hutchinson himself says:
>
>
>
> "...no one really knows whether repeated ice baths have a practically significant effect on reducing adaption to training. From what I understand, the English Institute of Sport has been advising its athletes to avoid ice baths after routine sessions during heavy training phases, but to incorporate them during tapering and competition. In other words, periodize your recovery protocols so that you maximize adaption during training periods and maximize recovery during competition periods. Does this work? Maybe we’ll find out at next year’s Olympics!"

Ice baths are like taking antioxidant supplements - beneficial but should be timed right so as not to interfere with inflammation which, never mind adaptation, is crucial for healing to occur. However use of ice baths after/during training - a standard set by France's national institute of sport being 15 minutes in 50 degree cold water - is a far cry from CT...I think.

### Barry — 2012-04-07T02:15:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12327>

Simon

First up, do us a favour and lose the sarcastic tone , that's not what this forum is about.

Secondly , I'm here to merely postulate and hypothesis and to try at look at the big picture. I also happen to work as a sports scientist and have actually spent a number of years working with a colleague who actually did his phd in ice baths/recovery. So I know a few things and I know the research.

So my purpose here is to share and learn, that's all. I'm not trying to mislead anyone or even claim to be correct.

I also work with elite world class athletes and I know exactly what they do. And I can tell you, they don't ice bath post training. They are only advised to do so during competition, which if you read my very first post it's exactly what I pointed out.

Now if you want to look at the research , I have heaps of it . Why don't you read this in its entirety first and then we'll go from there [http://www.sonafe.org.br/images\_up/immersion\_cryotherapy.pdf](http://http://www.sonafe.org.br/images_up/immersion_cryotherapy.pdf)

>

> What would save some time is if you give us a reference or two when you are launching what you call a hypothesis on us. But really it didn't read like a hypothesis, it was more like a done-and-dusted, case proven statement:
>
>
>
> If you look at all the studies, and there are plenty of meta-analysis to go by, the conclusions are as follows:
>
>
>
> \- Ice baths do numb the pain
>
> \- But they blunt adaptations
>
>
>
> Basically, ice/cold therapy inhibits the acute inflammation. This prevents the body from adapting to the stress. This is not what you want to happen. You want to adapt so that after subsequent training sessions you don't cause as much damage and can thus train harder.

With one Google search I found a meta analysis from 2011 in which the researchers said the effects on adaptation are unproven. If you are hear to share and learn it would be helpful if you would address that, rather than refer me to a paper from 2007 which was a one-off treatment measuring inflammation markers and not addressing anything to do with adaptation. I don't know what the second link refers to as it doesn't work.

So you work with elites who don't use ice after training; that is not my experience of what the elite runners and triathletes here in Boulder do, not to mention the pro US football teams who are currently seem to exploiting cryotherapy chambers en masse if the media are to be believed.

We know that ice baths reduce inflammation; they MAY blunt adaptation, but unless there's been a better or more up to date meta-analysis than the one I referred to, published September 2011 in the British Journal of Sports Medicine, that hasn't been proven. So I'd still like to know how you are so sure. And I need to know this not just on an intellectual level but for highly practical reasons. You may work with elite world-class athletes; I AM one, albeit at the masters level, and getting this wrong has major implications for me.

As far as I'm concerned the spirit and purpose of these forums is about questioning and exploration. We are all trying to sort out how best to use Jack's discoveries for our own best purposes. I don't want to get into a useless argument with you, so I'm sorry if my tone has offended you... but we are, as you say, here to question everything.

### Barry — 2012-04-08T23:25:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-cold-post-exercise-blunts-adaptations.660/post-12789>

Simon

right, things get lost in translation, so screw my comments... lets get straight to the point and we can help each other, power in numbers and all that :

Regarding the first study: the reason I posted this is because I know the researcher. What happened after this study is as follows :

Several weeks later, the same group then conducted a follow-up study to look at the effects on subsequent training bouts. By repeating the model of the initial study following a bout of exercise promoting exercise induced muscle soreness, they achieved similar results. However, they then waited a week & repeated the exercise bout but this time allowed neither group to recover using cold water immersion & what they found has significant implications for the use of ice baths in the training phase. The results showed that, after the second bout of exercise, the group that had performed cold water immersion after the initial exercise test demonstrated ratings of perceived muscle soreness, decrements in maximum voluntary contraction & levels of serum myoglobin post-exercise consistent to the those recorded on the control group of subjects following the initial bout. In contrast, the group that had initially acted as the control group, showed significantly lower ratings of perceived muscle soreness, decrements in maximum voluntary contraction & levels of serum myoglobin when the results were compared - ie, the group that hadn't had an ice bath had managed to adapt & gain some form of protection from the damaging effects of the exercise, whilst the adaptation had been blunted in the group that initially had had an ice bath.

This isn't published yet so you'll just have to take my word for it for now. But what happened as a result of these findings is this - The English Institute of Sport now advise all their athletes NOT to ice bath post standard training sessions, only competition. In terms of the mechanisms involved, well we are not entirely sure. Possibly something related to mTOR, Sacromere's and Interluekins. Not my specialist field (I'm more into the Nutrition), so this is a guess for now on my part.

However, if you look at all the meta-analysis, all conclude the same thing - more research is needed, its not clear whether ice baths improve recovery. Also, there are very few long term studies, apart from the unpublished one I refered to above. Yet, when you think of it, acute inflammation is what drives the hormetic affect and thus physiological adaptations. So, I believe that it doesn't make sense to constantly (and immediately) ice the muscles post training. Let the body adapt.. that's how it works best

Your take ?
