# Thread: dr. Kruse - please help understand the labs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-please-help-understand-the-labs.712/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### marieee — 2012-04-07T05:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-please-help-understand-the-labs.712/post-12376>

Hello Dr. Kruse. I have been following strict low carb no dairy/fruit/alcohol/grains//legumes/nuts Paleo for the recent 3 months. Additionally I have been also doing short high intensity training 3 - 5 days a week. Yesterday, I've checked my blood sugar after exercise using glucometer, and it was 140(!) right after, and I started to wonder if I'm not doing any damage to myself!I have had some hormonal problems (unbalanced women hormones, thyroid)for several years now due to eating disorder(only fruits and veggies no fat for several years!!). I know now how big damage I did to my body by doing that...and I can only regret it now..i didn't now anything about the right nutrition then...

Anyway, recently I have my blood drawn, and here are the results:A1C-6,4%(!)-which suggest diabetes!?-never tested it before though,AST 40 U/I (elevated), ALT 57 U/I (elevated), TSH 1,788, ft3 2,08, ft4 1,28, (FSH 4,17mIU/ml; LH 1,52mIU/ml;estradiol 16 pg/ml - all on 3rd day of cycle),cholesterol 8,15 mmol/l; HDL 3,43mmol/l; LDL 4,51, TG 0,47mmol/l; 21 day progesterone : 1,6 ng/ml, DHEA 8,99 ng/ml, hs-CRP 0,06, homocysteine: 11,78, vit D3:44.9, free testosterone 2,01pg/ml, testosterone 80 ng/l, insulin 3,2. I have checked my blood sugar after eating meals and it never exceeds 110 after low carb. Can this high number of A1C be caused by HIT alone or am I missing something? My fasting blood sugar is on high end though - 97/98. I just hope I do nat have diabetes on top of that. Can elevated AsT/ALT be caused by high cholesterol and recent high fat consumption or maybe smth else cause I did not have this problem before...I'd appreciate if someone could help me understand these results...because my body is obviously crying for help...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-07T06:44:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-please-help-understand-the-labs.712/post-12386>

sorry cant practice medicine on the internet......

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-07T22:23:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-please-help-understand-the-labs.712/post-12522>

I see the powers that be got to you!

### LisaAPB — 2012-04-08T06:58:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-please-help-understand-the-labs.712/post-12594>

marieee, you sound like you are in a similar place to where I was 18 months ago. The high ALT/AST is indicative of fatty liver disease. Last year, even when eating low carb, I would notice elevated blood glucose after an intense workout. You also said you have thyroid and other hormone imbalances? The good news is, you can get better. I did. My fatty liver is gone, my fasting blood glucose is below 80, my high triglycerides are low, my weight is down and overall things are pretty good. But, it did **not** happen in three months. Give yourself a minimum of 6-9 months of eating clean seasonal Paleo style. Continue workouts, but don't overdo it. Make sure you get good sleep nightly. Also, the introduction of cold exposure has dropped my fasting glucose by 10 points, because up until 2 months ago my fasting BG averaged in the low 90's. Many of us are noticing that side effect. Keep plugging away, and re-test in 6-9 months. I believe you will be pleased with the results. You didn't develop metabolic imbalance overnight, so it will take a little more time. Keep up the good work!

### Dextery — 2012-04-08T23:34:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-kruse-please-help-understand-the-labs.712/post-12790>

Marieee, take your fasting blood glucose in the morning after sleeping and 12 hours since eating last night's dinner. It would be around 90-95 or below. Then eat your normal breakfast and then wait two hours after finishing breakfast and then again test your FBG.

It should be around the same as before eating and if it is, then you are secreting enough insulin to control the sugar spike you got from breakfast. If not close the same, then you need to cut any carbs you are eating and retest.

If you are eating more than 50-60 grams of protein and you are in ketosis, your body will make glucose from the protein to feed the brain and body of the glucose it needs. In some people eating a ketogenic diet, their fast blood glucose will be in the 110-115 range.

As far as your A1c being elevated...this is an average of the last three months of blood sugar...probably reflects you have only been limiting your carbs as in a paleo diet for three months.

And it you want to consult with Dr Kruse, you can sign up for consultation. But he cannot by law practice medicine on the internet...period.

And supplement with Vit D3. Your level at 44 is low. 25000IU a day for a month, then 10000IU/day for another month. Then retest. Dr K wants his patients to be 70 to 100ng/ml. These doses are what many of us did to get to optimal levels.

High intensity interval training should be done in the 3-6pm time slot. And do it three days a week. You do not want to stress your body by hard core HIT.

Stick with your paleo diet. Use lots of coconut oil. Marry the Leptin Reset protocol with the Leaky Gut Protocol and you will begin to see good bloods and body composition. And best of all you will feel great. It may take a while but stick with the program.

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-07T07:54:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-12401>

I wasn't 100% sure where to put this but, I'm wondering what kind of effects the Leptin RX and Cold Thermogenesis on things like mental illness.

For me, I've dealt with social anxiety, self-loathing, and depression for YEARS, since I was about 13 or 14. I'm 27 right now.

While my depression has greatly lifted from Paleo/Primal alone, I still have to deal with the social anxiety and low self-esteem. I don't know how much of these behaviors I still have are from some sort of inflammation in the brain or if they were learned behaviors that need to be unlearned. FYI my mom was an alcoholic up until I graduated high school at 17 and my dad was (still is) pretty verbally abusive. My house wasn't really a place to feel safe, it was a place of fear and loneliness.

I feel like my body is getting healthy but my mind is still a mess. I'd like to have a healthy mind too. I've done cognitive behavioral therapy and it's helped get out of the hole of depression. But my self-esteem issues are still as bad as ever. I know that some of the things that happened to me as a child have left marks, but there are many people out there who have had it MUCH worse and who are much more resilient mentally.

### Melinda — 2012-04-07T08:16:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-12403>

Hi Rhoswen,

You have the answer in your last sentence. People have had it worse than you and developed resilience so you can too. Focus on this minute, accomplish something small like washing the dishes; then focus on this hour, put things away, get ready for tomorrow, go to bed. Repeat. Hopefully, you will build on this routine of accomplishing small but important tasks of daily living and feel better over time. Don't worry about how the future will work out, just do something right now. It works.

I would love to hear Dr. Kruse's ideas about mental illnesses too. I can tell you his protocol has reduced my panic attacks. I am thinking and hoping that it is an inflammation or nutritional issue. When you realize that the neurotransmitters and hormones play such a key part in moods/emotions, you see how easy it is to have a deficiency somewhere. ### Rhoswen — 2012-04-07T09:05:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-12411>

Perhaps I should go into more details...

I feel like I have accomplished a lot in my life... I'm getting healthier right now, I have a nice apartment, I have a great job, I cook good foods for myself, and I take care of my body.

I have people in my life who like me, I'm well liked at work, even though my family is a bit dysfunctional, they still love me too. However, I am in a constant battle with what I would call the little hate monkey in my head who tells me no matter how much these people care, I am unlovable. There is something about me that automatically makes it so that I am a loathsome person. Anyone who says they care MUST be lying and trying to use me, and will throw me away the second I have no use anymore, which will be fairly quick. Even if I was super model lovely, I would still be unlovable. That's what my little hate monkey says and it's something that plays out in my head 75% of the time that I'm awake.

Now, I have been using many cognitive behavioral techniques to combat this negative little monkey, and they work, but I'm finding it's not enough. And I wish I could understand why I'm my own worst enemy. I know I've seen some information about depression being linked to leaky gut and brain inflammation. Some thing it's a genetic thing. I've only been doing the Leptin RX and Cold Thermogenesis since February 22nd, I'm hoping it will help.

Could this also be some sort of hormonal issue? When I look for information on depression, I find a lot of CW sorts of things... Mostly they say take anti-depressants. And I don't want to do that for sure.

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-07T09:22:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-12413>

> **August;12481 wrote:** I'm not sure this exactly fits with what everyone else is saying . . but I was thinking today . . what about Seasonal Affective Disorder? if I tend to get SAD in the winter . . even though apparently I've really not been experiencing winters the way I supposed to because I'm living in endless summer . . . what is going to happen when my endless summer . . . ends . . re: SAD?

I've heard of SAD before! When I still was deeply depressed, my last MAJOR depressive episode was a couple of years ago... But it was during mid-summer. The seasons didn't seem to have an uber effect on my mood or anything. I tend to get happier around fall though it's because it's my favorite season with all my favorite holidays... And my birthday.

I've just always been filled with self-doubt, as long as I can remember. When I was under the age of 10, I don't remember being sad a lot or hating myself though... Not till I was around 13, so maybe this could be a tie to my hormones. I know what the monkey says isn't true, not everyone is out to get me and I'm always trying to be the best person I can, but when your own brain is giving you this constant stream of doodoo, it's hard not to sometimes start to believe it, even when outside feedback tells you it's not true.

I've driven the people I've cared about away from me, and I don't want to do that anymore. I haven't actually gotten any labs done on myself since starting the Leptin RX or Cold Thermogenesis... Would getting some labs done maybe give some insight into this nasty monkey? I hope Dr. Kruse will comment, or anyone else who might be dealing with something similar.

### ealachan — 2012-04-07T12:01:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-12419>

> **August;12481 wrote:** I'm not sure this exactly fits with what everyone else is saying . . but I was thinking today . . what about Seasonal Affective Disorder? if I tend to get SAD in the winter . . even though apparently I've really not been experiencing winters the way I supposed to because I'm living in endless summer . . . what is going to happen when my endless summer . . . ends . . re: SAD?

Getting my vitamin D3 levels up has been a god-send for me with regard to my Seasonal Affective Disorder. This winter was the first winter that I was able to get through with absolutely no antidepressants or anti-anxiety meds. I wasn't like Cheery McHappypants every minute of every day (because, ugh, who ever is?)...but compared to past winters where I've basically struggled to be able to get out of bed, struggled to do even the most basic things I needed to do to get by...this past winter is the best I've had in a LONG time. It's also the first winter that I supplemented D3. Certainly doesn't seem like coincidence to me.

### Owl — 2012-04-07T18:28:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-12476>

> **Rhoswen;12480 wrote:**
>
> Could this also be some sort of hormonal issue? When I look for information on depression, I find a lot of CW sorts of things... Mostly they say take anti-depressants. And I don't want to do that for sure.

Definitely have your hormones tested. They have a profound effect on mood. This could definitely be the cause of your depression.

Test for: Progesterone, estrogen, testosterone, DHEA, Vit. D, Free T3, Free T4, Reverse T3, TPO Ab, Antithyroglobulin. Also test B12 and ferritin.

If you can't get it done through insurance, you can order and pay for the tests yourself online.

### quelsen — 2012-04-09T17:06:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-12984>

Rhoswen.

Take it from someone who has walked your path.

Nothing you "know" will correct sugar imbalance in the brain.

Nothing you are taught will overcome hormone imbalances.

NLP, Hypnosis, Spirit Walks, Cognitive Therapy, etc ad nauseam; none of it works in a brain that in under seige.

**Vitamin D is paramount. If you look up the effects of an "overdose" of vitamin d you will easily see why i suggest that there is no such thing as an overdose. I took 100k daily for 3 months then 50K daily for 3 months and that is what it took to get my levels up to 100. as a depressive you will never want your D level below 100.

**DHEA** is a large player in this equation as well. You want high normal lab values and you want them there stat! dont know what they are??? Get them checked. pronto

Finally, Fasting insulin**, needs to be very very close to 0 but at least under 15 . Sorry to say, but for the chronically depressed you must accept that your brain has no fucking clue what to do with glucose. the only way to get rid of that is to go ketogenic, possibly for life, but at least for 6 months. I cannot tell you the results of moving back toward a more balanced macro nutrient ratio as i have not yet increased my weekly average to over 20% carbohydrates.

Only by addressing all three was i able to go entire weeks without unwelcome thoughts of suicide. I have enough will and logic to address these thoughts however i was unable to make them go away... I now no longer believe that they can be made to "go away" in a sugary hormonally challenged environment.

CT will dump toxins into your system and that will flood you with negative chemical emotions ( never forget all emotions are simply neuropeptides which in turn are simply a combination of chemicals mixed just right. ) and you WILL feel any emotion you have stored in your tissue that gets released. Ketogenic adaptation will adjust your o3/o6 ratio so that you can tolerate the CT effect of drastically reordering your cell boundaries.

before i forget. your gut has more nerve endings than your spinal track. Think about that for a second. we easily react to touch sight and sound, but what is going on inside our guts is a volume of data that overwhelms those signals we are most familiar with.

fix your gut fix your mind is not such a far off idea. if you have a leaky gut or if you have had antibiotics without a course of probiotics you are compromised . it can be fixed... see to it.

PM if you want more specifics on my path.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-12T07:38:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-14116>

> **Rhoswen;13353 wrote:** Even if it means having to stay Ketogenic for the rest of my life, I would do anything to not drive the people I love crazy with my own crazy. Going Paleo has helped a lot, I haven't felt suicidally depressed in a year or more, but the unreasonable, illogical self-hatred has not left my mind.
>
>
>
> Dunno if these stats will help but I'm 5'4" and 135 pounds right now. Labs would be best I suppose, but that's all I got right now.
>
>
>
> I know that leaky gut is connected to depression... But I'm actually afraid to take probiotics. Dr. Kruse commented on a thread I made on MDA about how when I took probiotics I got unbelievably terrible carb cravings. I have 0 cravings right now and my carbs are only 11% of my calories.

There are a lot of other ideas for leaky gut fixing besides just probiotics (such as deglycyrrhizinated licorice root (DGL), L- Glutamine (which I had a lot of luck with) and aloe vera. See: [http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/)).

From my experience, there are just as many hidden 'mismatches' in our mental health as in our physical health (if there is actually any difference). Just as many of our behaviours and choices that drive poor health are unconscious to us until we wake up at the foot of our version of Michelangelo's David, the same is true for our internal voice.

I believe that the gut, inflammation level and immune health are key to mental health (and if you're suffering from depression, my bet would be that you've got inflammation - so go for lots of CT, tumeric, resveratrol, Omega 3's/krill oil - as well as the Vitamin D3). I also believe that those early, pre-memory thoughts that were programmed into us drive ill-health and inflammation, too. But they can be accessed and short-circuited or deleted just like our other mismatches.

Just like when you hear 'yourself' screaming for bread or pasta (fill in the blank with your old nemesis comfort food of choice), you use another part of yourself to put that voice in perspective, to say "I know where you come from and I see you for what you are." That is actually parallel to a Buddhist mental health stance that does the same thing with intrusive thoughts that tell us we are bad/stupid/unworthy. You start to see the thought - to realise it's there - and then to accept it (vs try to pretend it isn't there) but then to be able to say 'that isn't me', that is something I was given (like pasta!) that I no longer want. In moving toward it, in facing it, in accepting it but also not believing it, you create a new thought and a new identity and a new strength that is bigger than the old belief. And as you do this, thought by thought, you end up leaving the old way of being in the dust.

It's not quick and easy, but healing isn't. But it is possible. I know, because I've got my own before- and after- photos. You can't see the difference on the outside, but the change is huge.

### PaleoSue — 2012-04-13T16:53:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-14711>

I, too, have struggled with depression, anxiety and social anxiety for my entire life.

Iâ€™ve discovered what I term "natural emotional healing" that has really helped me over the last year and a half, and I feel so lucky to have had the opportunity to learn these techniques. But to be most effective I do think these need to be paired with paleo/leptin RX.

There is something called co-counseling, in which two people take turns listening to the other. There are some very specific techniques used by the â€œcounselorâ€ that anybody can learn. Co-counseling has been around for a few decades, but many people have never heard of it.

I have found co-counseling, rather than professional therapy, to be very effective for me because:

1\. I feel a close friendship with the person I am co-counseling -- it reminds me a little of being part of a tribe. This helps me feel that people like me, that I belong, and that I am worthy of friendship because I am helping my friend by being the counselor as well. You donâ€™t get this in professional therapy because therapists are taught to keep their distance.

2\. Co-counseling has helped teach me what to say in social situations. The techniques we learn in co-counseling include things like not giving advice, listening well, being understanding and paraphrasing what the other person is saying.

3\. Co-counseling has also raised my self-esteem. I am able to more easily see that my own negative thoughts aren't true because the person I am co-counseling has the same thoughts. I can look at her objectively and see that it's not true that "no one likes her" because I think she's a great person. This helps me believe the same about myself.

One other aspect of this natural healing is embracing what is called emotional releases. We are born to deal with all the emotional hurts that life gives us. If you watch young children, they will cry at the top of their lungs, sometimes at what seems to be the most minor emotional hurt. And then after theyâ€™re done crying, if no adults chide them into stopping, they will be fine â€“ happy even. Children will laugh, rage and cry whenever they need to.

But as we grow up, weâ€™re taught that crying is not OK. That women who cry are hysterical, that men who cry are weak, or that we are not in control of our emotions. So we spend all this energy trying NOT to cry, when crying is exactly the thing we have to do.

Not crying is akin to bottling everything up inside, stuffing our hurts down. But if we donâ€™t let them out, then they cause us not only emotional harm, but physical harm as well. (As you know, depression can cause all kinds of physical symptoms.) I try to get a good cry about once a week. It sounds a bit hokey, but it seriously works.

I'd be happy to talk with anyone about co-counseling or these emotional releases. Just PM me.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-14T01:01:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-14914>

From a summary of Nora Gegaudas talk at Paleo FX (and more insight into stress and inflammation and the importance of continuing on this path for our mental health as well as our physical!): (from: [http://roosclues.blogspot.co.nz/2012/03/nora-gedgaudas-on-adrenal-function.html](http://http://roosclues.blogspot.co.nz/2012/03/nora-gedgaudas-on-adrenal-function.html))... (thanks to Jennifer for posting on the Leptin RX thread)

_These notes are for one section of a talk given by Nora Gedgaudas during the Paleo Summit. She was refuting the idea of safe starches, put forward by Paul Jaminet (author of The Perfect Health Diet), and discussing hypoglycemia. She explains that hypoglycemia only occurs in people who are (unnaturally) temporarily adapted to getting glucose from dietary sugars and starches, and that for people who are fat burners- which is what the human body is adapted for- mood and cognitive functioning are not dependent on blood sugar levels. The human body can make all of the glucose it needs in the liver from amino acids, there is no need to get it from the diet.

The exception to this, she explains, is when people have chronically depressed cortisol levels. What really caught my attention is that she says that this low cortisol production is not because the adrenals themselves have been "exhausted" by stress, which she says is an outdated idea. She says that cortisol output is not controlled by the glad itself, but by the brain. It is mitigated by the HPA (Hypothalamus Pituitary Axis), specifically by a group of cells inside the hypothalamus called the Paraventricular Nuclear cells (PVN). The level of cortisol that we produce depends on how these cells are stimulated- whether the balance of neurochemicals leans towards excitatory or inhibitory, but in particular by the presence of inflammatory cytokines. To rephrase that, low cortisol levels are caused by inflammation in the brain. Various stressors on the body result in the release of these cytokines, including chronic infection. This process can also impact our balance of neurotransmitters._

I have been low carb for the past 12 months and saw some improvement with the depression though I kept to the same level of medication(Efexor) just in case.

It has only since starting CT in late Feb that the real improvement has taken off.

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-14T08:26:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15013>

> **Jack Kruse;15005 wrote:** nora G is spot on......but the high carbers hate her message too.

When someone posts in a forum saying that their depression seems to have gotten worse on Paleo, everyone always almost immediately starts wailing, "ZOMG U NOT GETTIN ENUGH CARBS!111111!!11" even when the person comes back and says they're eating near 100 grams of carbs a day. Even before I was doing the Leptin RX, I would scratch my head and wonder why. Everyone seems to agree in the Paleosphere that human beings should be using fat to function, not carbs. So why are some people so insanely angry when people want to eat LOW carb?

The excuse for getting more carbs to relieve depression stems from needing carbs to make serotonin... But from my very novice understanding, I thought that for the brain to make serotonin, it actually needs Tryptophan or 5-HTP and those are amino acids that should be found in proteins, right? Then why would someone need to up there carbs for that?

@PaleoSue - I've never heard of that type of therapy but it sounds pretty nice. I do realize I kinda bottle up my feelings, because I never want to upset other people BECAUSE I'm afraid they'll abandon me. LOL It triggers a cycle of me losing my mind, and that's not really appealing to people so what happens? They decide they can't be my friend anymore. So I end up triggering the exact thing I didn't want to do because I bottled my feelings up.

I must admit, this is the first time I've really acknowledged and recognized these patterns of behavior in myself. A few years ago, I was stumped as to what I was doing wrong. I couldn't see through that doom and gloom fog of depression what I was doing to my friends.

But even though I recognize this behavior I'm still having a hard time stopping myself, haha!

@August - Social Phobias - I think bad social experiences contribute to this... with people who's brain chemistry is already messed up. I know many people who have been bullied or harassed, far worse than anything I had experienced, but they still came out of it better, happy people. Why would this be? It makes sense to me that it's because of brain chemistry. Just like how some people can seem very healthy following a CW diet, even know we all know it's not healthy.

Feeling unlovable - I could tell you that for me, I know that voice in my brain that says that is not true. If it were, I'm sure most of my friends and family would have totally abandoned me at this point. I have a guy who tells me how much he adores me, if I was unlovable how could I attract anyone at all? I'm sure you have people like that in your life too. I found that even when I had people telling me they loved me and praising me, that evil voice would come along and say, "Har har, they're lying to you."

But you gotta challenge that voice, back it into a corner, and you'll see that the voice is full of bull crap. I always ask that voice, "Why would they lie to me? Give me a logical reason why."

I just keep asking it why and almost always, the voice can't give a good reason why. I do this in my head now, but I had to start out on paper. This was part of the cognitive behavioral therapy I did.

The reason is advice on some of "the" cliquish paleo sites owned by clinicians. It gets propagated all over the paleosphere and then it becomes paleo dogma. They never get called on it because they are all well liked and it hurts people. Kinda like depression drugs do but no one wants to talk about that nightmare either........it amazes me how they get a pass. I have heard both say many many time moderation is the key.......well guess what? Moderation leads to mediocrity and mediocrity is not how Mother Nature responds in nature in case anyone is watching her........I am. When I here the moderation line it is A CW wisdom response in disguise.......it is a modern healthcare facade and in my view is more dangerous to a modern hominid. I think you the public have to view this viewpoint and decide what is best for you. Don't rely on us to make the decisions. Rely on us to see how we think about thinks and then apply those things to yourself and test them for yourself and then adjust your life. I already know what a life of mediocre choices makes for a human. Apparently many do not. I want you to begin to think about reading between the lines about what many of us in the paleosphere really are advocating and why. It appears that many are afraid of creating waves in their local areas for fear of stepping out of line. If you live paleo and don't advocate it in your practice than you are nothing but a mirage of nonsense to me. Practice what you preach and dont worry about CYA from the lawyers and your clueless co workers. It appears your social standing matters more than the substance your person. Have the courage of one to stand up for what is correct. People will not follow you or seek you out if you are soft on your own beliefs.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-14T16:27:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15049>

IMHO, you need both - brain support and a way to explore new ways of viewing the world and reacting to the world. Here is a great podcast on brain drain. Dr Kalish talks about the effect of SSRIs on the brain. He also recommends the 5 HTP and L Tyrosine, always be taken together so there is a balance of serotonin and dopamine. Both my mom and I started taking them and feel better - more energy and more focus. I think its helpful to feel better but view it as a band-aide to get me started. Leptin Rx and CT are going to the root and bringing healing to the cause. Its easier to keep going on Leptin RX and CT when I feel good!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-14T23:40:29+0300

+1

Bad therapy/support is like bad diet. Just because there are bad diets, doesn't mean there aren't good ones. And even if you eat a good diet, there is more to health than that (CT, circadian cycles, etc). If 'one thought can change your life' then clearly there are multiple, mutually-reinforcing avenues towards alleviating depression and other problems. For many people, one avenue can make major change, but for many others (like me) it took a combination of right diet, right thinking and right relationships. Optimal for that polar bear meant taking her cub! That's what this forum is about.

### Guest — 2012-04-15T14:31:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15274>

Accepting my state of mind, at any given time, has made the most significant change in me. While I have battled a life time of depression acceptance is the key factor for me. Not accepting it like fate, but accepting what it is until it changes because of the steps I am taking. I understand it's not gonna go away because of some magical supplement or allopathic drug or even homeopathy, so I accept and it works for me. I believe this acceptance somehow has allowed me to move on and concentrate on other things such as the diet, especially the Leptin RX and CT at this stage of the game. I have to say acceptance makes me very teachable and what has helped me allow someone like Dr K into my space and give him and what he knows my full attention.

When I want to feel sorry for myself and think about how alone I feel because of years of craziness, and pushing others away, I always have that to rely on to keep me stuck. However, if I do this, mark my word, I will sabotage myself and blame others for not making it work for me. But, I haven't been there in quite a long time, thank goodness. I decided some years ago to take responsibility for how I feel even when much of this is beyond my control. I never gave up and have always known things will get better.

I am teachable because my mind is freer from worry over things I cannot change at any given moment. I don't have the poor me's and I have forgiven everyone in my past who I felt harmed me, especially myself. No one harmed me like I did.

I am taking baby steps into these protocols, but I feel hope and can see a change, no matter how minute it may be. Most of all, I am learning and can see past what is in front of me. I can lay bets that at one time Dr K was quite a different person, probably someone I would have loathed for his self imporance and selfishness. His soul is healther because of what he has done with the diet and CT and he has allowed himself to feel empathy, once again, for his fellow human being. In other words, he tapped into that ancient pathway, that has allowed it to happen. This is a man who's REAL character has been allowed to shine through despite what he has been taught through conventional wisdom. A real pioneer with a conscience to boot!!!. I couldn't admire anyone more for having the fortitude to know he himself needed fixing first and taking on the task to help others do the same for themselves.

This has been my experience, but feel everyone is different and what has helped me may not help others. Hope i am making sense and haven't offended anyone here. it's the last thing I would want to do.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-15T20:15:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15347>

> **Jack Kruse;15347 wrote:** Well then CT 10 is going to be for you........just wait til you read it. And the video at the end should open your eyes too........we have many prominent Paleo MD's advocating for moderation. I will tell you what moderation advocation is a symbol of. Then you can decide.

Greatly looking forward to it. My mother had low serotonin, which she dosed with small bits of sweet treats. Result: we grew up around a depressed mother, which is the pits, and I have a mouthful of metal from having sweets around at all times. And mild depression.

I'm interested in Quelsen's recommendation of a good high dose of D3. I'm theorizing that it might have a link to the tryptophan-serotonin question, because of something I think I picked up from the Jaminets.

If one has a chronic infection, one of the ways the body fights it is to keep the "terroir" lacking in niacin, which is supposedly a limiting factor for bacterial or viral growth. So, if one meets, for instance, grouchy grumpy old men, it might be from their being chronically low in niacin because of a high infection load. Niacin is needed for serotonin production. Taking niacin or niacinamide might temporarily make one feel better, but perhaps the chronic infection might worsen?

D3 -- overcome some chronic infection (might a low dose of iodine help with that as well?) -- gradually the body feels less need for restricting niacin -- serotonin gets back to normal?

Is this off-base, doc? Or might it be a factor?

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-16T22:35:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15690>

I'm late to the party, but wanted to share my experience, as little as it is, because I've seen bits of both sides of mental health. I'm actually fascinated by a lot of it, even without much suffering from it myself, because most people with the health problems I have (especially sugar addiction & CFS) have longstanding battles with mental health.

So I never had any problems with eating disorders and NOTHING ever resembling depression, not even for a moment, through any of my childhood or teenage years. Also, no troubled childhood whatsoever. Starting developing binge eating disorder with antibiotic abuse in my 20s (y'all know the rest of that story). Had about 24hrs of what I felt was vvery probably what people describe as depression after birth of second child, then it disappeared. Because of that, I knew it was chemical.

Had 2hrs of feeling the worst, mentally, I had ever had in my life, the week I stopped eating potatoes and dairy for the first time ever. Brain wouldn't stop, wouldn't sit on a thought for more than half a second, I felt hopeless and sad and miserable and couldn't even get out a single "Hail Mary" despite trying for hours. In the morning, it was gone and I felt great. I knew it was chemical because of how it went down, but I also really had it hit home that some people struggle though feelings like those for years, or their whole lives, and think it's because they're "screwed up," "bad," or "crazy." It made me so very sad to realize that, b/c it's obviously chemical when you go into and out of it like I did.

I've had very brief bouts of it a couple times since, obvious at the time that it was die-off from somethign crappy leaving my body.

I now believe depression, bipolar, and all their ilk (including all eating disorders) shouldn't be considered "mental health" to be treated with talk therapy, but physical health just like a bad back or IBS. In fact, very often, the root cause is digestive (see GAPS diet & Dr. Natasha Campbell-McBride). To get back to the topic of the original post in this thead, paleo diet + working on rebuilding gut health through healing foods and supplements can be life-changing.

> **Jennifer;15118 wrote:** IMHO, you need both - brain support and a way to explore new ways of viewing the world and reacting to the world.

I agree with this, too, and here's part of why - in battling my ED, I always tried to do it purely with behavioral techniques (like "no snacking" or "no eating while watching TV" or "take small bites and chew slowly" or "if you think you're hungry, drink water"). No luck. But once I started to have the underlying physical problems turn around, I could use those same techniques, successfully, that had always failed me. Without them, I seriously doubt I would have progressed as I did. It took both the behavioral & physical (dietary) changes to help me win.

Probably part of it is that people as caught in a troubled behavioral pattern as I was have either never known, or lost, the good habits that come naturally to others.

It seems to me that a lot of that would apply to non-ED mental health issues, as well. "Count your blessings," "think of others less fortunate," "count to 10," blah blah, are well-intentioned words often used for those with poorly functioning mental health, but I imagine they're not very helpful \*until\* the physical root causes are reversed. I imagine that then those very words can play a very helpful role, just as they do for those of us without those specific mental health issues.

### Guest — 2012-04-16T22:56:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15703>

Mamagrok, I took a couple of tests for mental illness to see what category they wanted to put me in, and I noticed they had several questions that asked about having stomach problems. I can't remember how they were worded because it was years ago. So conventional medicine links stomach ailments to mental disorders but that is as far as it goes for them. It just shows me how much they don't want us well. grrrrrrrr!!!!!!

I have believed for years that if your stomach is not working proberly then it is not sending the proper signals to the brain. And your right about not being addressed with talk therapy...just made it worse for me. Thank goodness I only took psych meds for about 6 months--it did it's damage still in those few short months.

If just healing my brain comes out of this diet and CT and nothing else I will be grateful.

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-17T04:28:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15804>

> **Jack Kruse;15684 wrote:** i'll be very interested to hear the comments on CT 10 for sure........

I'm definitely looking forward to it!

@MamaGrok - Love your post and I was agreeing with a lot that you say. My dad would say that "depression" didn't exist and that I was just lazy. I started to think that I was just a terrible person who deserved to be depressed. Unfortunately, my dad's attitude was a big factor in not seeking ANY treatment for my depression. I only started to get treatment when I was 26... And now I'm 27!

But I never realized the way hormones and gut flora can effect your brain till I discovered Paleo. There's so much great info out there and so many people who are suffering like I am aren't receiving it.

I read an article about the gut/brain/skin axis. I didn't understand it all but the gist was something like acne, poor gut flora, and depression were linked up together. When the doctors would feed patients lacto-fermented foods, not only did it help their acne, but it lifted their depression. I think they also mentioned supplementing with fish/fish oils. This is something so simple that a patient could do, and yet they're more likely to be put on anti-depressants for their depression and anti-biotics for the acne! Argh!

I'm glad your mental health experiences with depression weren't long term but I'm also glad the experience helps you have empathy for people like us.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-17T05:14:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15811>

> **Suhenna;15773 wrote:** It just shows me how much they don't want us well. grrrrrrrr!!!!!!

Perhaps I shouldn't admit this, but I am a psychotherapist. I have worked for 15 years in the field of mental health, mostly with mothers and children. I really believe that the field of mental health is like that of physical health. It is flooded with CW professionals, but there are others who see the light in various ways and who are there working for change. Additionally, I have rarely come into contact with a mental health professional who I believed wanted to keep people sick. There is SO much ignorance out there. And bear in mind, the only mental health professionals with any medical/physiology training are psychiatrists, yet 99% of those who offer therapy are psychologists, psychotherapists, counsellors, social workers, etc.

Just as with our medical docs, we need to educate our mental health professionals too. I have come to believe that diet/physical health (and all the things Dr K addresses) underpin mental health and that you can't differentiate between someone's brain, thoughts, feelings, body, etc. You must treat the WHOLE person.

I also believe there are many roads that are complementary. If someone gets good therapy (which is about rewiring the brain, often targeted at the HPA access and early mismatches), this accelerates reduction in inflammation and other benefits. I happen to agree that if the diet is way off or the gut is leaky all the good therapy in the world will never get you where you want to be. Conversely, I have also known people who heal their gut and eat a squeaky clean paleo diet and still struggle emotionally. Emotional health is anchored in relationships, and while getting physical health right will provide the capacity for healing, sometimes many people need additional support to rewire their relational networks, too.

And just to address the points MamaGrok made, I agree that these problems need to have their root physical causes attended to. However, that isn't always the total cure (though YES definitely needs to be the first thought and action). And good therapy to me is not strictly behavioural. People are much more than their behaviour. Which is why a relational approach is needed for many. You may be pleased to know that many many in the field of neuroscience and attachment theory (psychoneurobiology) talk openly about how the gut is involved in mental health and the centrality of inflammation.

Last point, never in my years of practice have I ever told anyone to 'count their blessings' or 'count to 10.' Good emotional support is about walking in someone else's shoes, not giving patronising advice!

### Guest — 2012-04-17T14:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15852>

KiwiLauren,

I doubt you and I will ever agree on this because you are part of the system. I see it from a perspective of one who was diagnosed with life long clinical depresson and put on disability. When I said they wanted to keep us sick, I meant the MD's (Psychiatrists) and pharmceutical companies. They know the links between the gut and the mind, yet have never addressed it to my knowledge.

I am my own therapist and a darn good one too, since I rarely suffer from depression long term anymore. Though I must admit, I have the best homeopathist in the world from Norway that has helped me along a great deal with the the one remedy only, wait and see approach (classical homeopaty). I have fought long and hard and it wasn't easy, but I never gave up. I don't know anyone who has fought as hard as myself to overcome the worst sort of depression one could suffer. I just never gave in to allopathic treaments which would have made my journey far more complex and almost impossible to bring about a cure--yes, i am looking for a cure. I doubt there is anyone out there that has used Dr K's protocols, long term, to address their mental health but if there is I would love to hear from them. A little suffering for long term benefits is okay by me.

Oh and one hour of emotional support a week only made me want to commit suicide for the most part. I can get rather cockey and arrogant over this because I did try a few therapists along my journey. Two of them I actually feel like I turned into their therapist (I know, crazy but true). The others who left me devastated because of their one hour sessions of wanting to know my life story can go to hell and rot. Finally, I realized I needed to help myself and I have been on that path ever since and never looked back.

I am not saying my way works for everyone but I will never be convinced conventional drugs, therapy work for anyone. I have never witnessed in all my 57 years with anyone. Most only got worse.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-17T16:58:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-15882>

> **KiwiLauren;15881 wrote: And good therapy to me is not strictly behavioural. People are much more than their behaviour. Which is why a relational approach is needed for many.
>
>
> Thank you; mea culpa, as I was quickly looking for a word to encapsulate \*all\* non-physical approaches to mental health, and grabbed one that wasn't at all broad enough. Behavioral is definitely only a part of it.
>
>
>
>
> > You may be pleased to know that many many in the field of neuroscience and attachment theory (psychoneurobiology) talk openly about how the gut is involved in mental health and the centrality of inflammation.
>
>
>
> That's shocking and appalling. To me, to know that it's not "just in your head," and to treat it like it is, anyway, is unconscionable. Or even worse, IF this is true about Dr. Deans, someone who knows it's physiological at its root, _and knows which diet will help,_ yet refuses to use that with her patients, that's the bottom of the barrel. That's treachery. I hold those who know it's not all in the head, but medicate, in higher esteem than those who know it's not all in the head, \*and\* know how root cause treatment is better than meds, but medicate anyway, without first trying all in the root cause path. With knowledge comes responsibility.
>
>
>
> never in my years of practice have I ever told anyone to 'count their blessings' or 'count to 10.' Good emotional support is about walking in someone else's shoes, not giving patronising advice!**

**

Absolutely - I was thinking more of the advice from well-meaning friends & family, similar to the advice from the same for BED or sugar addiction.

Suhenna - I don't see any difference at all in the mental health & physical health professions, here. For both

\- 99% go into the field to help people

\- 98% get distracted from that because the treatments used don't work, the drug companies buy their souls, and the only way they see forward is to medicate symptoms or keep preaching the same lifestyle prescriptions that don't work (like my PCP who, despite hearing me say that I had done serious cardio for YEARS and no longer had the energy to walk to the mailbox, said he'd rather see me spend $300 on running shoes than on a lab test).

\- Only the smallest fraction will ever learn how to apply the collective wisdom of the past to the technological advances of the present and become useful providers. Dr. Kruse is one of these in the physical health field. I have no reason to believe someone like KiwiLauren can't become one in the mental health field (again, I should state that I do NOT believe mental health is separate from physical health).

\- IMO, someone dispensing the \*right\* lifestyle advice in the physical field would be the same as someone dispensing the \*right\* behavioral/emotional/relational/etc. advice in the "mental health" field - rare** and supremely helpful.

My experience with doctors of the body is the same as yours with doctors who are supposed to treat the mind - NOT ONE of them has ever solved any problem I have had, and many made them much worse, or would have if I had given them the chance. I started thoughtfully leaving behind their advice years ago,and now no longer go to any of them at all. But if I can find someone like Dr. Kruse, then I will reconsider my position.

### PaulaRichards — 2012-04-17T18:06:48+0300

Oh my goodness!! I couldn't agree more strongly with what MamaGrok has just said. I think in order for things to change in any measureable way the whole system needs to change. ### Rhoswen — 2012-04-18T07:50:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-16159>

Oh yes, bad therapy is probably the WORST thing that can happen for someone who's already suffering from a mental illness. Some of the stories I read were very disturbing, especially the stories of the therapists engaging in romantic relationships with their clients. What the hell could they be thinking? :/

> IF this is true about Dr. Deans, someone who knows it's physiological at its root, and knows which diet will help, yet refuses to use that with her patients, that's the bottom of the barrel. That's treachery.

That's a very good point.

Can't she just ask her patients to try for 30 days? I'm sure there would be plenty of them who would be willing to try something new for 30 days if it meant they'd feel better.

Seems like she's scared she'll be thought of as a quack instead of wanting her patients to get better. In my opinion, as a doctor you can't afford to act that way. I understand that no one wants to be ostracized by their peers or worse, lose their patients trust. However, I think their responsibility as a doctor out weighs that. If one can't accept that possibility, then maybe they shouldn't have become a doctor in the first place.

In the end, when these doctors are more concerned for their own status than assisting their patients, they do harm to ALL of us.

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-20T04:38:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-16893>

> **agatha;16799 wrote:** I have suggested CT to a few of my patients with insomnia (I'm a psychologist) - as an experiment. Most of them have firmly declined!

I find that pretty bizarre. I know if a psychologist had told me that cold therapy might relieve my depression, even if only a little bit, as an alternative to pills, I would have jumped all over that.

Although... I think CT might be difficult for someone who's already miserable. The first few minutes I do CT are usually pretty uncomfortable. Throwing someone with depression into something like that may be hard. I know when I was depressed I didn't care enough to help myself, even with normal CW approaches of treatment. It had to get to the point of where I almost killed myself to finally decide to help myself.

### chocolate — 2012-04-20T06:55:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-16919>

I think I would mention that even if they are uncomfortable, at least they would feel different...uncomfortable doesn't mean feeling worse, and gradual is good. Even if you do warm/ cold/ warm, its a different sensation. The cold water is such a reboot though...

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-20T07:58:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-16926>

> **chocolate;16989 wrote:** I think I would mention that even if they are uncomfortable, at least they would feel different...uncomfortable doesn't mean feeling worse, and gradual is good. Even if you do warm/ cold/ warm, its a different sensation. The cold water is such a reboot though...

Yes but someone who is depressed doesn't see things that way. "Oh it'll make me uncomfortable, but the feeling might be different? HA! Yeah right, you're full of \*\*\*\* and nothing will ever make me feel better no matter what anyone says. They're all full of \*\*\*\* and I hate \*\*\*\*ing all of them."

That's the mindset of someone who's deeply depressed. It's pretty difficult to find hope when you're like that. I was like that in my early twenties, I just didn't care about getting help or getting healthy. That's when I gained a lot of weight. I didn't care what I ate, didn't care to feel better, didn't want to do a thing with my life other than find a suitable way to end it. I might be an extreme example, I've never really truly talked with anyone else about this stuff.

So treating someone who's seriously depressed with even just a Paleo-style diet might be difficult, unless they're already on one. And I think a Paleo Diet will have helped enough with their symptoms to get them to a point of convincing them to try CT. It's just finding a way to get them outta that negative thinking to get them to try something different.

I think that if a doctor was able to gain enough trust with the patient they could do it though.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-21T00:57:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-17242>

Okay. I'm going to give a warning and that is that I've barely read anything on these 9 pages... so sorry if this is going to be not very helpful. Just sharing my little tidbit...

To the people who are dealing with depression... do you know your o3/6 ratio and vitamin D level. That was my door to freedom. It didn't fix everything at once but it sure got things moving in the right direction.

I'm more than surprised that I'm plain not into this thread after dealing with mental illness for more than 20 years  You can recover from this. Entirely. I have never been as happy and motivated as the last few months. Paleo did a lot... Leptin Rx, AI Rx, and CT has closed that chapter in my life. I am starting to feel like a new person  For some reason my recent head injury has catapulted me to feeling even better... or is it the extreme CT...

**You need to eat the best food you can find.** And you will get better. This had very little to do with "mental" for me... and a whole lot with "physical". The mental part was what was caused by the physical part.

I'm starting to believe that depression is more often than not, a purely neolithic disease.

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-21T09:47:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-17342>

Oh! Here's the article I was reading about the Brain-Gut-Skin Axis with acne's connections to depression![Acne vulgaris, probiotics and the gut-brain-skin axis - back to the future?](http://http://www.gutpathogens.com/content/3/1/1)

_"As mentioned, there had been older commentaries and clinical anecdotes suggesting that orally consumed lactic acid bacteria might be of benefit in alleviating depressive symptoms. It was also reported that patients with mental health disorders appeared to have very low levels of L. acidophilus. In a series of case reports in 1924, one Illinois physician reported value of oral L. acidophilus for the treatment of both acne and mental health disorders; in addition to L. acidophilus improving the complexion, it was stated that 'in certain patients it even seemingly contributes to mental improvement'."

"The first formal investigation of the potential psychological benefits of probiotic supplementation in humans involved 132 otherwise healthy adults; those who had more depressive sympotoms at baseline had significant improvement in mood scores after taking a probiotic Lactobacillus casei fermented beverage compared to the placebo group. A separate placebo-controlled study involved 39 chronic fatigue syndrome patients who were administered the same oral Lactobacillus casei probiotic vs. placebo. At the conclusion of the 8-week study, depression scores remained unchanged between the groups, however there were significant improvements in anxiety as measured via the Beck Anxiety Inventory vs. placebo."_

Probiotics for acne AND depression... Killing two birds with one stone. But what kinda makes me ANGRY is more than likely, you're gonna be prescribed an ANTI-BIOTIC for your acne instead in todays world!

My mom wanted me to use **Accutane for my acne. Glad I decided not to touch that stuff. Even when I was still into CW sorts of things the side effects of Accutane** scared the snot outta me:

Depression

Violent behavior/aggression

Psychosis (seeing or hearing things that are not real)

Suicidal ideation

Suicide attempts

Hearing impairment

Loss in bone mass and strength/weak and fragile bones

Liver damage

Inflammation and damage to blood vessels

Hair-thinning/hair-loss/baldness

Fragile and weaker skin

Vision problems

Stroke

Seizures

Dear god, no thank you!!!

I don't recommend you do this with cod liver oil (especially not the fermented one) but I was taking multiple times that amount of omega 3. I tend to dose myself by intuition and that was 1 tablespoon of double strength fish oil. It turned out to be 9 grams of omega 3 a day. I don't know much about the fermented CLO - I don't need it anymore. Twin Lab is a good brand if you want to try a different one (WAPF approved).

4,000 IU of D is a maintenance dose for most people. For some, not even that. I love Phosphatidylserine for the calming effect. And it's so good for your brain.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-23T05:43:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/an-unhealthy-mind.713/post-17979>

> **Rhoswen;17727 wrote:** People keep saying that FCLO is the best but I've heard some strange side effects from it... Stomach problems and acne being among them and that because the oil is fermented, it's bad because it's oxidized fish oils. don't totally understand that all.

Because the oil is fermented, it's made like all fish oil has been made for all time. Processed fish oil from a factory where it's molecularly distilled and whatnot is rancid. fCLO is not.

If it causes acne, it's because it's detoxing. I haven't heard of stomach problems. You're only supposed to take from 1/4tsp to 1tsp/day.

I'd probably use fish oil if I was taking a lot, because fCLO is naturally very rich in vitamins A & D. Nordic Naturals' CLO that has \*NO VITAMINS ADDED\* can be used for the same thing. Be very careful - do NOT take one that has vitamin A & D added back, because they are adding back SYNTHETIC vitamins A & D, which are very toxic in not-so-high doses. (They are the reason vitamin A has a bad rep for birth defects, etc.) That includes almost every "natural" brand out there - they all get their oil from the same sources (huge vats of oil in Norway), all are processed the same way (including Nordic Naturals), and all (except for the one variety of NN) have the synthetic vitamins added back. There are no longer any companies adding natural A & D back, and NN is the only company offering the processed factory oil without any A & D added back (it is completely lost in the distillation process).

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-23T15:21:18+0300

Totally non-linear. I just had a few hours of slipping back into feeling overwhelmed & irritable, with leaden arms again, on Saturday. Best I can figure, it was die-off, exactly one week after accidentally being glutened. I had gotten crazy bloated right afterward, and that went down gradually all week, but suddenly Fri evening I got a little worse, then Sat morning a lot worse. But by Sat evening, I was feeling great again.

It comes and it goes, but always, I'm better overall, significantly, than I was six months earlier.

i don't know much about using fish oils for mental health (except that they WORK), but my understanding is that a good quality fish oil, or that NN CLO (or any other than processes out, then doesn't replace, the fat-soluble vitamins - I don't know of any other, but it's possible someone else, like Twin Labs, is now doing it), would be a good choice if you wanted to use a \*lot\*. Also, maybe the salmon oil that Dexter (on this forum) was using in large quantities? Or krill oil?

> **Rhoswen;19606 wrote:** Yep, I don't want to throw any of my hormones off or anything like that. No need to make things worse, haha.

You do need to get tested. I was supplementing with 25 mg pregnenolone per day but my test result came back at 7 ng/ml (7!). Optimal range (according to LEF) is 130 to 180 ng/ml. So I have doubled my pregnenolone to 50 mg per day and added 25 mg of DHEA and BHRT with tri-est, progesterone and testosterone (all of which were rock bottom too). I feel much better and will get more tests soon to see how my levels have changed with adequate supplementation. You need a provider who understands how to do this...

# Thread: After 5 weeks I burned myself!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-5-weeks-i-burned-myself.715/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vwaggs — 2012-04-07T15:27:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-5-weeks-i-burned-myself.715/post-12427>

I have been spot icing and ice bathing for 5 weeks now and yesterday when I took off the ice, I had a huge welt/burn from the ice brick. The SAME ice brick I always use and have actually kept on longer before. My other leg is completely fine. I thought I was past having something like this happen after 5 weeks. What gives?

### LinD — 2012-04-07T16:52:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-5-weeks-i-burned-myself.715/post-12449>

That IS odd vwaggs. I am curious to see the answer.

### vwaggs — 2012-04-08T01:35:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-5-weeks-i-burned-myself.715/post-12548>

No suggestions?? I guess I am the odd one then! Hurts really bad today with a bruised feeling and is hot to the touch.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-08T05:38:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-5-weeks-i-burned-myself.715/post-12584>

No, you're not the only one. I'm 4 weeks in and just did this to myself too! My stomach feels really bruised and sensitive. The ice pack that did it was two days ago, but the tenderness didn't show up until today. Just like you, it is the same pack I always use and wasn't for a longer time. I only had this happen once before when I first started, funny how it showed up again!

### vwaggs — 2012-04-08T16:36:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-5-weeks-i-burned-myself.715/post-12656>

I am glad I am not the only one but sorry you are hurting! My spot instantly became hot and raised. Today is looks better but left a little bruise and is still sore. Wonder why this would happen.

### LinD — 2012-04-08T16:43:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-5-weeks-i-burned-myself.715/post-12658>

Two steps forward, one step back?

Feeling confident you will get better and better.

Today...after I endured 2 5 gallon buckets of ice in my 30 minute bath I got out and did 5 30 second face dunks...My face emerged lobster red...and I am lit..Has anyone done this?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/face-dunking-after-ct-bath.716/post-12457>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uzzi-reiss-books-natural-hormone-balance-for-women.719/post-12465>

Just read it last night and took lots of notes. I see he has a more recent book out, too. Is it worth it or is the original all I need? Your 2 cents appreciated.

### Lyndra — 2012-04-07T20:04:51+0300

# Thread: puzzled friends
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### agatha — 2012-04-07T19:47:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-12493>

Ha Ha! I saw some friends today who commented that I am looking slimmer and healthier than last year and asking me how I did it. I replied 'The key to weight loss is to eat more fried eggs and bacon' There was a puzzled silence and then some nervous laughter...

Has anyone else enjoyed reactions from friends/family - do share...

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-07T21:00:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-12504>

My husband made a comments this morning about breakfast he got a chorizo-egg burrito at the farmers market, and said he left over half for me and that I could pull out the insides ( I am allergic to wheat) and eat them AND there was plenty there to eat - a reference to my BABs. lol The burrito was good - mostly chorizo, although it have a bit of cheese on it. I know it wasn't paleo, but it was low carb.

And he is always making comments when I go get butter and put it on the vegetables he steamed for us. Actually the amount of butter I put on the vegetables.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-07T21:02:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-12506>

My hubby is currently lepin Rx'ing and he and a friend have a weekly check-in for weight loss and work-related goals (they're both self-employed creatives).

Hubby has been steadily losing (he's 3 weeks in) and is now looking noticeably thinner and his friend, who hangs out with a lot of yoga and spinning people and, I'm guessing, is eating like they tend to eat, isn't losing much and is puzzled at how my hubby is eating so much and isn't working out at all (other than walking) and the weight is just coming off

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-14T20:43:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-15107>

Yep they see me losing and I tell 'em no grains no dairy no legumes no sugar.... They ask what do I eat and I tell them I eat 4 eggs 5 slices of bacon most mornings..... They can't believe I'm losing weight!

I used to have a heater under my desk since the office is so cold. Now I don't even bring a jacket when they are all bundled up indoors. I just tell them I love the cold now...

I swear my next car will be a convertible so i can enjoy cold + vitamin D (depending on the season)..... I'll call it the CT-mobile...imagine the things they'll think driving with top down when it's cold out...! (added bonus to CT)!

### Jim — 2012-04-14T20:51:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-15109>

Ha ha. Everyone thinks I'm crazy driving to work in 30 degree weather with the AC going full blast while wearing a short sleeved shirt.

### chocolate — 2012-04-14T21:39:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-15116>

I tell people that I just fat, fish, liver, nuts, and meat. I don't present fat, but my metabolics could be better and are getting that way. I'm 5'8" and wear a three. I'm not getting the optimal meats, but I'm a little challenged sometimes. I am waiting for the nut consumption to bite me, but until i feel "off", that's my traveling fat. If someone tells me I'm wrong, well.... it's none of my business what they think of me. I have a mirror and I don't think its broken.

### agatha — 2012-04-15T19:56:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-15343>

Ha ha. I love telling people about the BAB - they can't believe their ears!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-04-15T20:28:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-15353>

People come to my house now just to see what I am cooking.....They just look more shocked when they see how much I eat....and then I let them watch me pack myself in ice.

### ealachan — 2012-04-15T20:36:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-15355>

I posted the other day on Facebook about my CT session, and I had four people reply almost immediately saying "Why would you do something like that to yourself?!" I told them that I do it because it makes me feel good, because it will help me live longer, and because I'm a little bit of a sicko.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-15T20:59:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-15358>

CT'd in front of my husband last night and he "officially" thinks I have lost it now! hahahahaha!!

### PaleoSue — 2012-04-15T21:18:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/puzzled-friends.720/post-15366>

A couple of co-workers have commented on my not wearing a jacket and wearing short sleeves in our notoriously cold office. But I no longer find it cold.
