# Thread: Boredom
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ginastarke — 2012-04-07T21:46:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12513>

Hi all,

I'm trying to be as close to zero carb as I can, with a few carbs from vegetables, unsweetened chocolate, eggs, and coconut manna included for sanity's sake.

I'm also on a very tight budget so that unfortunately means cafo meat, with as much frozen wild caught fish and local grass-fed "variety" cuts as I can afford.

With nightshades banned, much of the spices I cook with are also out - paprika, pepper, and the varieties of Tabasco sauce that I thought I couldn't live without. So almost everything- beef, chicken, pork, fish all winds up with garlic salt on it - any other ideas for seasonings? I read a lot hear about turmeric- what meats is it good on?

I understand Guynet's views on reward food - that if the food had too much flavor, we'll eat more than we need to, but I think the boredom is causing me to eat less than I should, leading to a frustrating stall.I'm pretty sure I'm in ketosis because even when I get home hungry, I eat much less than I plan on, especially since I'm only eating my BAB and dinner with nothing in between. - any ideas?

### ealachan — 2012-04-07T21:50:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12516>

Turmeric can kind of be an acquired taste, I think. If you've never cooked with it before, you might want to taste it first.

I mainly use turmeric in my curries because I'm not fond of the taste of it by itself, but my dad loves turmeric mixed into sauteed veggie mixtures (his favorite is kale, onion, carrot, broccoli, garlic and turmeric).

### Croak — 2012-04-07T21:58:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12517>

I use tumeric on just about everything these days, eggs, meat, and vegetables. It's more of a "savory enhancer" than an outright flavor, it's like powdered soy sauce in that regard, and it's very good for you. The only problem I have with it is that it's bad for "finger food", because of the stains on my fingers that make it look like I've just smoked a carton of Lucky unfiltered.

You can also try coconut aminos, or Braggs Liquid Aminos (lot of hate for that in some circles, based on what it's made from and how it is made), liquid smoke, butter/coconut oil basting, bacon grease, and just about anything else from the spice section that doesn't have sugar, wheat, or nightshades in it. Experiment!

And Gina, nightshades _might need to be avoided, it is not set in stone that everybody_ has to avoid them. And finally, and I'll make this easy to read:

BLACK PEPPER IS NOT A NIGHTSHADE!

### ginastarke — 2012-04-07T22:06:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12519>

> **Croak;12586 wrote:** I use tumeric on just about everything these days, eggs, meat, and vegetables. It's more of a "savory enhancer" than an outright flavor, it's like powdered soy sauce in that regard, and it's very good for you. The only problem I have with it is that it's bad for "finger food", because of the stains on my fingers that make it look like I've just smoked a carton of Lucky unfiltered.
>
>
>
> And Gina, nightshades _might need to be avoided, it is not set in stone that everybody_ has to avoid them. And finally, and I'll make this easy to read:
>
>
>
> BLACK PEPPER IS NOT A NIGHTSHADE!

Thanks! I'm avoiding the night shade vegetables until I get a new cholesterol test because I can't remember what my ldl was last time around. I had a feeling that pepper and capers were ok , but I wanted to be sure.

### agatha — 2012-04-07T22:41:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12526>

Hi Gina

Yes, I find meat and fish a tad boring too. Mint, lemon juice, cumin, thyme, oregano, basil, rosemary, sage, thai fish sauce, ground coriander, cloves, cinnamon, ginger, mace, galangal, fresh coriander, chives, black pepper, sechuan pepper, dill, tarragon - these are what I use to make things interesting. yum.

### ginastarke — 2012-04-07T22:56:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12530>

lemon juice is OK? I though all fruit was out, It would sure be nice to have lemon or lime for my fish!

Well I've had a long ride on my bicycle to think it out. Sorry about the whining, the nightshades are are a part of the cuisine I grew up with. I'm going to do some research into cooking before they became commonplace, or into cuisines like French/German that don't use as them much. Hopefully I'll be posting some recipes soon!

### Guest — 2012-04-08T13:09:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12619>

@ Croak --I stay away from black pepper because of the mold. I even think that the peppercorns I grind are moldy. I miss it but it still makes me sneeze in either form so I will continue not to use it.

### David — 2012-04-08T13:22:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12623>

Many moons ago I had occasion to be learning proper laboratory techniques in my third-year microbiology class that required the generation of Petri dish streak plates on growth media. The sources were left to the student, so I chose various sources from my daily experience, among which were such things as a swab from my dog’s mouth, after one of his self-satisfying butt-licks (his butt, not mine) and black pepper from a student union cafeteria shaker. To my surprise, after incubation, the plate with the pepper streak had generated many times the biotic growth than any other sample. Quite revealing. But I still use a lot of black pepper.

### agatha — 2012-04-08T13:56:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12627>

I'm not exactly sure about lemon juice - but since it is only a squeeze and isn't sweet I figured it would be OK. Does anyone else know about this?

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-08T14:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-12629>

You could try lemongrass instead? I must confess to using a little squeeze of lemon juice in my mortar and pestle, with chopped lemongrass which I then pound into a paste, which is heavenly on fish or chicken - sometimes a squeeze of lime juice is better, and lemon or lime zest are tasty, too - but I don't know if they're OK? One of my favourite marinades is to mix a squeeze of lime juice with chopped lemongrass, a crushed clove of garlic or garlic salt, some fresh coriander/cilantro and a little bit of sea salt, it's also great thinned with a little stock/bone broth if you need more liquid. It can be used on loads of different meats and fish and even some vegetables, too.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-11T21:51:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-13873>

Lemon juice is fine since it's the carbs you want to avoid and lemon juice is low in carbs.

Other fun spices/herbs: Chinese Five Spice, Garam Masala, leeks, fennel bulb (raw or sauteed), and you can make coconut chutney for fish.

### ealachan — 2012-04-11T21:59:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-13874>

> **hazyjane;13942 wrote:** Lemon juice is fine since it's the carbs you want to avoid and lemon juice is low in carbs.
>
>
>
> Other fun spices/herbs: Chinese Five Spice, Garam Masala, leeks, fennel bulb (raw or sauteed), and you can make coconut chutney for fish.

cauliflower roasted with garam masala is super nummy!

Also, last night I made a [Thai Red Curry fish stew](http://http://onegirlcooks.blogspot.com/2012/04/thai-red-curry-fish-stew.html) (with shrimp and cod, but you could use any white fish), if anyone is interested. It was super easy and very yummy. My blog post doesn't give an actual recipe as such, but I can write one up easily enough for anyone who wants it - just PM me.

### freshveggies — 2012-04-11T22:59:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-13881>

> **ealachan;13943 wrote:** cauliflower roasted with garam masala is super nummy!
>
>
>
> Also, last night I made a [Thai Red Curry fish stew](http://http://onegirlcooks.blogspot.com/2012/04/thai-red-curry-fish-stew.html) (with shrimp and cod, but you could use any white fish), if anyone is interested. It was super easy and very yummy. My blog post doesn't give an actual recipe as such, but I can write one up easily enough for anyone who wants it - just PM me.

Your soup looks so good. I am not a cook, but want to add more variety in my food. When you make this stew, is it made with whole coconut milk from a can? Is the red curry a seasoning? Thank you

### ealachan — 2012-04-11T23:15:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-13893>

> **freshveggies;13950 wrote:** Your soup looks so good. I am not a cook, but want to add more variety in my food. When you make this stew, is it made with whole coconut milk from a can? Is the red curry a seasoning? Thank you

Thanks! I'm not a professional cook either...just a food nerd.

I used whole canned coconut milk, yes...and the red curry is a paste you can get at most grocery stores that have an "international foods" section. The brand I used is Thai Kitchen. It's gluten-free, dairy-free, and sugar-free, and it's pretty widely available. It DOES have a nightshade element to it as it contains chili peppers. Also, it's not super spicey, but you can add chile peppers to the curry to spice it up if you like hot stuff.

### freshveggies — 2012-04-11T23:20:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boredom.721/post-13897>

> **ealachan;13962 wrote:** Thanks! I'm not a professional cook either...just a food nerd.
>
>
>
> I used whole canned coconut milk, yes...and the red curry is a paste you can get at most grocery stores that have an "international foods" section. The brand I used is Thai Kitchen. It's gluten-free, dairy-free, and sugar-free, and it's pretty widely available. It DOES have a nightshade element to it as it contains chili peppers. Also, it's not super spicey, but you can add chile peppers to the curry to spice it up if you like hot stuff.

thanks for the information.

# Thread: Detoxing Emotions?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-04-07T21:50:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12515>

I may be an emotional person, but this has been a roller coaster. Does a detox dump cause emotional upheaval? Is it possible to even have negative emotions stored in the cells that are now being released...hopefully permanently?

### debbie98055 — 2012-04-08T01:56:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12551>

I felt like my detoxing was estrogen, and I was VERY grouchy! Like an amped up version of pms, and I was more annoyed at males than I was at females : ).

### hazyjane — 2012-04-08T02:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12559>

Yes! In Chinese medicine, the liver stores anger and frustration and it's common for these emotions to surface when your liver is being detoxed or is having a hard time keeping up with a toxin dump. PMS is a classic liver "stagnation"/overload syndrome.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-08T22:06:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12772>

I think \*hopefully\* I'm done the estrogen dumping and/or liver toxin dump b/c my last cycle which was two weeks late (on an already long cycle ~30-33 day) and I was very emotional. However my hormones were getting very out of whack leading up to that with the two prior months actually having breast swelling and pain. I was NEVER a person to experience that kind of symptom EVER. I also was more pms-y. I also had a weight gain with each cycle starting in November. It was very frustrating. lol.

So the last cycle did not have shitty soreness and it was normal. Then after that I had a massive dump of fat. So I'm lean now, and hopefully next cycle will be a) no pain/cramp b) not emotional wreck c) no weight gain (although I didn't have that the recent cycle) d) no cravings.

So yes, I really think that once hormones and liver (and I guess ultimately leptin) is/are fixed, then yes, shitty emotions subside! Instead are replaced with being able to cope/manage stress. :-D

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-04-08T22:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12774>

p.s. It's implied that I was doing CT since end of Feb/early March.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-09T01:26:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12816>

I have been an emotional wreck as well. This CT stuff really does crazy things to one's hormones. At least I believe that is the reason. I was pms'ing for a full week and then the TOM was really bad and I was still a wreck for the whole week of. I am finally feeling more normal and I have been doing this 6 or 7 weeks now. Hoping we are on the path to better health after all of this.

### joyfuljoyful — 2012-04-09T01:35:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12820>

I have had a similar experience....my poor husband and kids. It was a few weeks with what seemed like constant PMS. Little patience, and more annoyed with things, and just more irritated. I am almost 4 weeks in and I'm starting to see much more improvement in this,. However I have noticed that some detox symptoms will lessen, and then come back days later. Honestly it makes me kind of happy, since I know that it is doing something!! And I think it will definitely be worth it in the end. I want all that junk OUT of my body!

Hang in there.

### Pamela — 2012-04-09T04:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12874>

I had a similar thing with really feeling grumped out and kind of pissed at everything. Couldn't wait to get in the cold bath today and afterwards I did feel much better with no return of the major grumps.

### ColdBren — 2012-04-09T04:54:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12886>

It would be awesome if this was really clearing out all of that junk.....I can't wait to get to the other side. Sorry you guys are going through it too, but happy to hear it seems to be getting better....and maybe for good!

### Glamorama — 2012-04-09T05:10:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/detoxing-emotions.722/post-12896>

I had a lot of negative emotions during my first month of detox! Mostly linked to the excess estrogen being released, but I was often feeling suicidal and super-depressed (this is not me at all!!!!). It cleared though, now I'm back to my mostly happy selfs.

# Thread: HDL and leaky gut
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### optimus prime — 2012-04-07T22:53:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-12529>

Hi! Wondering if you have to have low HDL to have leaky gut. Dr. Kruse says "...and if your gut is leaky as shown by a low HDL level." ([http://jackkruse.com/hormone-cascade-101/](http://http://jackkruse.com/hormone-cascade-101/))

I have a 107 HDL, 173 LDL, and I am not responding to my Vitamin D supplements. I assumed that I had a leaky gut issue...but now I don't know!

Thanks!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-07T22:59:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-12531>

You can have a nice HDL number and still have leaky gut. Ask MamaGrok!

I suspect there is other stuff going on that may be revealed in other labs. I had to start using an emulsified formula which is a common problem for autoimmune conditions. I'm getting testing next Tuesday so I will know for sure quantitatively. I could tell an increase in energy when I started it. I use Biotics D Mulsion and have for about 6 months now. Maybe something to look at...

### LinD — 2012-04-07T23:08:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-12533>

That's interesting. While I know it can be higher, October bloodwork has me at 89 HDL... And I have MS. No grains or legumes since November 2011 (if not before--I forget).

I tried to get my neuro to run some of the suggested labs, but it was a no go. :-( Guess I need to work on WHY I need these or find another doc in United Healthcare health insurance plan who will. I looked up John (Jack) Kruse (TN) and he's on there!

Dr. K... Please?

### Owl — 2012-04-08T03:39:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-12567>

Optimus, are you taking Vit **D3**? And most of us took 10,000iu/day for 3 to 6 months to get our numbers up. Some people took way more than that, because they didn't absorb it, or their bodies used up a huge amount. It's fat soluble, so you need to take it with a meal with fat.

### optimus prime — 2012-04-08T18:44:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-12721>

> **Owl;12636 wrote: Optimus, are you taking Vit D3**? And most of us took 10,000iu/day for 3 to 6 months to get our numbers up. Some people took way more than that, because they didn't absorb it, or their bodies used up a huge amount. It's fat soluble, so you need to take it with a meal with fat.

I've been taking 5,000 a day for 2 months. My D has been in the 30s and 40s for 3 years now. No idea what it was before that.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-09T04:25:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-12878>

> **optimus prime;12790 wrote:** I've been taking 5,000 a day for 2 months. My D has been in the 30s and 40s for 3 years now. No idea what it was before that.

Make sure you are taking an oil-based D3. You will not absorb it without the oil. If you don't have an oil-based D3 you can take it with olive oil. My level was 32 prior to starting D3. I was on 10,000 IU for over two years. The last time I tested my level was 76. I also read on Dr. Davis' blog that the longer you take D3 the less you have to take. I went down to 5,000 IU recently and will test again soon to see how I'm doing.

\--Steph

### shilohman — 2012-04-09T05:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-12890>

Also do not take D3 the same time you take magnesium because they compete for absorption.

### optimus prime — 2012-04-09T17:00:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-12976>

> **stephsxty9;12947 wrote:** Make sure you are taking an oil-based D3. You will not absorb it without the oil. If you don't have an oil-based D3 you can take it with olive oil. My level was 32 prior to starting D3. I was on 10,000 IU for over two years. The last time I tested my level was 76. I also read on Dr. Davis' blog that the longer you take D3 the less you have to take. I went down to 5,000 IU recently and will test again soon to see how I'm doing.--Steph

Wow, nice progress! Curious if you've noticed your body feeling different with higher D. Do you have more energy? Are you losing weight more easily (if weight is an issue).

My supplement is olive oil based, plus I take it right after breakfast.

### optimus prime — 2012-04-09T17:01:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-12978>

> **shilohman;12959 wrote:** Also do not take D3 the same time you take magnesium because they compete for absorption.

Thank you, I didn't know that! Good thing I take my magnesium at night.

### chasee33 — 2012-04-10T21:16:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-13439>

Blue Bonnet has a nice vitamin D3 supplement mixed with Medium Chain Triglycerides and oils like lemon that works nicely. I found it at Whole Foods.

### BenG — 2012-04-10T21:25:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-13442>

You typically get 1 ng/dl for every 100iu you take. I'd double yours to 10,000 IU per day. Even the Vitamin D council says that amount is safe. I was taking 20,000 IU a day for 3 months and got mine up fron 7ng/dl to 83 ng/dl, but if you are heavy, you need double to bring up levels. Dr. Kruse likes it between 70-100 ng/dl

> **optimus prime;12790 wrote:** I've been taking 5,000 a day for 2 months. My D has been in the 30s and 40s for 3 years now. No idea what it was before that.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-12T02:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hdl-and-leaky-gut.723/post-14016>

I didn't feel noticeably better when my D levels went up, but I had sever adrenal fatigue, which I've been trying to heal over the last two years. Also no weight loss with improved D levels, but that's because I was leptin resistant and wasn't able to exercise because of the AF. I'm also hypothyroid. Anyway, I'm sure that getting my D level up is helping with all the issues, but I definitely didn't have any noticeable improvement. It did possibly help with mood, but that's hard to judge since I've also done a lot to improve hormones.

### [email redacted] — 2012-04-08T05:08:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rabbit.726/post-12580>

I was at the winter farmer's market here and was disappointed to see only 4 vendors. Very little in the way of selection. But one vendor had rabbit with a sign saying it was really healthy to eat.

Anyone out there have any experience in this area? I didn't purchase any and stuck with duck eggs and pork belly for now.

Thanks

And sorry, Easter Bunny!

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-11T21:35:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rabbit.726/post-13867>

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T21:06:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rabbit.726/post-14809>

I use to eat rabbit, but then read how you can contract some eye disease from eating rabbit. Wonder if being cold adapted has any effect on being able to not worry about any eye diseases?

### AKMan — 2012-04-14T02:38:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rabbit.726/post-14952>

Have you ever seen those plastic bags they sell that you can cook a roast or chicken in? Those work great for rabbit. Use same seasonings as for chicken, don't cook in a way you will dry it out, it's easy to dry out and it's not so ggod then. Crock pot is good, too.

### [email redacted] — 2012-05-17T05:54:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rabbit.726/post-24809>

Thanks a lot for the tip. Haven't purchased one yet but still on my list to try. I love crock pot cooking! So easy and fast!

> **AKMan;15021 wrote:** Have you ever seen those plastic bags they sell that you can cook a roast or chicken in? Those work great for rabbit. Use same seasonings as for chicken, don't cook in a way you will dry it out, it's easy to dry out and it's not so ggod then. Crock pot is good, too.
